modules.title: Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge
modules.description: Aeronautical knowledge, systems, performance, weather, navigation, and pilot physiology.

sections.title: Introduction to Flying
sections.summary: Apply faa roles, regulations, and guidance, pilot certificates, ratings, and privileges, medical eligibility and fitness, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: How U.S. civil aviation is organized
lessons.concept: The FAA promotes safe civil aviation by issuing and enforcing standards under Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations.
lessons.explanation: Rules, guidance, and operating information serve different purposes. Regulations are legally binding; the Aeronautical Information Manual explains accepted procedures; advisory circulars describe guidance and acceptable methods without becoming regulations themselves.
lessons.workedExample: Before a training flight, a student may check 14 CFR for a legal requirement, the AIM for an operating procedure, and an advisory circular for deeper guidance. Treating all three as interchangeable could hide whether a statement is mandatory.

practice.prompt: Which source contains legally enforceable federal aviation rules?
practice.explanation: Title 14 CFR contains federal aviation regulations; handbooks, the AIM, and advisory circulars provide supporting information and guidance.
options: Advisory circulars
Title 14 CFR
The Pilot's Handbook
A flight school's syllabus

lessons.title: Certificates, ratings, and privileges
lessons.concept: A pilot certificate establishes a level of qualification, while category, class, and type ratings identify the aircraft privileges attached to it.
lessons.explanation: Eligibility, aeronautical knowledge, experience, and proficiency requirements vary by certificate. A certificate alone does not authorize every operation: pilots must also meet recency, medical, endorsement, aircraft-rating, and operating-rule requirements.
lessons.workedExample: A private pilot airplane certificate with an airplane single-engine land rating does not by itself authorize acting as pilot in command of a complex airplane. The pilot also needs the applicable training and logbook endorsement.

practice.prompt: Match each credential term to its role.
practice.explanation: Certificates define qualification level; category and class ratings narrow the kinds of aircraft for which privileges apply.

pairs.right: Overall pilot qualification level
Broad family such as airplane
Subdivision such as single-engine land

lessons.title: Training and certification checks
lessons.concept: Flight training combines knowledge instruction, flight proficiency, endorsements, and a practical test measured against Airman Certification Standards.
lessons.explanation: A certificated flight instructor teaches, evaluates readiness, and provides required endorsements. An authorized evaluator conducts the practical test, which integrates knowledge, risk management, and skill rather than treating them as isolated subjects.
lessons.workedExample: When a learner can perform a maneuver but misses its hazards, the task is not yet ACS-ready. The instructor should connect the maneuver to its knowledge elements and risk controls before endorsing the applicant.

practice.prompt: What does an ACS task integrate?
practice.explanation: The ACS evaluates the knowledge, risk management, and flight-skill elements of each task together.
options: Only stick-and-rudder skill
Knowledge, risk management, and skill
Only regulations and weather
Aircraft ownership and insurance

lessons.title: FAA roles, regulations, and guidance
lessons.concept: 14 CFR part 91 provides guidance in the areas of general flight rules, visual flight rules (VFR), and instrument flight rules (IFR), while 14 CFR part 43 covers aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alterations.
lessons.explanation: to flight training and pilot certification. For instance, 14 CFR part 61 pertains to the certification of pilots, flight instructors, and ground instructors. It also defines the eligibility, aeronautical knowledge, and flight proficiency, as well as training and testing requirements for each type of pilot certificate issued.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how faa roles, regulations, and guidance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about faa roles, regulations, and guidance: "___, visual flight rules (VFR), and instrument flight rules (IFR), while 14 CFR part 43 covers aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alterations."
options: under 14 CFR part 61 and part 91
Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations
pilots use them with a physical preflight to determine airworthiness
14 CFR part 91 provides guidance in the areas of general flight rules
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 14 CFR part 91 provides guidance in the areas of general flight rules, visual flight rules (VFR), and instrument flight rules (IFR), while 14 CFR part 43 covers aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alterations.

lessons.title: Medical eligibility and fitness
lessons.concept: [Figure 1-21] Sport Pilot To become a sport pilot, the student pilot is required to have flown, at a minimum, the following hours depending upon the aircraft: • Airplane: 20 hours • Powered Parachute: 12 hours • Weight-Shift Control (Trikes): 20 hours • Glider: 10 hours • Rotorcraft (gyroplane only): 20 hours •...
lessons.explanation: For example, a certain certificate may have privileges and limitations under 14 CFR part 61 and part 91. Endorsements, a form of authorization, are written to establish that the certificate holder has received training in specific skill areas. Endorsements are written and signed by an authorized individual, usually a certificated flight instructor (CFI), and are based on aircraft classification.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how medical eligibility and fitness affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on medical eligibility and fitness: "For example, a certain certificate may have privileges and limitations ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: A pilot certificate establishes a level of qualification
Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations
airspace to have a control tower (Lake City
under 14 CFR part 61 and part 91
practice.explanation: The cited page states: For example, a certain certificate may have privileges and limitations under 14 CFR part 61 and part 91.

lessons.title: Flight training and instructor responsibilities
lessons.concept: Students often judge the effectiveness of the flight instructor and the success of the pilot training program based on their ability to pass the requisite FAA practical test.
lessons.explanation: should observe recognized safety practices, as well as regulations during all flight operations. The student pilot who enrolls in a pilot training program commits considerable time, effort, and expense to achieve a pilot certificate. A competent flight instructor stresses to the student that practical tests are a sampling of pilot ability compressed into a short period of time.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how flight training and instructor responsibilities affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on flight training and instructor responsibilities: "Students often judge the effectiveness of the flight instructor and the success of the pilot training program based on their ___."
options: a practical test measured against Airman Certification Standards
A pilot certificate establishes a level of qualification
ability to pass the requisite FAA practical test
Airspeed markings for VX
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Students often judge the effectiveness of the flight instructor and the success of the pilot training program based on their ability to pass the requisite FAA practical test.

quiz.prompt: Which publication is regulatory?
quiz.explanation: Title 14 CFR is regulatory; the other publications support learning and operations.
options: 14 CFR
AIM
PHAK
An advisory circular

quiz.prompt: Match the FAA resource to its primary use.
quiz.explanation: Regulations impose requirements, while the AIM and advisory circulars explain procedures and guidance.

pairs.right: Binding rules
Operating procedures and information
Nonregulatory guidance

quiz.prompt: Who normally provides the endorsements that establish an applicant's readiness for a practical test?
quiz.explanation: A CFI provides required training endorsements; an authorized evaluator administers the practical test.
options: An air traffic controller
A certificated flight instructor
An aircraft mechanic
An airport manager

quiz.prompt: The FAA page contrasts early flapping-wing ideas with Cayley's glider. Which advance moved design toward the modern airplane?
options: Using fixed lifting wings with separate control and propulsion concepts
Increasing the number of flapping joints
Replacing air with a vacuum around the wing
Eliminating aerodynamic surfaces

image.caption: Early heavier-than-air flight concepts

sections.title: Risk Foundations and Personal Minimums
sections.summary: Apply hazards, risk, and consequences, pave preflight risk scan, personal minimums, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: ADM is a continuous process
lessons.concept: Aeronautical decision-making is a systematic process for selecting the safest practical course of action in a changing flight environment.
lessons.explanation: Good ADM starts before engine start and continues through postflight. The pilot detects changes, estimates their significance, chooses an outcome, identifies actions, carries them out, and evaluates the result instead of defending the original plan.
lessons.workedExample: A lowering ceiling is not merely noted; the pilot compares it with personal and regulatory limits, selects a diversion or landing option, acts while choices remain, and checks whether conditions improve as expected.

practice.prompt: A pilot notices weather deteriorating faster than forecast. What is the best ADM response?
practice.explanation: ADM requires updating the plan as conditions change and acting before options narrow.
options: Continue until the weather becomes illegal
Reassess options and act while margins remain
Ignore the change because the briefing was legal
Wait for ATC to make the decision

lessons.title: Use PAVE to expose risk
lessons.concept: PAVE organizes hazards into Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, and External pressures so that no major risk domain is skipped.
lessons.explanation: A checklist does not make a flight safe by itself. Each identified hazard needs a control, such as delaying, carrying more fuel, choosing a different runway, taking an instructor, or canceling. Multiple moderate hazards can combine into an unacceptable overall risk.
lessons.workedExample: A current pilot, legal airplane, marginal crosswind, and pressure to arrive for an event may look acceptable in isolation. PAVE reveals the interaction between environment and external pressure and supports an early no-go or alternate plan.

practice.prompt: Match each observation to its PAVE category.
practice.explanation: PAVE separates personal, machine, operating-environment, and schedule/social hazards.

pairs.right: Fatigue after a short night
Inoperative landing light
Low ceilings along the route
A promised arrival time

lessons.title: Hazards, risk, and consequences
lessons.concept: Risk During each flight, the single pilot makes many decisions under hazardous conditions.
lessons.explanation: To fly safely, the pilot needs to assess the degree of risk and determine the best course of action to mitigate the risk. Risk Assessment Matrix Severity Assessing Risk For the single pilot, assessing risk is not as simple as it sounds. For example, the pilot acts as his or her own quality control in making decisions.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how hazards, risk, and consequences affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on hazards, risk, and consequences: "Risk During each flight, ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: External pressures so that no major risk domain is skipped
course of action in a changing flight environment
the single pilot makes many decisions under hazardous conditions
Single-pilot resource management uses all available people
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Risk During each flight, the single pilot makes many decisions under hazardous conditions.

lessons.title: Personal minimums
lessons.concept: Earlier it was suggested pilots set their own personal minimums, especially when it comes to weather.
lessons.explanation: V = EnVironment Weather Weather is a major environmental consideration. As pilots evaluate the weather for a particular flight, they should consider the following: • • guidance? Is the terminal airport equipped with them?
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how personal minimums affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Earlier it was suggested pilots set their own personal minimums, ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: the single pilot makes many decisions under hazardous conditions
course of action in a changing flight environment
especially when it comes to weather
alternate sensations of “fullness” and “clearing.” During descent
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Earlier it was suggested pilots set their own personal minimums, especially when it comes to weather.

quiz.prompt: What makes ADM effective during flight?
quiz.explanation: ADM is iterative: detect, decide, act, and evaluate as the situation evolves.
options: Never changing the original plan
Continuously evaluating results and new information
Delegating all decisions to ATC
Using intuition without a process

quiz.prompt: Match the PAVE category.
quiz.explanation: PAVE is a comprehensive preflight and inflight risk scan.

pairs.right: Illness or fatigue
Fuel or equipment status
Weather and terrain
Schedule pressure

quiz.prompt: Several individually manageable hazards occur together. What should the pilot do?
quiz.explanation: Risk often compounds; the combined exposure can exceed a safe margin.
options: Consider their combined effect
Score only the single worst hazard
Ignore any hazard that is legal
Assume experience cancels them

quiz.prompt: Using the illustrated PAVE checklist, where should pressure to reach a meeting on time be recorded?
options: Pilot
Aircraft
enVironment
External pressures

image.caption: PAVE preflight risk checklist

quiz.explanation: A promised arrival or schedule goal is an external pressure that can distort otherwise sound decisions.

sections.title: Decision Models and Single-Pilot Resources
sections.summary: Apply aeronautical decision-making cycle, decide and 3p models, hazardous attitudes and antidotes, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Manage attitudes and workload
lessons.concept: Single-pilot resource management uses all available people, equipment, information, and time while countering hazardous attitudes.
lessons.explanation: The five classic attitudes are anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, and resignation. Their antidotes slow impulsive choices and restore disciplined risk control. Task management, automation management, and situational awareness protect mental capacity.
lessons.workedExample: If a reroute arrives during turbulence, the pilot can engage appropriate automation, ask ATC for delay or clarification, stabilize the aircraft, and then reprogram. Aviate, navigate, communicate remains the workload priority.

practice.prompt: Which thought best counters impulsivity?
practice.explanation: The antidote to impulsivity is to slow down and think before acting.
options: Rules do not apply to me
Do it quickly
Not so fast - think first
What is the use?

lessons.title: Aeronautical decision-making cycle
lessons.concept: Its application is illustrated in column A while automatic/naturalistic decision-making is shown in column B.
lessons.explanation: Because the left engine is the only engine delivering thrust, the yaw increases to the right, which necessitates application of additional left rudder application. lessons.title: DECIDE and 3P models
lessons.concept: A solid approach to decision-making is through the use of analytical models, such as the 5 Ps, 3P, and DECIDE.
lessons.explanation: Decision-Making in a Dynamic Environment pitfalls that come with the development of pilot experience. Good decisions result when pilots gather all available information, review it, analyze the options, rate the options, select a course of action, and evaluate that course of action for correctness. In some situations, there is not always time to make decisions based on analytical decision-making skills.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how decide and 3p models affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Aircraft Construction
sections.summary: Apply major airplane components, axes, motion, and center of gravity, truss, monocoque, and semimonocoque structures, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Components work as a system
lessons.concept: The fuselage, wings, empennage, landing gear, and powerplant each carry specific loads and together form the airplane.
lessons.explanation: The fuselage supports occupants and connects major assemblies. Wings produce most lift. The empennage provides stabilizing and control surfaces. Landing gear absorbs ground loads, and the powerplant creates thrust while supporting electrical and other systems.
lessons.workedExample: During preflight, a loose fairing is not judged only by appearance. Its location near a control surface, structural attachment, or cooling path determines how it could affect the whole aircraft.

practice.prompt: Which assembly normally includes the horizontal and vertical stabilizers?
practice.explanation: The tail assembly, or empennage, contains stabilizing and control surfaces.
options: Powerplant
Empennage
Landing gear
Wing strut

lessons.title: Axes, motion, and center of gravity
lessons.concept: An airplane rolls about its longitudinal axis, pitches about its lateral axis, and yaws about its vertical axis, all referenced to the center of gravity.
lessons.explanation: The three axes intersect at the center of gravity. Ailerons primarily control roll, the elevator controls pitch, and the rudder controls yaw. Because motion is coupled, coordinated control inputs may be required rather than treating each axis in isolation.
lessons.workedExample: In a right turn, right aileron input initiates roll about the longitudinal axis while coordinated rudder counters adverse yaw about the vertical axis.

practice.prompt: Match each motion to its axis.
practice.explanation: Roll-longitudinal, pitch-lateral, and yaw-vertical are the standard motion-axis pairings.

pairs.right: Longitudinal axis
Lateral axis
Vertical axis

lessons.title: Structure carries flight loads
lessons.concept: Truss, monocoque, and semimonocoque structures distribute loads differently, but damage to a load path can reduce strength beyond the visible area.
lessons.explanation: Truss construction relies on a framework of members. Monocoque construction relies heavily on stressed skin. Semimonocoque construction combines stressed skin with frames, bulkheads, and stringers and is common in modern airplanes.
lessons.workedExample: A small wrinkle near a riveted frame may signal transferred or excessive stress. A pilot should not press it flat or dismiss it; the aircraft needs evaluation under approved maintenance procedures.

practice.prompt: Why are stringers used in semimonocoque construction?
practice.explanation: Stringers reinforce the skin and help carry longitudinal structural loads.
options: To replace all stressed skin
To help carry and distribute longitudinal loads
To generate engine thrust
To control fuel flow

lessons.title: Major airplane components
lessons.concept: In some cases, the outside skin can support all or a major portion of the flight loads.
lessons.explanation: Stringers and bulkheads, or formers, are added to shape the fuselage and support the covering. As technology progressed, aircraft designers began to enclose the truss members to streamline the airplane and improve performance. This was originally accomplished with cloth fabric, which eventually gave way to lightweight metals such as aluminum.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how major airplane components affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "___, the outside skin can support all or a major portion of the flight loads." Which detail completes the statement?
options: powerplant each carry specific loads and together form the airplane
damage to a load path can reduce strength beyond the visible area
In some cases
External pressures so that no major risk domain is skipped
practice.explanation: The cited page states: In some cases, the outside skin can support all or a major portion of the flight loads.

quiz.prompt: Which component primarily provides yaw control?
quiz.explanation: The rudder controls yaw about the vertical axis.
options: Aileron
Elevator
Rudder
Flap

quiz.prompt: Match the airplane motion.
quiz.explanation: The primary controls correspond to roll, pitch, and yaw.

pairs.right: Roll
Pitch
Yaw

quiz.prompt: Which construction uses stressed skin plus internal frames and stringers?
quiz.explanation: Semimonocoque construction shares loads among skin and internal members.
options: Pure truss
Semimonocoque
Fabric-only
Rotor mast

options: Empennage
Powerplant
Main landing gear

sections.title: Principles of Flight
sections.summary: Apply atmospheric properties and standard conditions, newton's laws and aerodynamic force, bernoulli, continuity, and pressure distribution, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Atmosphere sets the operating medium
lessons.concept: Air has mass, pressure, density, and viscosity; changes in temperature, pressure, and humidity change aircraft and engine performance.
lessons.explanation: Standard sea-level conditions provide a common reference: 29.92 inches of mercury and 15 degrees Celsius. Pressure and density normally decrease with altitude, and warmer air is less dense than colder air at the same pressure.
lessons.workedExample: On a hot high-elevation day, fewer air molecules move through the wing and engine. Expect reduced lift, propeller efficiency, and engine power compared with standard conditions.

practice.prompt: Enter standard sea-level pressure in inches of mercury.
practice.explanation: The standard datum is 29.92 inHg.

lessons.title: Newton and pressure explain airflow effects
lessons.concept: Lift is consistent with both pressure differences around an airfoil and the downward change in momentum of the airflow.
lessons.explanation: Bernoulli's principle relates higher flow speed to lower static pressure along a streamline, while Newton's laws describe how deflecting air produces an equal and opposite force. Neither should be used as a one-sentence explanation that ignores angle of attack and circulation.
lessons.workedExample: As a wing redirects air downward, the air receives downward momentum and the wing receives an upward reaction. At the same time, the pressure field around the wing produces a net aerodynamic force.

practice.prompt: Match the principle to the observation.
practice.explanation: These principles describe compatible parts of the same airflow behavior.

pairs.right: Higher speed accompanies lower static pressure
Air deflected down produces an upward reaction
Flow speeds up through a smaller passage

lessons.title: Airfoils shape pressure and vortices
lessons.concept: Airfoil shape and angle of attack establish a pressure distribution; the spanwise pressure difference also produces wingtip vortices and induced drag.
lessons.explanation: The center of pressure can move as angle of attack changes. Near the tips, higher-pressure air beneath the wing curls toward lower pressure above it, creating trailing vortices that are strongest when the airplane is heavy, clean, and slow.
lessons.workedExample: Behind a departing heavy aircraft, a light airplane pilot stays above the heavy's flight path and lands beyond its touchdown point, avoiding the sinking, outward-moving vortices.

practice.prompt: When are wingtip vortices generally strongest?
practice.explanation: Heavy weight, clean configuration, and slow speed require high lift and produce strong vortices.
options: Light, dirty, and fast
Heavy, clean, and slow
Light, clean, and parked
Only during descent

lessons.title: Atmospheric properties and standard conditions
lessons.concept: Under standard conditions at sea level, the average pressure exerted by the weight of the atmosphere is approximately 14.70 pounds per square inch (psi) of surface, or 1,013.2 millibars (mb).
lessons.explanation: Therefore, like any other substance, it has weight, and because of its weight, it has force. Since air is a fluid substance, this force is exerted equally in all directions. Its effect on bodies within the air is called pressure.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how atmospheric properties and standard conditions affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "Under standard conditions at sea level, the average pressure exerted by the weight of the atmosphere is approximately ___, or 1,013.2 millibars (mb)."
options: 14.70 pounds per square inch (psi) of surface
humidity change aircraft and engine performance
the downward change in momentum of the airflow
flight hours/hour pounds of fuel/hour or 120 Indicated
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Under standard conditions at sea level, the average pressure exerted by the weight of the atmosphere is approximately 14.70 pounds per square inch (psi) of surface, or 1,013.2 millibars (mb).

quiz.prompt: What is standard sea-level temperature in degrees Celsius?
quiz.explanation: The standard sea-level temperature is 15 °C.

quiz.prompt: Match each term.
quiz.explanation: These properties determine the aerodynamic environment.

pairs.right: Ambient pressure exerted by the air
Pressure associated with motion
Mass per unit volume

quiz.prompt: What causes a wingtip vortex?
quiz.explanation: The pressure differential drives spanwise flow around the tip and rolls into a vortex.
options: Fuel moving in the wing
Spanwise flow from high pressure below toward low pressure above
The magneto firing order
Cabin ventilation

quiz.prompt: The FAA airfoil figure compares several profiles. What geometric feature distinguishes a cambered airfoil from a symmetric one?
options: Its mean camber line is curved
It has no chord line
Its lower surface must be flat
It cannot create lift

image.caption: Airfoil sections and pressure behavior

quiz.explanation: A cambered airfoil has a curved mean camber line; a symmetric airfoil's mean camber line coincides with its chord line.

sections.title: Forces, Vortices, and Airplane Motion
sections.summary: Apply the four forces in equilibrium, lift and drag components, ground effect, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Balance the four forces
lessons.concept: Lift, weight, thrust, and drag act continuously; steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path, not because any force disappears.
lessons.explanation: In straight-and-level unaccelerated flight, lift equals weight and thrust equals drag. During a stabilized climb, lift may be less than weight because part of thrust acts upward along the inclined flight path.
lessons.workedExample: Adding power without retrimming changes the force balance and may change both airspeed and pitch. The pilot establishes the desired attitude and power, then trims after the airplane stabilizes.

practice.prompt: In unaccelerated straight-and-level flight, which relationship is correct?
practice.explanation: Forces balance: lift equals weight and thrust equals drag.
options: Lift is greater than weight
Thrust equals drag
Drag is zero
Weight equals thrust

lessons.title: Lift and drag components
lessons.concept: The first is called parasite because it in no way functions to aid flight, while the second, induced drag, is a result of an airfoil developing lift.
lessons.explanation: Figure 5-6 depicts the L/DMAX by the lowest portion of the blue line labeled “total drag.” The configuration of an aircraft has a great effect on the L/D. Drag Drag is the force that resists movement of an aircraft through the air. There are two basic types: parasite drag and induced drag.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how lift and drag components affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing lift and drag components, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "___, while the second, induced drag, is a result of an airfoil developing lift."
options: steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
The first is called parasite because it in no way functions to aid flight
the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
the spanwise pressure difference also produces wingtip vortices and induced drag
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The first is called parasite because it in no way functions to aid flight, while the second, induced drag, is a result of an airfoil developing lift.

lessons.title: Ground effect
lessons.concept: [Figure 5-16] Ground effect, then, is due to the interference of the ground (or water) surface with the airflow patterns about the aircraft in flight.
lessons.explanation: This phenomenon is called ground effect. When an aircraft in flight comes within several feet of the surface, ground or water, a change occurs in the threedimensional flow pattern around the aircraft because the vertical component of the airflow around the wing is restricted by the surface. This alters the wing’s upwash, downwash, and wingtip vortices.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how ground effect affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for ground effect. Which phrase completes "Ground effect, then, is due to the interference of the ground (or water) surface with ___."?
options: the airflow patterns about the aircraft in flight
The first is called parasite because it in no way functions to aid flight
steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
then checks predictions against visual checkpoints
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Ground effect, then, is due to the interference of the ground (or water) surface with the airflow patterns about the aircraft in flight.

lessons.title: Wingtip vortices and wake avoidance
lessons.concept: This alters the wing’s upwash, downwash, and wingtip vortices.
lessons.explanation: This is due to the air that is trapped between the wing and the landing surface, as if there were an air cushion. This phenomenon is called ground effect. When an aircraft in flight comes within several feet of the surface, ground or water, a change occurs in the threedimensional flow pattern around the aircraft because the vertical component of the airflow around the wing is restricted by the surface.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wingtip vortices and wake avoidance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about wingtip vortices and wake avoidance: "___, downwash, and wingtip vortices."
options: the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
the airflow patterns about the aircraft in flight
the spanwise pressure difference also produces wingtip vortices and induced drag
This alters the wing’s upwash
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This alters the wing’s upwash, downwash, and wingtip vortices.

lessons.title: Axes, moments, and coordinated motion
lessons.concept: This type of stability also has three subtypes: [Figure 5-22] • Positive dynamic stability—over time, the motion of the displaced object decreases in amplitude and, because it is positive, the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state.
lessons.explanation: when disturbed from a given pitch, yaw, or bank. Neutral dynamic stability—once displaced, the displaced object neither decreases nor increases in amplitude. A worn automobile shock absorber exhibits this tendency.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how axes, moments, and coordinated motion affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on axes, moments, and coordinated motion: "This type of stability also has three subtypes: • Positive dynamic stability—over time, the motion of the displaced object decreases in amplitude and, because it is positive, ___."
options: steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
The first is called parasite because it in no way functions to aid flight
the center of gravity
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This type of stability also has three subtypes: • Positive dynamic stability—over time, the motion of the displaced object decreases in amplitude and, because it is positive, the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state.

quiz.prompt: What directly causes an aerodynamic stall?
quiz.explanation: A stall occurs when the critical angle of attack is exceeded.
options: Low airspeed alone
Exceeding the critical angle of attack
Retracting the landing gear
A nose-low attitude

quiz.prompt: On the coefficient graph, what happens after the wing passes the angle for maximum lift coefficient?
options: Lift coefficient decreases as the wing stalls
Drag becomes zero
Angle of attack resets to zero
Weight disappears

image.alt: FAA PHAK Angle of attack, lift, and drag relationships, handbook page 5-4
image.caption: Angle of attack, lift, and drag relationships

quiz.explanation: Beyond the critical angle, separated flow grows and the lift coefficient decreases.

pairs.right: The first is called parasite because it in no way functions to aid flight, while the second, induced drag, is a result of an airfoil developing lift.
Ground effect, then, is due to the interference of the ground (or water) surface with the airflow patterns about the aircraft in flight.
This alters the wing’s upwash, downwash, and wingtip vortices.

options: the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
the airflow patterns about the aircraft in flight
This alters the wing’s upwash
the spanwise pressure difference also produces wingtip vortices and induced drag
quiz.explanation: The cited page states: This alters the wing’s upwash, downwash, and wingtip vortices.

sections.title: Stability, Maneuvering, and Stalls
sections.summary: Apply static and dynamic stability, longitudinal, lateral, and directional stability, turning forces and overbanking, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Stability resists displacement
lessons.concept: Static stability describes the initial tendency after displacement; dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns, diverges, or oscillates.
lessons.explanation: Longitudinal stability depends strongly on center-of-gravity position and tail forces. Lateral stability is influenced by dihedral and sweep, while directional stability relies heavily on the vertical tail and keel effect.
lessons.workedExample: After a gust raises the nose, an airplane with positive static longitudinal stability initially tends back toward its trimmed angle. Whether the oscillations diminish describes its dynamic stability.

practice.prompt: Match the stability type to its axis.
practice.explanation: Longitudinal, lateral, and directional stability correspond to pitch, roll, and yaw.

pairs.right: Pitch
Roll
Yaw

lessons.title: Static and dynamic stability
lessons.concept: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.
lessons.explanation: When turbulence or rudder application causes the aircraft to yaw to one side, the opposite wing presents a longer leading edge perpendicular to the relative airflow. The airspeed of the forward wing increases and it acquires more drag than the back wing. The additional drag on the forward wing pulls the wing back, turning the aircraft back to its original path.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how static and dynamic stability affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Which source-grounded fact about static and dynamic stability is correct?
options: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.
effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
the actual load imposed on the wing
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.

lessons.title: Longitudinal, lateral, and directional stability
lessons.concept: Spiral Instability Spiral instability exists when the static directional stability of the aircraft is very strong as compared to the effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium.
lessons.explanation: Those aircraft with continuing Dutch roll tendencies are usually equipped with gyro-stabilized yaw dampers. Manufacturers try to reach a midpoint between too much and too little directional stability. Because it is more desirable for the aircraft to have “spiral instability” than Dutch roll tendencies, most aircraft are designed with that characteristic.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how longitudinal, lateral, and directional stability affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing longitudinal, lateral, and directional stability, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "Spiral Instability Spiral instability exists when the static directional stability of the aircraft is very strong as compared to the ___."
options: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium
the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Spiral Instability Spiral instability exists when the static directional stability of the aircraft is very strong as compared to the effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium.

lessons.title: Turning forces and overbanking
lessons.concept: When the aircraft’s wheels are on the ground during the takeoff roll, an additional turning moment around the vertical axis is induced by torque reaction.
lessons.explanation: The discussion here is with reference to those engines. Generally, the compensating factors are permanently set so that they compensate for this force at cruising speed, since most of the aircraft’s operating time is at that speed. However, aileron trim tabs permit further adjustment for other speeds.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how turning forces and overbanking affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on turning forces and overbanking: "When the aircraft’s wheels are on the ground during the takeoff roll, ___."
options: an additional turning moment around the vertical axis is induced by torque reaction
effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
practice.explanation: The cited page states: When the aircraft’s wheels are on the ground during the takeoff roll, an additional turning moment around the vertical axis is induced by torque reaction.

lessons.title: Critical angle of attack and stall recognition
lessons.concept: Angle of attack is a better parameter to use to avoid a stall.
lessons.explanation: When beginning flight training, students typically rely solely on airspeed and the published 1G stall speed to avoid stalls. This creates problems since this speed is only valid when the following conditions are met: • Unaccelerated flight (a 1G load factor) • Coordinated flight (inclinometer centered) • At one weight (typically maximum gross weight) Speed by itself is not a reliable parameter to avoid a stall. An airplane can stall at any speed.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how critical angle of attack and stall recognition affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about critical angle of attack and stall recognition: "Angle of attack is a better parameter ___."
options: to use to avoid a stall
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.
A wing stalls when it exceeds its critical angle of attack
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Angle of attack is a better parameter to use to avoid a stall.

quiz.prompt: Match stability with motion.
quiz.explanation: The three stability axes map to pitch, roll, and yaw.

pairs.right: Pitch
Roll
Yaw

quiz.prompt: Select the source-grounded fact from the lesson on static and dynamic stability.
options: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.
effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
the actual load imposed on the wing
quiz.explanation: The cited page states: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.

quiz.prompt: Which handbook detail fills the blank in this lesson recall? "Spiral Instability Spiral instability exists when the static directional stability of the aircraft is very strong as compared to the ___."
options: The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.
effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
quiz.explanation: The cited page states: Spiral Instability Spiral instability exists when the static directional stability of the aircraft is very strong as compared to the effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium.

quiz.prompt: Use the cited handbook page image. Which handbook detail fills the blank in this lesson recall? "When the aircraft’s wheels are on the ground during the takeoff roll, ___."
options: effect of its dihedral in maintaining lateral equilibrium
dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns
steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
an additional turning moment around the vertical axis is induced by torque reaction

sections.title: Load Factors, Performance, and High-Speed Flight
sections.summary: Apply load factor in turns and turbulence, maneuvering speed and the v-g diagram, weight effects on performance and controllability, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Stalls depend on angle of attack
lessons.concept: A wing stalls when it exceeds its critical angle of attack, regardless of indicated airspeed, attitude, or aircraft weight.
lessons.explanation: Banking while maintaining altitude increases load factor and the lift required, which raises stall speed. An accelerated stall can therefore occur at a higher airspeed than a one-G stall. Recovery requires reducing angle of attack first.
lessons.workedExample: A level 60-degree bank produces about 2 G. Because stall speed changes with the square root of load factor, an airplane that stalls at 50 knots at 1 G stalls near 71 knots at 2 G.

practice.prompt: A level 60° bank produces approximately what load factor?
practice.explanation: A coordinated level 60° bank has a load factor of about 2 G.

lessons.title: Load factor in turns and turbulence
lessons.concept: Whether the increase in G forces is caused by turns, rough control usage, or turbulence, the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same.
lessons.explanation: There are also occasions when the buffet can be experienced at much lower speeds known as the “low-speed Mach buffet.” An aircraft flown at a speed too slow for its weight and altitude necessitating a high AOA is the most likely situation • High altitudes—the higher an aircraft flies, the thinner the air and the greater the AOA required to produce the lift needed to maintain level flight. Heavy weights—the heavier the aircraft, the greater the lift required of the wing, and all other factors being equal, the greater the AOA. G loading—an increase in the G loading on the aircraft has the same effect as increasing the weight of the aircraft.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how load factor in turns and turbulence affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about load factor in turns and turbulence: "Whether the increase in G forces is caused by turns, rough control usage, or turbulence, ___."
options: the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same
the actual load imposed on the wing
A wing stalls when it exceeds its critical angle of attack
Reduced tolerance to G-forces Inner-ear disturbance and spatial
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Whether the increase in G forces is caused by turns, rough control usage, or turbulence, the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same.

lessons.title: Maneuvering speed and the V-g diagram
lessons.concept: High speed dives or acrobatics involving speed above the known maneuvering speed should never be practiced in rough or turbulent air.
lessons.explanation: Speeds up to, but not exceeding, the maneuvering speed allow an aircraft to stall prior to experiencing an increase in load factor that would exceed the limit load of the aircraft. Most AFM/POH now include turbulent air penetration information, which help today’s pilots safely fly aircraft capable of a wide range of speeds and altitudes. It is important for the pilot to remember that the maximum “never-exceed” placard dive speeds are determined for smooth air only.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how maneuvering speed and the v-g diagram affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing maneuvering speed and the v-g diagram, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "High speed dives or acrobatics involving speed above the known maneuvering speed should ___."
options: never be practiced in rough or turbulent air
the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same
the actual load imposed on the wing
engine power determine thrust
practice.explanation: The cited page states: High speed dives or acrobatics involving speed above the known maneuvering speed should never be practiced in rough or turbulent air.

lessons.title: Weight effects on performance and controllability
lessons.concept: Important among these effects are changes in controllability, stability, and the actual load imposed on the wing.
lessons.explanation: A zero indication on the trim tab control is not necessarily the same as “neutral trim” because of the force exerted by downwash from the wings and the fuselage on the tail surfaces. The effects of the distribution of the aircraft’s useful load have a significant influence on its flight characteristics, even when the load is within the CG limits and the maximum Load imposed by tail Gross weight CG Forward CG Center of lift Stronger Down load on tail Load imposed by tail Gross weight CG AFT CG Lighter Down load on tail Figure 5-63. Effect of load distribution on balance.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how weight effects on performance and controllability affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing weight effects on performance and controllability, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "Important among these effects are changes in controllability, stability, and ___."
options: the actual load imposed on the wing
A wing stalls when it exceeds its critical angle of attack
the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same
while center-of-gravity limits protect stability
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Important among these effects are changes in controllability, stability, and the actual load imposed on the wing.

quiz.prompt: If a 1-G stall speed is 50 knots, estimate the stall speed at 2 G using Vs(new)=Vs×√n.
quiz.explanation: 50 × √2 is approximately 70.7 knots.

sections.title: Flight Controls
sections.summary: Apply primary flight controls, adverse yaw and coordinated control, flaps and high-lift devices, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Primary controls command three axes
lessons.concept: Ailerons control roll, the elevator or stabilator controls pitch, and the rudder controls yaw.
lessons.explanation: Control effectiveness depends on airflow. At low airspeed, larger deflections may be needed for the same response; at high airspeed, abrupt inputs can create large structural loads. Control feel and system design vary by aircraft.
lessons.workedExample: During a slow-flight turn, the pilot coordinates aileron and rudder while managing pitch with the elevator, avoiding large or abrupt deflections near the stall.

practice.prompt: Match the control to its primary motion.
practice.explanation: Aileron-roll, elevator-pitch, and rudder-yaw are the primary pairings.

pairs.right: Roll
Pitch
Yaw

lessons.title: Coordinate and manage adverse yaw
lessons.concept: Aileron deflection can create unequal drag that yaws the nose opposite the intended roll, called adverse yaw.
lessons.explanation: The down-going aileron increases camber and lift but also drag. Differential or Frise ailerons reduce adverse yaw, while coordinated rudder counters what remains. The inclinometer helps the pilot detect slip or skid.
lessons.workedExample: To roll right, the left aileron moves down and can pull the nose left. Timely right rudder keeps the longitudinal axis aligned with the flight path.

practice.prompt: When rolling right, adverse yaw initially tends to move the nose which way?
practice.explanation: The down-going left aileron adds drag, tending to yaw the nose left unless coordinated rudder is used.
options: Right
Left
Straight up
Straight down

lessons.title: Secondary controls reshape workload
lessons.concept: Flaps, leading-edge devices, spoilers, and trim systems change lift, drag, control force, or descent behavior.
lessons.explanation: Flaps generally increase both lift and drag, allowing lower approach speed and steeper descent. Trim relieves sustained control pressure but does not hold altitude automatically. Spoilers reduce lift and increase drag.
lessons.workedExample: After configuring landing flaps and stabilizing on final, the pilot trims away steady elevator pressure while continuing to control airspeed and glidepath.

practice.prompt: What is trim primarily used to do?
practice.explanation: Trim balances control forces around a selected condition; the pilot remains responsible for the flight path.
options: Increase maximum structural load
Relieve sustained control pressure
Replace the elevator
Guarantee altitude hold

lessons.title: Adverse yaw and coordinated control
lessons.concept: Like all control surfaces at lower airspeeds, the vertical stabilizer/rudder becomes less effective and magnifies the control problems associated with adverse yaw.
lessons.explanation: The yaw is especially evident in aircraft with long wing spans. Application of the rudder is used to counteract adverse yaw. The amount of rudder control required is greatest at low airspeeds, high angles of attack, and with large aileron deflections.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how adverse yaw and coordinated control affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for adverse yaw and coordinated control. Which phrase completes "___, the vertical stabilizer/rudder becomes less effective and magnifies the control problems associated with adverse yaw."?
options: Ailerons control roll
Like all control surfaces at lower airspeeds
called adverse yaw
the object displaced returns toward the equilibrium state
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Like all control surfaces at lower airspeeds, the vertical stabilizer/rudder becomes less effective and magnifies the control problems associated with adverse yaw.

quiz.prompt: Which control primarily moves the airplane about the lateral axis?
quiz.explanation: Pitch about the lateral axis is controlled primarily by the elevator or stabilator.
options: Aileron
Elevator
Rudder
Spoiler

quiz.prompt: Match control and axis.
quiz.explanation: The primary flight controls act mainly about these axes.

pairs.right: Longitudinal axis
Lateral axis
Vertical axis

quiz.prompt: Extending flaps normally produces what combination?
quiz.explanation: Flaps alter camber and often area, increasing lift and drag.
options: Less lift and less drag
More lift and more drag
More thrust and less weight
No aerodynamic change

quiz.prompt: In the control-and-axes illustration, which surface initiates roll about the longitudinal axis?
options: Ailerons
Elevator
Rudder
Flaps together

image.alt: FAA PHAK Primary controls, movement, and axes, handbook page 6-3
image.caption: Primary controls, movement, and axes

quiz.explanation: Differential aileron movement creates a rolling moment about the longitudinal axis.

sections.title: Piston Powerplants and Propellers
sections.summary: Apply four-stroke engine cycle, reciprocating engine components, induction, ignition, and cooling, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Turn combustion into useful power
lessons.concept: A four-stroke reciprocating engine repeats intake, compression, power, and exhaust while the propeller converts shaft power into thrust.
lessons.explanation: The induction system meters air, the fuel system adds fuel, magnetos supply ignition independently of the main electrical system, and oil cools, cleans, seals, and lubricates. Instrument indications must be interpreted together rather than as isolated numbers.
lessons.workedExample: A rough engine with carburetor ice symptoms may improve after carburetor heat is selected, initially becoming rougher as ice melts. A falling oil-pressure indication paired with rising oil temperature demands prompt action under the checklist.

practice.prompt: Put each event with its four-stroke phase.
practice.explanation: The cycle is intake, compression, power, and exhaust.

pairs.right: Fuel-air charge enters
Charge is squeezed
Ignited gases drive the piston
Burned gases leave

lessons.title: Propeller and mixture choices change efficiency
lessons.concept: A propeller blade is a rotating airfoil; blade angle, rotational speed, forward speed, air density, and engine power determine thrust.
lessons.explanation: A fixed-pitch propeller is a compromise between climb and cruise. A constant-speed system varies blade angle to hold selected rpm. Mixture is adjusted as recommended to maintain efficient combustion as air density changes.
lessons.workedExample: In a climb with a constant-speed propeller, the governor changes blade angle to maintain selected rpm as aerodynamic load changes. The pilot still monitors manifold pressure, temperature, and limitations.

practice.prompt: What does a constant-speed propeller governor primarily maintain?
practice.explanation: The governor varies blade angle to maintain the selected rpm within its operating range.
options: A selected engine rpm
A fixed fuel quantity
Cabin pressure
A fixed groundspeed

lessons.title: Four-stroke engine cycle
lessons.concept: Even when the engine is operated at a fairly low speed, the four-stroke cycle takes place several hundred times each minute.
lessons.explanation: The exhaust stroke is used to purge the cylinder of burned gases. It begins when the exhaust valve opens, and the piston starts to move toward the cylinder head once again. [Figure 7-5] In a four-cylinder engine, each cylinder operates on a different stroke.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how four-stroke engine cycle affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on four-stroke engine cycle: "Even when the engine is operated at a fairly low speed, ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: the four-stroke cycle takes place several hundred times each minute
A four-stroke reciprocating engine repeats intake
engine power determine thrust
to place the pointer on the wingtip position
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Even when the engine is operated at a fairly low speed, the four-stroke cycle takes place several hundred times each minute.

lessons.title: Reciprocating engine components
lessons.concept: If repeated too frequently, this stress can weaken the cylinder components and eventually cause engine failure.
lessons.explanation: For any given rpm, there is a manifold pressure that should not be exceeded. If manifold pressure is excessive for a given rpm, the pressure within the cylinders could be exceeded, placing undue stress on the cylinders. A pilot can avoid conditions that overstress the cylinders by being constantly aware of the rpm, especially when increasing the manifold pressure.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how reciprocating engine components affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on reciprocating engine components: "___, this stress can weaken the cylinder components and eventually cause engine failure." What belongs in the blank?
options: A four-stroke reciprocating engine repeats intake
engine power determine thrust

lessons.title: Induction, ignition, and cooling
lessons.concept: Continuous operation of the engine depends on the simultaneous function of auxiliary systems, including the induction, ignition, fuel, oil, cooling, and exhaust systems.
lessons.explanation: Even when the engine is operated at a fairly low speed, the four-stroke cycle takes place several hundred times each minute. [Figure 7-5] In a four-cylinder engine, each cylinder operates on a different stroke. Continuous rotation of a crankshaft is maintained by the precise timing of the power strokes in each cylinder.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how induction, ignition, and cooling affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

If repeated too frequently

quiz.explanation: Combustion during the power stroke forces the piston down.
options: Intake
Compression

sections.title: Turbine Engines and Operating Limits
sections.summary: Apply turbojet, turbofan, turboprop, and turboshaft layouts, compression, combustion, and turbine sections, thrust, temperature, and rotational limits, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Turbojet, turbofan, turboprop, and turboshaft layouts
lessons.concept: [Figure 7-24] Turbofan Turbofans were developed to combine some of the best features of the turbojet and the turboprop.
lessons.explanation: Turboprop engines are most efficient at speeds between 250 and 400 mph and altitudes between 18,000 and 30,000 feet. They also perform well at the slow airspeeds required for takeoff and landing and are fuel efficient. The minimum specific fuel consumption Fuel injector of the turboprop engine is normally available in the altitude range of 25,000 feet to the tropopause.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how turbojet, turbofan, turboprop, and turboshaft layouts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Turbofan Turbofans were developed to combine some of the ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: combustion chamber
foreign object damage
best features of the turbojet and the turboprop
An OI caused a 737 to land on a runway occupied by a twin-engine turboprop.
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Turbofan Turbofans were developed to combine some of the best features of the turbojet and the turboprop.

lessons.title: Compression, combustion, and turbine sections
lessons.concept: Turbojet The turbojet engine consists of four sections—compressor, combustion chamber, turbine section, and exhaust.
lessons.explanation: The centrifugal-axial flow design uses both kinds of compressors to achieve the desired compression. The path the air takes through the engine and how power is produced determines the type of engine. There are four types of aircraft turbine engines—turbojet, turboprop, turbofan, and turboshaft.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how compression, combustion, and turbine sections affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Which item does the cited handbook specifically include under compression, combustion, and turbine sections?
options: four sections—compressor
the fire in the engine unintentionally goes out
foreign object damage
A four-stroke reciprocating engine repeats intake
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Turbojet The turbojet engine consists of four sections—compressor, combustion chamber, turbine section, and exhaust.

lessons.title: Thrust, temperature, and rotational limits
lessons.concept: They are engine temperature limits, foreign object damage, hot start, compressor stall, and flameout.
lessons.explanation: The high pressure compressor is governed by the N2 turbine wheel. The N2 turbine wheel is connected to the high pressure compressor through a concentric shaft. [Figure 7-27] Turbine Engine Operational Considerations The great variety of turbine engines makes it impractical to cover specific operational procedures, but there are certain operational considerations common to all turbine engines.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how thrust, temperature, and rotational limits affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: turbine section
the fire in the engine unintentionally goes out

engine power determine thrust
practice.explanation: The cited page states: They are engine temperature limits, foreign object damage, hot start, compressor stall, and flameout.

lessons.title: Turbine engine starts and abnormal indications
lessons.concept: Flameout A flameout occurs in the operation of a gas turbine engine in which the fire in the engine unintentionally goes out.
lessons.explanation: One system uses a variable inlet guide vane (VIGV) and variable stator vanes that direct the incoming air into the rotor blades at an appropriate angle. To prevent air pressure stalls, operate the aircraft within the parameters established by the manufacturer. If a compressor stall does develop, follow the procedures recommended in the AFM/POH.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how turbine engine starts and abnormal indications affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

quiz.explanation: Carburetor ice restricts airflow and commonly causes a gradual rpm loss with a fixed-pitch propeller.
options: A gradual rpm loss
An immediate oil quantity increase
Higher battery voltage
A brighter landing light

sections.title: Airframe and Supporting Systems
sections.summary: Apply fuel and electrical systems, hydraulic and pneumatic systems, landing gear and brakes, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Know the supporting systems
lessons.concept: Fuel, electrical, hydraulic, pressurization, oxygen, and anti-ice systems have distinct failure indications and backup paths.
lessons.explanation: An alternator normally supplies the electrical system and recharges the battery; the battery offers limited reserve. Fuel-system design determines venting, selector use, pumps, and unusable fuel. Pressurization and oxygen protect occupants from low ambient pressure but require specific emergency responses.
lessons.workedExample: An ammeter or loadmeter showing loss of alternator output prompts confirmation, load shedding, checklist use, and landing before the battery is exhausted rather than simply cycling switches repeatedly.

practice.prompt: After confirmed alternator failure, why should nonessential electrical loads be shed?
practice.explanation: Reducing electrical demand preserves the battery for essential equipment and landing.
options: To make the propeller turn faster
To extend limited battery endurance
To increase fuel octane
To pressurize the cabin

lessons.title: Fuel and electrical systems
lessons.concept: This method of heat is very safe as an overheat switch is provided on all combustion heaters, which is wired into the heater’s electrical system to shut off the fuel in the case of malfunction.
lessons.explanation: Air flowing over the outside of the burner chamber and inside the jacket of the heater absorbs the heat and carries it through ducts into the cabin. As the thermostat reaches its preset temperature, it turns off the fuel solenoid and stops the flow of fuel into the burner chamber. When ventilation air cools to the point that the thermostat again turns the fuel solenoid on, the burner starts again.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how fuel and electrical systems affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about fuel and electrical systems: "This method of heat is very safe as an ___, which is wired into the heater’s electrical system to shut off the fuel in the case of malfunction."

practice.explanation: The cited page states: This method of heat is very safe as an overheat switch is provided on all combustion heaters, which is wired into the heater’s electrical system to shut off the fuel in the case of malfunction.

lessons.title: Hydraulic and pneumatic systems
lessons.concept: Hydraulic Systems There are multiple applications for hydraulic use in aircraft, depending on the complexity of the aircraft.
lessons.explanation: The generator/alternator voltage output should be higher than the battery voltage. For example, a 12-volt battery would be fed by a generator/alternator system of approximately 14 volts. The difference in voltage keeps the battery charged.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how hydraulic and pneumatic systems affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Hydraulic Systems There are multiple applications for hydraulic use in aircraft, depending on the complexity of the aircraft.

lessons.title: Landing gear and brakes
lessons.concept: Retractable landing gear is designed to streamline the airplane by allowing the landing gear to be stowed inside the structure during cruising flight.
lessons.explanation: Because of these disadvantages, specific training is required to operate tailwheel airplanes. Fixed and Retractable Landing Gear Landing gear can also be classified as either fixed or retractable. Fixed landing gear always remains extended and has the advantage of simplicity combined with low maintenance.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how landing gear and brakes affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Retractable landing gear is designed to streamline the airplane by allowing the landing gear to be stowed inside the structure during cruising flight.

lessons.title: Pressurization and oxygen
lessons.concept: The degree of pressurization and the operating altitude of the aircraft are limited by several critical design factors.
lessons.explanation: The vacuum relief prevents ambient pressure from exceeding cabin pressure by allowing external air to enter the cabin when ambient pressure exceeds cabin pressure. The flight deck control switch actuates the dump valve. When this switch is positioned to ram, a solenoid valve opens, causing the valve to dump cabin air into the atmosphere.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pressurization and oxygen affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: The degree of pressurization and the operating altitude of the aircraft are limited by several critical design factors.
