sections.title: Flight Instruments
sections.summary: Apply pitot-static pressure sources, airspeed, altimeter, and vertical-speed indications, gyroscopic principles and instruments, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Pitot-static instruments compare pressure
lessons.concept: The altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator use static pressure; the airspeed indicator also uses pitot total pressure.
lessons.explanation: A blocked pitot tube or static port affects instruments differently depending on whether drain paths remain open. Altimeter setting, nonstandard temperature, and pressure changes create errors that pilots must anticipate.
lessons.workedExample: If the static port is blocked, the altimeter freezes, the VSI reads zero, and the airspeed indicator becomes unreliable as altitude changes. An approved alternate-static source may restore usable indications with known errors.

practice.prompt: Match each instrument to the way it uses pressure.
practice.explanation: The ASI compares pitot with static pressure, the altimeter senses pressure level, and the VSI senses its rate of change.

pairs.right: Static pressure acting on aneroid wafers
Static-pressure change through a calibrated leak
Pitot-to-static pressure difference

lessons.title: Gyros and compasses have limits
lessons.concept: Rigidity in space and precession support gyroscopic instruments, while the magnetic compass aligns with Earth's field and has turning and acceleration errors.
lessons.explanation: The attitude and heading indicators depend on gyroscopic or solid-state references; the turn coordinator displays rate and roll trend. Vacuum, pressure, or electrical power-source failures can remove multiple instruments together.
lessons.workedExample: A vacuum-system failure can make both an attitude indicator and heading indicator slowly unreliable. Cross-checking the turn coordinator, compass, and performance instruments helps detect the common-source failure.

practice.prompt: Why can two gyroscopic instruments fail together?
practice.explanation: Common vacuum or electrical power can create a shared failure mode.
options: They may share a power source
All gyros use pitot pressure
The compass controls them
They share the fuel selector

lessons.title: Electronic displays still need cross-checks
lessons.concept: A primary flight display integrates attitude, airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, and navigation, but sensors and software can still fail or mislead.
lessons.explanation: The air-data computer and attitude-heading reference system replace many mechanical functions. Redundancy, reversionary modes, annunciations, and disciplined cross-checking remain essential; a bright display is not proof that its data are valid.
lessons.workedExample: If the airspeed tape disagrees with pitch, power, and standby indications, the pilot verifies the failure, uses reliable backup information, and follows the abnormal checklist rather than chasing the suspect tape.

practice.prompt: What is the best response to one implausible indication on a glass display?
practice.explanation: Cross-checking independent sources helps identify the failed sensor or display.
options: Trust it because it is digital
Cross-check independent instruments and aircraft performance
Reset every circuit breaker
Turn off all avionics

lessons.title: Airspeed, altimeter, and vertical-speed indications
lessons.concept: As on traditional analogue ASIs, the electronic airspeed tape displays the color-coded ranges for the flap operating range, WPT _ _ _ _ _ _ DIS _ _ ._ NM DTK _ _ _°T TRK 360° 113.00 Slip skid indicator Attitude indicator 110.60 134.000 118.000 COM1 123.800 Altimeter 118.000 COM2 130 120 110 1 100 9 20 80 80 270° 70...
lessons.explanation: The TAS is displayed at the bottom of the tape through the input to the air data computer (ADC) from the outside air temperature probe. Airspeed markings for VX, VY, and rotation speed (VR) are displayed for pilot reference. An additional pilot-controlled airspeed bug is available to set at any desired reference speed.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how airspeed, altimeter, and vertical-speed indications affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about airspeed, altimeter, and vertical-speed indications: "___, VY, and rotation speed (VR) are displayed for pilot reference."
options: the airspeed indicator also uses pitot total pressure
Airspeed markings for VX
A primary flight display integrates attitude
Because courses are measured in reference to geographical meridians that point toward TN
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Airspeed markings for VX, VY, and rotation speed (VR) are displayed for pilot reference.

lessons.title: Gyroscopic principles and instruments
lessons.concept: To understand how these instruments operate requires knowledge of the instrument power systems, gyroscopic principles, and the operating principles of each instrument.
lessons.explanation: Supporting Instruments Figure 8-17. Gyroscopic Flight Instruments Several flight instruments utilize the properties of a gyroscope for their operation. The most common instruments containing gyroscopes are the turn coordinator, heading indicator, and the attitude indicator.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how gyroscopic principles and instruments affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing gyroscopic principles and instruments, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "___, gyroscopic principles, and the operating principles of each instrument."
options: To understand how these instruments operate requires knowledge of the instrument power systems
while the magnetic compass aligns with Earth's field and has turning and acceleration errors
the airspeed indicator also uses pitot total pressure
humidity change aircraft and engine performance
practice.explanation: The cited page states: To understand how these instruments operate requires knowledge of the instrument power systems, gyroscopic principles, and the operating principles of each instrument.

lessons.title: Magnetic compass errors
lessons.concept: [Figure 8-36A] Left turn A Right turn DIP R CA D ct 30 p Di e ff e 3 N 33 30 DIP 3 p Di 3 Acceleration Error The magnetic dip and the forces of inertia cause magnetic compass errors when accelerating and decelerating on easterly and westerly headings.
lessons.explanation: Because of this lead of the compass card, or float assembly, a northerly turn should be stopped prior to arrival at the desired heading. This compass error is amplified with the proximity to either magnetic pole. One rule of thumb to correct for this leading error is to stop the turn 15 degrees plus half of the latitude (i.e., if the aircraft is being operated in a position near 40 degrees latitude, the turn should be stopped 15+20=35 degrees prior to the desired heading).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how magnetic compass errors affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "The magnetic dip and the forces of inertia cause magnetic compass errors when ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: while the magnetic compass aligns with Earth's field and has turning and acceleration errors
Airspeed markings for VX
accelerating and decelerating on easterly and westerly headings
steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The magnetic dip and the forces of inertia cause magnetic compass errors when accelerating and decelerating on easterly and westerly headings.

quiz.prompt: Which instrument uses both pitot and static pressure?
quiz.explanation: The ASI measures the difference between pitot total pressure and static pressure.
options: Altimeter
Vertical speed indicator
Airspeed indicator
Magnetic compass

quiz.prompt: Match each indication to the primary sensed quantity.
quiz.explanation: Each instrument converts pressure information into a different flight indication.

pairs.right: Static pressure level
Rate of static-pressure change
Pitot-static pressure difference

quiz.prompt: What altimeter setting represents standard pressure?
quiz.explanation: Standard pressure is 29.92 inHg.

options: Airspeed indicator
Altimeter

sections.title: Flight Manuals and Documents
sections.summary: Apply approved flight manual and operating handbook, required aircraft documents, airworthiness directives and inspections, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: The approved manual governs the airplane
lessons.concept: The approved AFM or POH supplies operating limitations, procedures, performance, and loading information specific to an aircraft model and, where marked, the individual airplane.
lessons.explanation: Section numbering is standardized in many manuals. Limitations are mandatory, while normal and emergency procedures turn those limits into actions. Supplements modify the manual for installed optional equipment.
lessons.workedExample: Installing an approved GPS may add a supplement with new limitations and procedures. The pilot reviews that supplement as part of the operating limitations, not as optional marketing material.

practice.prompt: Where should a pilot look for mandatory operating limits?
practice.explanation: Approved limitations and required placards establish the airplane's operating boundaries.
options: POH/AFM limitations section and required placards
A general aviation magazine
A fuel receipt
An airport directory only

lessons.title: Required documents establish status
lessons.concept: Airworthiness and registration certificates, operating limitations, and current weight-and-balance information must be available as required for operation.
lessons.explanation: The airworthiness certificate remains effective only while the aircraft conforms to its type design and is in condition for safe operation. Registration identifies the aircraft but does not prove its mechanical condition.
lessons.workedExample: A current registration certificate cannot make an airplane airworthy when a required inspection is overdue. Legal operation depends on both documentation and actual condition.

practice.prompt: Match document to purpose.
practice.explanation: Each required document answers a different legal or operational question.

pairs.right: Shows approved airworthiness status
Identifies registered ownership
Defines approved operating boundaries
Supports loading determination

lessons.title: Inspections and records prove compliance
lessons.concept: Maintenance records show inspections, repairs, alterations, and airworthiness-directive compliance; pilots use them with a physical preflight to determine airworthiness.
lessons.explanation: Annual and, when applicable, 100-hour inspections have different return-to-service rules. Other recurring requirements can include transponder, ELT, altimeter/static-system, and VOR checks depending on the operation.
lessons.workedExample: For a VFR rental flight, the pilot checks that applicable inspections are current and discrepancies resolved, then still conducts a preflight inspection. Paper compliance alone cannot reveal new damage.

practice.prompt: Does a completed annual inspection eliminate the need for a pilot preflight?
practice.explanation: Inspections document maintenance status; the pilot must still determine that the aircraft is safe for the proposed flight.
options: Yes, for 12 calendar months
No, the pilot must assess current condition before flight
Yes, if the tanks are full
Only at towered airports

lessons.title: Airworthiness directives and inspections
lessons.concept: Airworthiness Directives (ADs), under 14 CFR, part 39, define the authority and responsibility of the Administrator for requiring the necessary corrective action.
lessons.explanation: If a special flight permit is needed, assistance and the necessary forms may be obtained from the local FSDO 9-12 or Designated Airworthiness Representative (DAR). [Figure 9-10] Airworthiness Directives (ADs) A primary safety function of the FAA is to require correction of unsafe conditions found in an aircraft, aircraft engine, propeller, or appliance when such conditions exist and are likely to exist or develop in other products of the same design. The unsafe condition may exist because of a design defect, maintenance, or other causes.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how airworthiness directives and inspections affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about airworthiness directives and inspections: "Airworthiness Directives (ADs), under 14 CFR, part 39, ___."
options: pilots use them with a physical preflight to determine airworthiness
current weight-and-balance information must be available as required for operation
course of action in a changing flight environment
define the authority and responsibility of the Administrator for requiring the necessary corrective action
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Airworthiness Directives (ADs), under 14 CFR, part 39, define the authority and responsibility of the Administrator for requiring the necessary corrective action.

quiz.prompt: Which POH/AFM material is mandatory?
quiz.explanation: Approved limitations define mandatory operating boundaries.
options: Limitations
The table of contents
A manufacturer's advertisement
A training-school note

quiz.prompt: Match the aircraft record or document.
quiz.explanation: Together these sources establish identity, approval, condition history, and operating information.

pairs.right: Registered aircraft identity
Approved status while conformity and condition continue
Inspection and repair history
Procedures, limitations, and performance

quiz.prompt: Who is responsible for determining that an aircraft is in condition for safe flight before acting as PIC?
quiz.explanation: The PIC must determine the aircraft is airworthy and safe for the intended operation.
options: The pilot in command
Only the last mechanic
The airport manager
The fuel provider

quiz.prompt: The FAA page shows sample certificates. Which document identifies the aircraft's registered owner but does not by itself establish mechanical airworthiness?
options: Registration certificate
Airworthiness certificate
Weight-and-balance report
Equipment list

image.caption: Aircraft registration documentation

quiz.explanation: Registration records aircraft ownership/registration; current conformity and safe condition are separate airworthiness questions.

sections.title: Weight and Balance
sections.summary: Apply weight, balance, and center-of-gravity effects, datum, arm, moment, and index, loading computations, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Weight and CG both matter
lessons.concept: Maximum weight protects structural and performance margins, while center-of-gravity limits protect stability, control, and recoverability.
lessons.explanation: An aft CG reduces longitudinal stability and can make stall recovery difficult. A forward CG increases stability but may require excessive elevator force and increases stall speed or takeoff distance. Lateral imbalance can also affect controllability.
lessons.workedExample: A loading plan below maximum gross weight can still be unsafe if baggage moves the CG aft of its limit; both total weight and CG must pass.

practice.prompt: Which is a typical effect of an excessively aft CG?
practice.explanation: An aft CG reduces the tail force and longitudinal stability, narrowing recovery margins.
options: Greater longitudinal stability
Reduced stability and more difficult stall recovery
Lower cruise speed only
Automatic load-factor protection

lessons.title: Moment turns loading into math
lessons.concept: Moment equals weight multiplied by arm, and loaded center of gravity equals total moment divided by total weight.
lessons.explanation: Arms are measured from the datum with the sign convention defined by the manufacturer. Moments may be divided by 100 or 1,000 in a loading form, so units and index scale must remain consistent.
lessons.workedExample: A 150-pound passenger at a 40-inch arm creates 6,000 pound-inches of moment. Moving the passenger aft increases moment and shifts the total CG aft without changing total weight.

practice.prompt: What moment is produced by 150 lb at an arm of 40 in?
practice.explanation: Moment = weight × arm = 150 × 40 = 6,000 lb-in.

lessons.title: Finish with the approved envelope
lessons.concept: A completed loading calculation is acceptable only when total weight and the resulting CG or moment fall within the approved envelope for that weight.
lessons.explanation: Loading graphs are interpolation tools; the final envelope check accounts for limits that may change with weight. Fuel burn can move CG, so departure, landing, and critical intermediate conditions may all need evaluation.
lessons.workedExample: A point just inside the takeoff envelope may move outside as fuel burns from a forward tank. The pilot calculates the expected landing condition instead of assuming lower weight is always safer.

practice.prompt: After plotting a loading point inside the envelope at takeoff, what else may require checking?
practice.explanation: Fuel burn changes weight and can shift CG, so later loading conditions may matter.
options: Only tire color
The CG after expected fuel burn
The airport restaurant hours
The magnetic compass deviation card font

lessons.title: Weight, balance, and center-of-gravity effects
lessons.concept: Effects of Adverse Balance Adverse balance conditions affect flight characteristics in much the same manner as those mentioned for an excess weight condition.
lessons.explanation: Flying an aircraft that is out of balance can produce increased pilot fatigue with obvious effects on the safety and efficiency of flight. The pilot’s natural correction for longitudinal unbalance is a change of trim to remove the excessive control pressure. Excessive trim, however, has the effect of reducing not only aerodynamic efficiency but also primary control travel distance in the direction the trim is applied.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how weight, balance, and center-of-gravity effects affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing weight, balance, and center-of-gravity effects, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "Effects of Adverse Balance Adverse balance conditions affect flight characteristics in much the same manner ___."
options: loaded center of gravity equals total moment divided by total weight
while center-of-gravity limits protect stability
steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path
as those mentioned for an excess weight condition
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Effects of Adverse Balance Adverse balance conditions affect flight characteristics in much the same manner as those mentioned for an excess weight condition.

quiz.prompt: An item weighs 80 lb and is placed at a 50 in arm. What is its moment?
Match each loading term.
quiz.explanation: These definitions form the basis of every loading calculation.

pairs.right: Reference plane for arms
Distance from datum
Weight multiplied by arm
Total moment divided by total weight

quiz.prompt: Total weight is 2,000 lb and total moment is 80,000 lb-in. What is the CG arm?
What does a loading point outside the FAA center-of-gravity envelope indicate?
options: The loading is outside approved weight/CG limits
The calculation is automatically rounded into limits
Only cruise power must be reduced
The aircraft is legal if total weight is low

image.alt: FAA PHAK Loading graph and center-of-gravity envelope, handbook page 10-8
image.caption: Loading graph and center-of-gravity envelope

quiz.explanation: Both weight and CG must place the loading point inside the approved envelope.

sections.title: Aircraft Performance
sections.summary: Apply performance factors and density altitude, takeoff and landing charts, climb performance and service ceiling, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Density altitude predicts performance
lessons.concept: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperature and is a practical index of how the airplane performs.
lessons.explanation: High temperature, high elevation, low pressure, and humidity reduce density. The result is less engine power, propeller thrust, and wing lift at a given true airspeed, producing longer takeoff roll and weaker climb.
lessons.workedExample: At a mountain airport on a hot afternoon, the airplane may accelerate sluggishly even though the altimeter indicates only field elevation. A density-altitude calculation reveals the performance altitude the aircraft effectively experiences.

practice.prompt: Field elevation is 2,500 ft and altimeter setting is 29.42 inHg. Estimate pressure altitude using field elevation + (29.92-setting)×1,000.

lessons.title: Read performance charts conservatively
lessons.concept: Approved performance charts require the exact configuration, weight, altitude, temperature, wind, runway, and technique stated in their conditions and notes.
lessons.explanation: Interpolate carefully and round toward the conservative result. A dry paved level-runway chart does not directly predict a wet grass upslope takeoff. Add every published correction rather than choosing the most favorable one.
lessons.workedExample: If a chart gives 1,200 feet over a 50-foot obstacle before a 15-percent grass correction, the corrected minimum is 1,380 feet. A prudent runway margin is then added for real-world variation.

practice.prompt: A chart distance is 1,200 ft and the POH requires a 15% increase. What is the corrected distance?

lessons.title: Climb, range, and landing involve tradeoffs
lessons.concept: Best angle of climb gains the most altitude per horizontal distance; best rate gains the most altitude per unit time, and both change with altitude.
lessons.explanation: Wind changes groundspeed and therefore climb gradient, range, and time without changing the airplane's indicated climb performance. Weight, configuration, surface condition, slope, and braking action strongly influence landing distance.
lessons.workedExample: To clear a nearby obstacle, VX may be appropriate until clear; for efficient altitude gain afterward, transition to VY. The exact speeds come from the current POH and may vary with conditions.

practice.prompt: Match each performance speed.
practice.explanation: VX optimizes altitude per distance, VY altitude per time, and best glide distance without power.

pairs.right: Best angle of climb
Best rate of climb
Maximum distance in still air after power loss

lessons.title: Takeoff and landing charts
lessons.concept: Performance charts allow a pilot to predict the takeoff, climb, cruise, and landing performance of an aircraft.
lessons.explanation: Combined graphs allow the pilot to predict aircraft performance for variations in density altitude, weight, and winds all on one chart. Because of the vast amount of information that can be extracted from this type of chart, it is important to be very accurate in reading the chart. A small error in the beginning can lead to a large error at the end.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how takeoff and landing charts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "___, climb, cruise, and landing performance of an aircraft." Which detail completes the statement?
options: time without changing the airplane's indicated climb performance
flight hours/hour pounds of fuel/hour or 120 Indicated
carbon monoxide can degrade performance even when a pilot is technically legal
Performance charts allow a pilot to predict the takeoff
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Performance charts allow a pilot to predict the takeoff, climb, cruise, and landing performance of an aircraft.

lessons.title: Climb performance and service ceiling
lessons.concept: 24,000 22,000 Standard altitude (feet) 20,000 18,000 Absolute ceiling 16,000 Service ceiling 14,000 specific endurance = 12,000 10,000 8,000 Best angle of climb (Vx) Best rate of climb (Vy) or 6,000 specific endurance = 4,000 2,000 Sea level 70 80 90 100 110 flight hours/hour pounds of fuel/hour or 120 Indicated...
lessons.explanation: Range must be clearly distinguished from the item of endurance. Range involves consideration of flying distance, while endurance involves consideration of flying time. Thus, it is appropriate to define a separate term, specific endurance.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how climb performance and service ceiling affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for climb performance and service ceiling. Which phrase completes "24,000 22,000 Standard altitude (feet) 20,000 18,000 Absolute ceiling 16,000 Service ceiling 14,000 specific endurance = 12,000 10,000 8,000 Best angle of climb (Vx) Best rate of climb (Vy) or 6,000 specific endurance = 4,000 2,000 Sea level 70 80 90 100 110 ___..."?
options: flight hours/hour pounds of fuel/hour or 120 Indicated
This type of table is designed to give TAS
time without changing the airplane's indicated climb performance
14.70 pounds per square inch (psi) of surface
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 24,000 22,000 Standard altitude (feet) 20,000 18,000 Absolute ceiling 16,000 Service ceiling 14,000 specific endurance = 12,000 10,000 8,000 Best angle of climb (Vx) Best rate of climb (Vy) or 6,000 specific endurance = 4,000 2,000 Sea level 70 80 90 100 110 flight hours/hour pounds of fuel/hour or 120 Indicated...

lessons.title: Range, endurance, and fuel planning
lessons.concept: This type of table is designed to give TAS, fuel consumption, endurance in hours, and range in miles at specific cruise configurations.
lessons.explanation: The next example is a cruise and range performance chart. Use Figure 11-27 to determine the cruise and range performance under the given conditions. Sample Problem 6 Pressure Altitude...............................................5,000 feet RPM..................................................................2,400 rpm Fuel Carrying Capacity..................38 gallons, no reserve Find 5,000 feet pressure altitude in the first column on the left side of the table.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how range, endurance, and fuel planning affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about range, endurance, and fuel planning: "___, fuel consumption, endurance in hours, and range in miles at specific cruise configurations."
options: flight hours/hour pounds of fuel/hour or 120 Indicated
time without changing the airplane's indicated climb performance
This type of table is designed to give TAS
From To VISUAL FLIGHT LOG TIME OF DEPARTURE
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This type of table is designed to give TAS, fuel consumption, endurance in hours, and range in miles at specific cruise configurations.

quiz.prompt: Which conditions produce the highest density altitude?
quiz.explanation: High elevation, high temperature, and low pressure raise density altitude.
options: Low, cold, high pressure
High, hot, low pressure
Sea level, cold, dry
Low elevation at standard temperature

quiz.prompt: Match the performance measure.
quiz.explanation: Wind affects ground distance and therefore gradient even when rate is unchanged.

pairs.right: Altitude gained per horizontal distance
Altitude gained per unit time
Altitude gain divided by ground distance

quiz.prompt: A POH takeoff distance is 1,000 ft and a required correction adds 20%. What corrected distance should be used before adding a safety margin?
On the density-altitude chart, moving to a higher temperature at the same pressure altitude produces what result?
options: Higher density altitude
Lower density altitude
No change
Zero pressure altitude

image.alt: FAA PHAK Density altitude chart, handbook page 11-4
image.caption: Density altitude chart

quiz.explanation: Warmer air is less dense, so density altitude increases at a fixed pressure altitude.

sections.title: Weather Theory
sections.summary: Apply atmospheric pressure, temperature, and moisture, wind and global circulation, stability, lapse rates, and clouds, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Pressure, heat, and moisture drive weather
lessons.concept: Unequal heating creates pressure differences and circulation, while temperature and moisture determine density, visibility, clouds, and precipitation.
lessons.explanation: Air flows in response to pressure-gradient force but is turned by Earth's rotation and modified by surface friction. Relative humidity rises as temperature approaches dewpoint even when water-vapor content does not change.
lessons.workedExample: After sunset, surface air cools toward its dewpoint. A small temperature-dewpoint spread with light wind can support radiation fog, especially in low-lying moist areas.

practice.prompt: What happens to relative humidity as air cools toward its dewpoint without losing moisture?
practice.explanation: Cooling toward dewpoint raises relative humidity and may produce saturation.
options: It generally increases
It always becomes zero
It is unrelated to temperature
It becomes wind speed

lessons.title: Stability shapes clouds and turbulence
lessons.concept: Stable air resists vertical motion and favors layered clouds, smooth air, and widespread steady precipitation; unstable air favors vertical clouds, turbulence, and showery precipitation.
lessons.explanation: Lifting can occur through convection, terrain, fronts, or convergence. Cloud bases form when rising air cools to saturation. Temperature-dewpoint spread offers a rough estimate of convective cloud-base height.
lessons.workedExample: With surface temperature 24 °C and dewpoint 14 °C, the 10-degree spread suggests a convective cloud base near 4,000 feet AGL using roughly 400 feet per degree Celsius.

practice.prompt: Estimate convective cloud-base height AGL for 24 °C temperature and 14 °C dewpoint using 400 ft per °C of spread.
practice.explanation: The spread is 10 °C; 10 × 400 = about 4,000 ft AGL.

lessons.title: Recognize fronts, storms, and icing
lessons.concept: Fronts organize wind shifts, pressure changes, clouds, precipitation, and turbulence; thunderstorms add severe turbulence, lightning, hail, icing, wind shear, and heavy precipitation.
lessons.explanation: Every thunderstorm needs moisture, unstable air, and lift and progresses through cumulus, mature, and dissipating stages. Structural icing requires visible moisture and freezing temperatures at the aircraft surface; freezing rain can create especially rapid accumulation.
lessons.workedExample: A mature cell near a route is avoided by a wide margin rather than penetrated beneath its anvil. A pilot also considers outflow, gust fronts, and cells hidden in cloud or precipitation.

practice.prompt: Match thunderstorm stage to dominant airflow.
practice.explanation: The mature stage contains both strong updrafts and downdrafts and the full range of hazards.

pairs.right: Mostly updrafts
Updrafts and downdrafts
Mostly downdrafts

lessons.title: Wind and global circulation
lessons.concept: Wind Patterns In the Northern Hemisphere, the flow of air from areas of high to low pressure is deflected to the right and produces a clockwise circulation around an area of high pressure.
lessons.explanation: The combination of atmospheric pressure differences, Coriolis force, friction, and temperature differences of the air near the earth cause two kinds of atmospheric motion: convective currents (upward and downward motion) and wind (horizontal motion). Currents and winds are important as they affect takeoff, landing, and cruise flight operations. Most importantly, currents and winds or atmospheric circulation cause weather changes.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wind and global circulation affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for wind and global circulation. Which phrase completes "Wind Patterns In the Northern Hemisphere, the flow of air from areas of high to low pressure is deflected to the right and produces a ___."?
options: thunderstorms add severe turbulence
clockwise circulation around an area of high pressure
Unequal heating creates pressure differences and circulation
the spanwise pressure difference also produces wingtip vortices and induced drag
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Wind Patterns In the Northern Hemisphere, the flow of air from areas of high to low pressure is deflected to the right and produces a clockwise circulation around an area of high pressure.

lessons.title: Air masses and fronts
lessons.concept: Whereas warm fronts bring low ceilings, poor visibility, and rain, cold fronts bring sudden storms, gusty winds, turbulence, and sometimes hail or tornadoes.
lessons.explanation: Cold front cross-section with surface weather chart depiction and associated METAR. Violent weather activity is associated with cold fronts, and the weather usually occurs along the frontal boundary, not in advance. However, squall lines can form during the summer months as far as 200 miles in advance of a strong cold front.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how air masses and fronts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Whereas warm fronts bring low ceilings, poor visibility, and rain, ___, gusty winds, turbulence, and sometimes hail or tornadoes." Which detail completes the statement?
options: cold fronts bring sudden storms
thunderstorms add severe turbulence
Unequal heating creates pressure differences and circulation
the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Whereas warm fronts bring low ceilings, poor visibility, and rain, cold fronts bring sudden storms, gusty winds, turbulence, and sometimes hail or tornadoes.

lessons.title: Thunderstorms and turbulence
lessons.concept: Turbulence Potentially hazardous turbulence is present in all thunderstorms, and a severe thunderstorm can destroy an aircraft.
lessons.explanation: Since the vortex extends well into the cloud, any pilot inadvertently caught on instruments in a severe thunderstorm could encounter a hidden vortex. Families of tornadoes have been observed as appendages of the main cloud extending several miles outward from the area of lightning and precipitation. Thus, any cloud connected to a severe thunderstorm carries a threat of violence.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how thunderstorms and turbulence affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on thunderstorms and turbulence: "Turbulence Potentially hazardous turbulence is present in all thunderstorms, and ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: cold fronts bring sudden storms
all IFR aircraft operating within the TRSA
Single-pilot resource management uses all available people
a severe thunderstorm can destroy an aircraft
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Turbulence Potentially hazardous turbulence is present in all thunderstorms, and a severe thunderstorm can destroy an aircraft.

quiz.prompt: Which cloud pattern most often indicates unstable air?
quiz.explanation: Unstable air supports vertical development, turbulence, and showers.
options: Tall cumulus with showery precipitation
Uniform stratus with smooth air
No vertical motion anywhere
A temperature inversion alone

quiz.prompt: Match weather feature and typical signal.
quiz.explanation: Front type helps anticipate the sequence and concentration of weather.

pairs.right: Widespread layered clouds ahead
Narrower band with more abrupt change
Cold front overtakes a warm front

quiz.prompt: Temperature is 20 °C and dewpoint is 15 °C. Estimate a convective cloud base using 400 ft per °C of spread.
quiz.explanation: The 5 °C spread × 400 gives about 2,000 ft AGL.

quiz.prompt: In the thunderstorm life-cycle figure, which stage contains both updrafts and downdrafts?
options: Mature
Cumulus only
Dissipating only
Pre-cumulus

image.alt: FAA PHAK Thunderstorm life cycle and hazards, handbook page 12-23
image.caption: Thunderstorm life cycle and hazards

quiz.explanation: Precipitation initiates downdrafts while updrafts persist, marking the mature stage.

sections.title: Aviation Weather Services
sections.summary: Apply preflight briefing responsibilities, surface observations and metar, terminal forecasts and winds aloft, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Build a briefing from multiple sources
lessons.concept: No single weather product supplies the whole picture; a briefing combines current observations, forecasts, advisories, radar, pilot reports, and NOTAM-related constraints.
lessons.explanation: A standard briefing supports a planned flight, an abbreviated briefing updates selected information, and an outlook briefing supports a flight six or more hours away. Automated tools are useful, but pilots remain responsible for interpreting the information and obtaining updates.
lessons.workedExample: The evening before a morning trip, a pilot uses an outlook briefing to identify major systems, then obtains a standard briefing near departure and checks updates en route.

practice.prompt: Which briefing is intended primarily for a flight more than six hours away?
practice.explanation: An outlook briefing is used for planning when departure is at least six hours away.
options: Outlook briefing
Abbreviated briefing
Inflight emergency briefing
Airport advisory

lessons.title: Decode observations and forecasts
lessons.concept: METARs report observed airport weather, TAFs forecast terminal conditions, and PIREPs report conditions actually encountered aloft.
lessons.explanation: Codes convey wind, visibility, weather, sky condition, temperature/dewpoint, and pressure compactly. Valid time and modifiers matter: a report can be correctly decoded but too old or too distant to describe the route.
lessons.workedExample: In `METAR KABC 121755Z 22012G20KT 5SM -RA BKN020`, winds are from 220 degrees at 12 knots gusting 20, visibility is five statute miles, light rain is present, and the ceiling is broken at 2,000 feet AGL.

practice.prompt: Match product to purpose.
practice.explanation: METARs observe, TAFs forecast terminal weather, and PIREPs add airborne observations.

pairs.right: Routine surface observation
Terminal forecast
Pilot-observed condition aloft

lessons.title: Use advisories and radar with limits
lessons.concept: AIRMETs, SIGMETs, and convective SIGMETs highlight significant hazards, while radar estimates precipitation rather than directly measuring every aviation hazard.
lessons.explanation: Radar age, beam geometry, attenuation, and product latency limit tactical use. A gap in returns can reflect attenuation rather than a safe corridor. PIREPs help validate cloud tops, icing, turbulence, and conditions between stations.
lessons.workedExample: A pilot sees a dark gap between intense cells on a delayed radar image. Instead of treating it as a passage, the pilot maintains wide separation, checks cell movement and product age, and uses updated weather and ATC support strategically.

practice.prompt: Why is a datalink radar image unsuitable for weaving between thunderstorms?
practice.explanation: Latency and radar limitations make datalink imagery a strategic avoidance tool, not tactical storm penetration guidance.
options: It may be delayed and does not provide a real-time tactical picture
It shows only airport pavement
It cannot show precipitation
It is a legal clearance

lessons.title: Preflight briefing responsibilities
lessons.concept: Get your preflight weather briefing!
lessons.explanation: potential delay when using in-cockpit NEXRAD capabilities, as the movement and/or intensification of weather could adversely affect safety of flight. Understand that the common perception of a “5-minute latency” with radar data is not always correct. Having in-cockpit weather capabilities does not circumvent the need for a complete weather briefing before takeoff.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how preflight briefing responsibilities affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "Having in-cockpit weather capabilities does not circumvent the ___."
options: No single weather product supplies the whole picture
need for a complete weather briefing before takeoff
PIREPs report conditions actually encountered aloft
A primary flight display integrates attitude
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Having in-cockpit weather capabilities does not circumvent the need for a complete weather briefing before takeoff.

lessons.title: Surface observations and METAR
lessons.concept: Weather Depiction Chart A weather depiction chart details surface conditions as derived from METAR and other surface observations.
lessons.explanation: The weather depiction chart is prepared and transmitted by computer every 3 hours beginning at 0100Z time and is valid data for the forecast period. It is designed to be used for flight planning by giving an overall picture of the weather across the United States. [Figure 13-12] The weather depiction chart also provides a graphic display of IFR, VFR, and marginal VFR (MVFR) weather.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how surface observations and metar affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about surface observations and metar: "Weather Depiction Chart A weather depiction chart details surface conditions ___."
options: PIREPs report conditions actually encountered aloft
• Airmen’s Meteorological Conditions (AIRMET) • Significant Meteorological
14.70 pounds per square inch (psi) of surface
as derived from METAR and other surface observations
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Weather Depiction Chart A weather depiction chart details surface conditions as derived from METAR and other surface observations.

lessons.concept: Aviation Routine Weather Reports (METARs) • Special Aviation Reports (SPECIs) • Terminal Area Forecasts (TAFs) and their amendments • NEXRAD (regional and CONUS) precipitation maps • Notice to Airmen (NOTAM) Distant and Flight Data Center • Airmen’s Meteorological Conditions (AIRMET) • Significant Meteorological...
lessons.explanation: Pilots have a choice between FAA-certified devices or portable receivers as a source of weather data. Data Link Weather Products Flight Information Service- Broadcast (FIS-B) Flight Information Service–Broadcast (FIS-B) is a ground broadcast service provided through the Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast (ADS-B) Services network over the 978 MHz UAT data link. The FAA FIS-B system provides pilots and flight crews of properly-equipped aircraft with a flightdeck display of certain aviation weather and aeronautical information which are listed below.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how terminal forecasts and winds aloft affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: PIREPs report conditions actually encountered aloft
No single weather product supplies the whole picture
• Airmen’s Meteorological Conditions (AIRMET) • Significant Meteorological
as those mentioned for an excess weight condition
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Aviation Routine Weather Reports (METARs) • Special Aviation Reports (SPECIs) • Terminal Area Forecasts (TAFs) and their amendments • NEXRAD (regional and CONUS) precipitation maps • Notice to Airmen (NOTAM) Distant and Flight Data Center • Airmen’s Meteorological Conditions (AIRMET) • Significant Meteorological...

quiz.prompt: What does `BKN020` report?
quiz.explanation: Cloud heights in METARs are hundreds of feet AGL: 020 means 2,000 feet.
options: Broken cloud layer at 2,000 ft AGL
Visibility 20 miles
Wind 020 degrees
Barometer 20.00 inHg

quiz.prompt: Match aviation weather product and function.
quiz.explanation: Each product contributes a different time, place, or hazard perspective.

pairs.right: Observed surface weather
Forecast terminal weather

quiz.prompt: What is an abbreviated briefing best used for?
quiz.explanation: An abbreviated briefing updates or supplements information already obtained.
options: Updating selected information after an earlier briefing
Replacing all preflight planning
Obtaining aircraft maintenance approval
Filing a registration change

quiz.prompt: Using the FAA weather-code table, what descriptor does `SH` represent in a METAR?
options: Showers
Shallow
Snow hail
Sea haze

image.alt: FAA PHAK METAR and PIREP coding references, handbook page 13-7
image.caption: METAR and PIREP coding references

quiz.explanation: In METAR coding, SH is the descriptor for showers.

sections.title: Airport Data, Markings, Signs, and Lighting
sections.summary: Apply airport publications and diagrams, runway and taxiway markings, airport signs and holding positions, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Read the airport surface
lessons.concept: Runway and taxiway markings, signs, and lights form a consistent language that separates movement areas and protects runway entrances.
lessons.explanation: A red sign with white characters is mandatory instruction; a yellow sign with black characters provides direction; a black sign with yellow characters identifies the current location. Runway holding-position markings have solid and dashed yellow lines.
lessons.workedExample: Approaching a hold-short line from the solid-line side, the pilot stops before the marking unless cleared to cross or enter the runway. Reading back a runway crossing or hold-short instruction reduces ambiguity.

practice.prompt: Match sign appearance to meaning.
practice.explanation: Color and layout distinguish mandatory, direction, and location signs.

pairs.right: Mandatory instruction
Direction
Location

lessons.title: Airport publications and diagrams
lessons.concept: publications receives immediate dissemination by the NOTAM system.
lessons.explanation: The NOTAM information could affect your decision to make the flight. It includes such information as taxiway and runway closures, construction, communications, changes in status of navigational aids, and other information essential to planned en route, terminal, or landing operations. Exercise good judgment and common sense by carefully regarding the information readily available in NOTAMs.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how airport publications and diagrams affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about airport publications and diagrams: "publications receives immediate dissemination ___."
options: by the NOTAM system
yellow background indicating a destination at the airport
lights form a consistent language that separates movement areas and protects runway entrances
overheat switch is provided on all combustion heaters
practice.explanation: The cited page states: publications receives immediate dissemination by the NOTAM system.

lessons.title: Runway and taxiway markings
lessons.concept: Runway holding position markings consist of four yellow lines, two solid and two dashed, that are painted on the surface and extend across the width of the taxiway to indicate where the aircraft should stop when approaching a runway.
lessons.explanation: Runway Holding Position Marking Noncompliance with a runway holding position marking may result in the FAA filing a Pilot Deviation against you. These markings are painted across the entire taxiway pavement, are in alignment, and are collocated with the holding position sign as described above. Runway holding position sign at a location other than the takeoff end of Runway 18-36 with collocated Taxiway Alpha location sign.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how runway and taxiway markings affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is briefing runway and taxiway markings. Which source-listed item belongs in that briefing?
options: lights form a consistent language that separates movement areas and protects runway entrances
yellow background indicating a destination at the airport
four yellow lines
steady flight occurs when forces are balanced along the flight path

lessons.title: Airport signs and holding positions
lessons.concept: Destination Signs Destination signs have black characters on a yellow background indicating a destination at the airport.
lessons.explanation: Enhanced Taxiway Centerline Markings At most towered airports, the enhanced taxiway centerline marking is used to warn you of an upcoming runway. It consists of yellow dashed lines on either side of the normal solid taxiway centerline and the dashes extend up to 150 feet prior to a runway holding position marking. [Figure 14-21A and B] They are used to aid you in maintaining awareness during surface movement to reduce runway incursions.

sections.title: Traffic Patterns, Communications, and Wake
sections.summary: Apply standard traffic pattern geometry, towered and nontowered communication, right-of-way and collision avoidance, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Fly a predictable traffic pattern
lessons.concept: Standard traffic patterns organize arrivals and departures through upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, and final legs, normally using left turns unless indicated otherwise.
lessons.explanation: Entries should integrate without disrupting established traffic. Wind indicators, segmented circles, markings, lighting, radio calls, and visual scanning work together; a radio does not replace seeing and avoiding.
lessons.workedExample: Arriving at a nontowered field, the pilot listens, observes the wind and traffic, announces position, and enters on the recommended 45-degree entry to downwind when practical.

practice.prompt: What is the standard traffic-pattern turn direction unless markings or published information indicate otherwise?
practice.explanation: Standard traffic patterns use left turns unless right traffic is designated.
options: Left
Right
Alternating
Toward the terminal

lessons.title: Towered and nontowered communication
lessons.concept: Control of Airport Lighting Airport lighting is controlled by ATC at towered airports.
lessons.explanation: Land and hold short lights consist of a row of pulsing white lights installed across the runway at the hold short point. Where installed, the lights are on anytime LAHSO is in effect. These lights are off when LAHSO is not in effect.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how towered and nontowered communication affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: controlled by ATC at towered airports
normally using left turns unless indicated otherwise

airspace to have a control tower (Lake City
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Control of Airport Lighting Airport lighting is controlled by ATC at towered airports.

lessons.title: Right-of-way and collision avoidance
lessons.concept: If departing the traffic pattern, continue straight out, or exit with a 45° turn (to the left when in a left-hand traffic pattern; to the right when in a right-hand traffic pattern) beyond the departure end of the runway, after reaching pattern altitude.
lessons.explanation: LEGEND En try Application of traffic pattern indicators Recommended standard left-hand traffic pattern (depicted) (standard right-hand traffic pattern would be mirror image) 1 Downwind 2 Base Crosswind Segmented circle 3 Final Departure 4 e ur rt a ep D 5 6 6 Departure RUNWAY Figure 14-39. Traffic pattern operations—single runway. If remaining in the traffic pattern, commence turn to crosswind leg beyond the departure end of the runway within 300 feet of pattern altitude.

lessons.title: Wake turbulence recognition and avoidance
lessons.concept: Wake Turbulence All aircraft generate wake turbulence during flight.
lessons.explanation: The purpose of this service is to provide separation between all participating VFR aircraft and all IFR aircraft operating within the TRSA. Class C service provides approved separation between IFR and VFR aircraft and sequencing of VFR aircraft to the primary airport. Class B service provides approved separation of aircraft based on IFR, VFR, and/or weight and sequencing of VFR arrivals to the primary airport(s).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wake turbulence recognition and avoidance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: Support coordination but do not replace see-and-avoid

sections.title: Runway Safety and Surface Risk
sections.summary: Apply runway incursion prevention, hot spots and runway confusion, taxi clearances and progressive instructions, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Prevent incursions and wake encounters
lessons.concept: Sterile-cockpit discipline, progressive taxi instructions, diagram use, position awareness, and explicit clearances reduce runway-incursion risk.
lessons.explanation: Wake vortices sink and move outward behind a generating aircraft. They are strongest behind heavy, clean, slow airplanes. Avoidance depends on the other aircraft's rotation or touchdown point and wind drift, not only elapsed time.
lessons.workedExample: Departing behind a larger airplane, a pilot rotates before its rotation point and stays above its path, adjusting for crosswind and accepting delay when safe spacing is uncertain.

practice.prompt: Where should a light airplane normally touch down behind a larger landing airplane?
practice.explanation: Touching down beyond the generator's touchdown point helps avoid sinking vortices.
options: Beyond the larger airplane's touchdown point
Before its touchdown point
Exactly on its touchdown point
On the adjacent taxiway

lessons.title: Runway incursion prevention
lessons.concept: In serious instances, any ground deviation (PD or VPD) can result in a runway incursion.
lessons.explanation: Many strategies and tactics pilots use to avoid airborne deviations also work on the ground. Pilots should also remain vigilant about vehicle/pedestrian deviations (V/PDs). A vehicle or pedestrian deviation includes pedestrians, vehicles or other objects interfering with aircraft operations by entering or moving on the runway movement area without authorization from air traffic control.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how runway incursion prevention affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about runway incursion prevention: "___, any ground deviation (PD or VPD) can result in a runway incursion."
options: landing on a taxiway or wrong runway
An OI caused a 737 to land on a runway occupied by a twin-engine turboprop.
In serious instances
lights form a consistent language that separates movement areas and protects runway entrances
practice.explanation: The cited page states: In serious instances, any ground deviation (PD or VPD) can result in a runway incursion.

lessons.title: Hot spots and runway confusion
lessons.concept: [Figure 14-48] Runway Confusion Runway confusion is a subset of runway incursions and often results in you unintentionally taking off or landing on a taxiway or wrong runway.
lessons.explanation: Ensure you have a current airport diagram, remain “heads-up” with eyes outside, and devote your entire attention to surface navigation per ATC clearance. All checklists should be completed while the aircraft is stopped. There is no place for non-essential chatter or other activities while maintaining vigilance during taxi.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how hot spots and runway confusion affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about hot spots and runway confusion: "Runway Confusion Runway confusion is a subset of runway incursions and often results in you unintentionally taking off ___."
options: In serious instances
outward behind a generating aircraft
that are painted on the surface
or landing on a taxiway or wrong runway
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Runway Confusion Runway confusion is a subset of runway incursions and often results in you unintentionally taking off or landing on a taxiway or wrong runway.

lessons.title: Taxi clearances and progressive instructions
lessons.concept: If, at any time, there is uncertainty about any ATC instructions or clearances, ask ATC to “say again” or ask for progressive taxi instructions.
lessons.explanation: You should be especially vigilant if another aircraft has a similar sounding call sign so there is no mistake about who ATC is contacting or to whom they are giving instructions and clearances. Read back your complete ATC clearance with your aircraft call sign. This gives ATC the opportunity to clarify any misunderstandings and ensure that instructions were given to the correct aircraft.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how taxi clearances and progressive instructions affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "If, at any time, ___, ask ATC to “say again” or ask for progressive taxi instructions."
options: landing on a taxiway or wrong runway
In serious instances
there is uncertainty about any ATC instructions or clearances
to arrive at each checkpoint and the destination
practice.explanation: The cited page states: If, at any time, there is uncertainty about any ATC instructions or clearances, ask ATC to “say again” or ask for progressive taxi instructions.

lessons.title: Land-and-hold-short operations
lessons.concept: An OI caused a 737 to land on a runway occupied by a twin-engine turboprop.
lessons.explanation: entering the runway, remember to scan the full length of the runway and its approach end for other aircraft. There have been collisions and incidents involving aircraft instructed to “line up and wait” while ATC waits for the necessary conditions to issue a takeoff clearance. The turboprop was holding in position awaiting takeoff clearance.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how land-and-hold-short operations affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

the effect of increasing the wing’s AOA is the same
practice.explanation: The cited page states: An OI caused a 737 to land on a runway occupied by a twin-engine turboprop.

sections.title: Airspace
sections.summary: Apply controlled and uncontrolled airspace, entry, equipment, and communication rules, vfr weather minimums, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Controlled classes reflect traffic and service
lessons.concept: Classes A through E are controlled airspace with different dimensions, entry rules, equipment, communication, and VFR weather minima; Class G is uncontrolled.
lessons.explanation: Class B surrounds the busiest airports and requires an explicit clearance. Class C requires two-way communications before entry. Class D normally surrounds a towered airport and also requires two-way communications. Class E often begins at 700 or 1,200 feet AGL but can start at the surface.
lessons.workedExample: A pilot calling a Class C tower has established two-way communication when ATC responds with the aircraft call sign. A generic 'aircraft calling, standby' does not establish it.

practice.prompt: Match class to a defining feature.
practice.explanation: Each class has distinct operating requirements; charted boundaries determine where they apply.

pairs.right: IFR operations only
Explicit ATC clearance before entry
Two-way communication before entry
Uncontrolled airspace

lessons.title: Weather minima preserve separation
lessons.concept: Basic VFR weather minima vary by airspace, altitude, and day or night to give pilots time to see and avoid traffic emerging from cloud.
lessons.explanation: The common Class E below 10,000 feet MSL minimum is three statute miles visibility and cloud clearance of 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above, and 2,000 feet horizontal. Rules differ in other airspace and at other altitudes.
lessons.workedExample: Cruising in Class E below 10,000 feet MSL with three miles visibility is not enough if the aircraft cannot also maintain the required cloud clearance.

practice.prompt: What is the common VFR visibility minimum in Class E below 10,000 ft MSL?
practice.explanation: Class E below 10,000 feet MSL generally requires three statute miles visibility plus 500-below, 1,000-above, 2,000-horizontal cloud clearance.
options: 1 statute mile
3 statute miles
5 statute miles
No minimum

lessons.title: Special-use airspace changes the plan
lessons.concept: Prohibited, restricted, warning, military operations, alert, and controlled firing areas differ in legal status and hazard.
lessons.explanation: Prohibited areas bar flight absent permission. Restricted areas contain unusual, often invisible hazards and require authorization when active. MOAs separate or alert IFR traffic from military activity, but VFR pilots should obtain activity status and use caution.
lessons.workedExample: A direct course crosses an active restricted area. The pilot obtains authorization from the controlling agency or routes around; simply remaining VFR does not permit entry.

practice.prompt: May a VFR pilot enter an active restricted area without authorization?
practice.explanation: Active restricted airspace requires authorization from the controlling agency.
options: Yes, if visibility exceeds five miles
No
Yes, below 3,000 ft AGL
Only with landing lights on

lessons.title: Controlled and uncontrolled airspace
lessons.concept: Uncontrolled Airspace It is possible for some airports within Class G airspace to have a control tower (Lake City, FL, for example).
lessons.explanation: However, if the aircraft radio fails in flight, the PIC may operate that aircraft and land if weather conditions are at or above basic VFR weather minimums, visual contact with the tower is maintained, and a clearance to land is received. If the aircraft radio fails in flight under IFR, the pilot should continue the flight by the route assigned in the last ATC clearance received; or, if being radar vectored, by the direct route from the point of radio failure to the fix, route, or airway specified in the vector clearance. In the absence of an assigned route, the pilot should continue by the route that ATC advised may be expected in a further clearance; or, if a route had not been advised, by the route filed in the flight plan.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how controlled and uncontrolled airspace affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about controlled and uncontrolled airspace: "Uncontrolled Airspace It is possible for some airports within Class G ___, FL, for example)."
options: Classes A through E are controlled airspace with different dimensions
airspace to have a control tower (Lake City
controlled firing areas differ in legal status and hazard
controlled by ATC at towered airports
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Uncontrolled Airspace It is possible for some airports within Class G airspace to have a control tower (Lake City, FL, for example).

quiz.prompt: What is required before entering Class B airspace?
quiz.explanation: Class B entry requires a specific ATC clearance.
options: An explicit ATC clearance
Only monitoring the frequency
A flight plan in all cases
No communication

quiz.prompt: Match airspace with its treatment.
quiz.explanation: The charted type indicates both legal restriction and operational hazard.

pairs.right: Flight barred absent permission
Authorization required when active
Military training activity; use caution and obtain status
High training or unusual aerial activity

quiz.prompt: In Class E below 10,000 ft MSL, what is the usual horizontal cloud clearance for VFR?
quiz.explanation: The common requirement is 500 below, 1,000 above, and 2,000 feet horizontal.
options: 500 ft
One statute mile

options: Class A
Class B
Class D
Class G

quiz.explanation: Class A generally extends from 18,000 feet MSL through flight level 600 over the contiguous United States and Alaska, including specified offshore airspace.

sections.title: Charts, Direction, Time, and Wind
sections.summary: Apply sectional chart information and currency, latitude, longitude, and direction, true, magnetic, and compass courses, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Charts turn geography into a route
lessons.concept: Sectional charts depict airports, airspace, terrain, obstructions, navigation aids, frequencies, and other information needed for visual navigation.
lessons.explanation: Latitude and longitude locate points; true course is measured from true north. Current chart editions and legends matter because airspace, frequencies, obstacles, and airport data change.
lessons.workedExample: A direct line between airports may cross high terrain or restricted airspace. The pilot uses the chart to select checkpoints, safe altitudes, alternates, and a route that preserves options.

practice.prompt: Why must a pilot use a current sectional chart?
practice.explanation: Navigation and airspace information is revised; current data is essential to planning.
options: Operational data and airspace can change
Old charts show no latitude
Only current charts have colors
A chart is an ATC clearance

lessons.title: Sectional chart information and currency
lessons.concept: The chart provides information that allows pilots to track their position and provides available information that enhances safety.
lessons.explanation: Aeronautical Charts An aeronautical chart is the road map for a pilot flying under VFR. The three aeronautical charts used by VFR pilots are: • Sectional • VFR Terminal Area • World Aeronautical A free catalog listing aeronautical charts and related publications including prices and instructions for ordering is available at the Aeronautical Navigation Products website: www.aeronav.faa.gov. Sectional Charts Sectional charts are the most common charts used by pilots today.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how sectional chart information and currency affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for sectional chart information and currency. Which phrase completes "The chart provides information that allows pilots to track their ___."?
options: other information needed for visual navigation
Because courses are measured in reference to geographical meridians that point toward TN
position and provides available information that enhances safety
pilot input determine reliability
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The chart provides information that allows pilots to track their position and provides available information that enhances safety.

lessons.title: Latitude, longitude, and direction
lessons.concept: 36 33 N NN E W N 6 9 ES W S E S W W E E S S E S SS W 24 S 2 1 1 2 27 W N E W N N W E N W N 3 N 3 0 The north magnetic pole is located close to 71° N latitude, 96° W longitude and is about 1,300 miles from the geographic or true north pole, as indicated in Figure 16-8.
lessons.explanation: If the Earth were uniformly magnetized, the compass needle would point toward the magnetic pole, in which case the variation between TN (as shown by the geographical meridians) and MN (as shown by the magnetic meridians) could be measured at any intersection of the meridians. The true heading (TH) is the direction in which the nose of the aircraft points during a flight when measured in degrees clockwise from TN. Usually, it is necessary to head the aircraft in a direction slightly different from the TC to offset the effect of wind.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how latitude, longitude, and direction affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about latitude, longitude, and direction: "The north magnetic pole is located close to 71° N latitude, 96° W longitude and is about 1,___, as indicated in Figure 16-8."
options: Because courses are measured in reference to geographical meridians that point toward TN
position and provides available information that enhances safety
the triangle illustrated in Figure 16-19 is not drawn
300 miles from the geographic or true north pole
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The north magnetic pole is located close to 71° N latitude, 96° W longitude and is about 1,300 miles from the geographic or true north pole, as indicated in Figure 16-8.

lessons.title: True, magnetic, and compass courses
lessons.concept: Because courses are measured in reference to geographical meridians that point toward TN, and these courses are maintained by reference to the compass that points along a magnetic meridian in the general direction of MN, the true direction must be converted into magnetic direction for the purpose of flight.
lessons.explanation: Note the agonic line where magnetic variation is zero. Zero degree variation exists on the agonic line where MN and TN coincide. This line runs roughly west of the Great Lakes, south through Wisconsin, Illinois, western Tennessee, and along the border of Mississippi and Alabama.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how true, magnetic, and compass courses affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on true, magnetic, and compass courses: "___, and these courses are maintained by reference to the compass that points along a magnetic meridian in the general direction of MN, the true direction must be converted into magnetic direction for the purpose of flight."
options: 300 miles from the geographic or true north pole
position and provides available information that enhances safety
while the magnetic compass aligns with Earth's field and has turning and acceleration errors
Because courses are measured in reference to geographical meridians that point toward TN
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Because courses are measured in reference to geographical meridians that point toward TN, and these courses are maintained by reference to the compass that points along a magnetic meridian in the general direction of MN, the true direction must be converted into magnetic direction for the purpose of flight.

quiz.prompt: A 90 NM leg at 120 knots groundspeed takes how many minutes?
quiz.explanation: 90 ÷ 120 = 0.75 hour = 45 minutes.

quiz.prompt: Match navigation quantity.
quiz.explanation: These quantities connect the charted route to aircraft motion and time.

pairs.right: Direction of route measured from true north
Heading adjustment for crosswind
Speed over Earth's surface
Predicted arrival time

quiz.prompt: A flight leg takes 2 hours at 8 gallons per hour. How much cruise fuel is required before reserves and other phases?
quiz.explanation: Cruise fuel is time multiplied by burn rate: 2 × 8 = 16 gallons.

quiz.prompt: On the FAA sectional example, what should a pilot consult to decode an unfamiliar chart symbol?
options: The current chart legend
The magnetic compass deviation card
The aircraft registration
The ELT battery label

image.caption: Sectional chart and legend

quiz.explanation: The sectional legend is the authoritative key for chart symbols, colors, and aeronautical information.

sections.title: Pilotage, Dead Reckoning, and Flight Planning
sections.summary: Apply pilotage and checkpoint selection, dead-reckoning workflow, wind triangle and groundspeed, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Dead reckoning predicts time and fuel
lessons.concept: Dead reckoning combines course, wind correction, heading, true airspeed, groundspeed, time, and fuel burn, then checks predictions against visual checkpoints.
lessons.explanation: Apply variation to convert between true and magnetic direction and compass deviation for compass heading. Wind correction changes heading and groundspeed; it does not move the destination on the chart.
lessons.workedExample: A 120-NM leg at 120 knots groundspeed takes one hour. At 9 gallons per hour, planned cruise fuel is 9 gallons before adding start, taxi, climb, reserve, and contingency fuel.

practice.prompt: A 120 NM leg is flown at 120 knots groundspeed. How many minutes will the leg take?
practice.explanation: Time = distance ÷ groundspeed = 120 ÷ 120 = 1 hour, or 60 minutes.

lessons.title: Pilotage and checkpoint selection
lessons.concept: The predicted heading takes the aircraft along the intended path and the GS establishes the time to arrive at each checkpoint and the destination.
lessons.explanation: A plotter (A), the computational and wind side of a mechanical flight computer (E6B) (B), and an electronic flight computer (C). Dead Reckoning Dead reckoning is navigation solely by means of computations based on time, airspeed, distance, and direction. The products derived from these variables, when adjusted by wind speed and velocity, are heading and GS.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pilotage and checkpoint selection affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.explanation: Now, on a plain sheet of paper draw a vertical line representing north to south. (The various steps are shown in Figure 16-21.) Step 1 Place the protractor with the base resting on the vertical line and the curved edge facing east. At the center point of the base, make a dot labeled “E” (point of departure) and at the curved edge, make a dot at 90° (indicating the direction of the true course) and another at 45° (indicating wind direction).
