sections.title: Radio and Satellite Navigation
sections.summary: Apply vor orientation and intercepts, dme and station passage, adf and relative bearing, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Electronic navigation needs monitoring
lessons.concept: VOR, distance information, and GPS can improve position awareness, but database currency, signal geometry, integrity, settings, and pilot input determine reliability.
lessons.explanation: GPS direct-to guidance can lead through airspace, terrain, or weather unless the route is evaluated. VOR accuracy checks and receiver indications matter. Pilotage and dead reckoning provide independent cross-checks and failure resilience.
lessons.workedExample: When the moving map shows a position that conflicts with two unmistakable landmarks, the pilot cross-checks source selection, database, receiver status, and heading rather than following the magenta line automatically.

practice.prompt: What is the safest use of GPS direct-to guidance?
practice.explanation: Electronic guidance supplies a course, not a complete risk assessment or clearance.
options: Evaluate the resulting route for terrain, airspace, weather, and fuel
Assume it guarantees obstacle clearance
Ignore database currency
Stop monitoring position

lessons.title: VOR orientation and intercepts
lessons.concept: These waypoints allow a straight course to be flown between almost any origin and destination, without regard to the orientation of VORTACs or the existence of airways.
lessons.explanation: In its simplest form, VOR/DME RNAV allows the pilot to electronically move VORTACs around to more convenient locations. Once electronically relocated, they are referred to as waypoints. These waypoints are described as a combination of a selected radial and distance within the service volume of the VORTAC to be used.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how vor orientation and intercepts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on vor orientation and intercepts: "These waypoints allow a straight course to be ___, without regard to the orientation of VORTACs or the existence of airways." What belongs in the blank?
options: a correction for a left crosswind would result in a magnetic heading of 330°
pilot input determine reliability
flown between almost any origin and destination
to arrive at each checkpoint and the destination
practice.explanation: The cited page states: These waypoints allow a straight course to be flown between almost any origin and destination, without regard to the orientation of VORTACs or the existence of airways.

lessons.title: DME and station passage
lessons.concept: When the RMI bearing pointer is moving rapidly or when several corrections are required to place the pointer on the wingtip position, the aircraft is at station passage.
lessons.explanation: in seconds and a 10° bearing change is made, the time from the station, in minutes, is determined by counting off one decimal point. Thus, if 75 seconds are required to fly a 10° bearing change, the aircraft is 7.5 minutes from the station. The distance from the station is computed by multiplying TAS or GS (in miles per minute) by the previously determined time in minutes.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how dme and station passage affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

to place the pointer on the wingtip position
practice.explanation: The cited page states: When the RMI bearing pointer is moving rapidly or when several corrections are required to place the pointer on the wingtip position, the aircraft is at station passage.

lessons.title: ADF and relative bearing
lessons.concept: For instance, if the magnetic bearing to the station is 340°, a correction for a left crosswind would result in a magnetic heading of 330°, and the ADF needle would indicate 10° to the right or a relative bearing of 010°.
lessons.explanation: [Figure 16-37] Figure 16-38 illustrates terms that are used with the ADF and should be understood by the pilot. 6 3 Tracking to the station requires correcting for wind drift and results in maintaining flight along a straight track or bearing to the station. When the wind drift correction is established, the ADF needle indicates the amount of correction to the right or left.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how adf and relative bearing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: to place the pointer on the wingtip position

practice.explanation: The cited page states: For instance, if the magnetic bearing to the station is 340°, a correction for a left crosswind would result in a magnetic heading of 330°, and the ADF needle would indicate 10° to the right or a relative bearing of 010°.

lessons.title: GPS integrity and database limitations
lessons.concept: If expired, update the database using the current revision.
lessons.explanation: Tips for Using GPS for VFR Operations Always check to see if the unit has RAIM capability. If no RAIM capability exists, be suspicious of a GPS displayed position when any disagreement exists with the position derived from other radio navigation systems, pilotage, or dead reckoning. Check the currency of the database, if any.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how gps integrity and database limitations affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: If expired, update the database using the current revision.

sections.title: Aeromedical Factors
sections.summary: Apply hypoxia types and oxygen use, pressure-change and trapped-gas effects, spatial disorientation, and their operational risk controls.

lessons.title: Protect oxygen and pressure physiology
lessons.concept: Hypoxia deprives body tissues of adequate oxygen and may impair judgment before the pilot recognizes symptoms.
lessons.explanation: Hypoxic, hypemic, stagnant, and histotoxic hypoxia have different causes. Altitude, smoking, carbon monoxide, poor circulation, alcohol, and some medications can reduce oxygen delivery or use. Supplemental oxygen and descent are key controls when altitude-related symptoms appear.
lessons.workedExample: A pilot develops headache, euphoria, and poor coordination at altitude. Because self-diagnosis is unreliable, the pilot uses oxygen, descends, and evaluates occupants rather than waiting for certainty.

practice.prompt: Match hypoxia type to cause.
practice.explanation: The four categories describe failures of pressure, carrying capacity, circulation, or cellular use.

pairs.right: Insufficient oxygen pressure
Blood cannot carry enough oxygen
Inadequate circulation
Tissues cannot use delivered oxygen

lessons.title: Trust instruments over false sensations
lessons.concept: The vestibular system can create powerful false sensations during acceleration or prolonged turns, especially without a visible horizon.
lessons.explanation: The leans, Coriolis illusion, graveyard spiral, somatogravic illusion, and inversion illusion can provoke dangerous control inputs. Avoid abrupt head movements, maintain an instrument cross-check, and recover by reference to reliable flight instruments.
lessons.workedExample: After a prolonged unnoticed bank, leveling the wings can feel like a bank in the opposite direction. The pilot accepts the instrument indication and keeps the wings level instead of re-entering the turn to feel upright.

practice.prompt: During suspected spatial disorientation in instrument conditions, what should the pilot trust?
practice.explanation: Body sensations can be false; a disciplined instrument cross-check provides the reliable attitude reference.
options: Reliable flight instruments
The strongest body sensation
Passenger balance
Engine sound alone

lessons.title: Manage vision, stress, fatigue, and substances
lessons.concept: Night vision needs time and protection, while fatigue, dehydration, stress, alcohol, medications, and carbon monoxide can degrade performance even when a pilot is technically legal.
lessons.explanation: Scanning with slight off-center viewing helps at night because rods are concentrated away from the fovea. The IMSAFE checklist screens illness, medication, stress, alcohol, fatigue, and emotion/eating. Minimum alcohol rules are not a guarantee of fitness.
lessons.workedExample: A pilot eight hours past a drink but still hung over does not fly. Legal elapsed time does not remove dehydration, sleep loss, or impaired judgment.

practice.prompt: Match IMSAFE item to check.
practice.explanation: IMSAFE is a personal fitness screen, not merely a regulatory checklist.

pairs.right: Symptoms that could impair flight
Side effects or contraindications
Mental workload outside the cockpit
Sleep and alertness deficit

lessons.title: Hypoxia types and oxygen use
lessons.concept: This reduces the blood’s capacity to combine with oxygen, so less oxygen is carried in the blood.
lessons.explanation: The effects of alcohol are long lasting and the residual effects of alcohol can also impair visual scanning efficiency. Tobacco Of all the self-imposed stressors, cigarette smoking most decreases visual sensitivity at night. Smoking significantly increases the amount of carbon monoxide carried by the hemoglobin in red blood cells.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how hypoxia types and oxygen use affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on hypoxia types and oxygen use: "___, so less oxygen is carried in the blood."
options: may impair judgment before the pilot recognizes symptoms
alternate sensations of “fullness” and “clearing.” During descent
This reduces the blood’s capacity to combine with oxygen
anti-ice systems have distinct failure indications and backup paths
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This reduces the blood’s capacity to combine with oxygen, so less oxygen is carried in the blood.

lessons.title: Pressure-change and trapped-gas effects
lessons.concept: Pilots become aware of this pressure change when they experience alternate sensations of “fullness” and “clearing.” During descent, the reverse happens.
lessons.explanation: These tubes are usually closed but open during chewing, yawning, or swallowing to equalize pressure. Even a slight difference between external pressure and middle ear pressure can cause discomfort. [Figure 17-2] During a climb, middle ear air pressure may exceed the pressure of the air in the external ear canal causing the eardrum to bulge outward.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pressure-change and trapped-gas effects affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

Reduced tolerance to G-forces Inner-ear disturbance and spatial
the downward change in momentum of the airflow
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Pilots become aware of this pressure change when they experience alternate sensations of “fullness” and “clearing.” During descent, the reverse happens.

lessons.title: Spatial disorientation
lessons.concept: Substance Generic Or Brand Name Treatment for Possible Side Effects Alcohol Beer Liquor Wine N/A Impaired judgment and perception Impaired coordination and motor control Reduced reaction time Impaired sensory perception Reduced intellectual functions Reduced tolerance to G-forces Inner-ear disturbance and spatial...
lessons.explanation: These drugs lower blood pressure, reduce mental processing, and slow motor and reaction responses. There are several types of drugs that can cause a depressing effect on the body, including tranquilizers, motion sickness medication, some types of stomach medication, decongestants, and antihistamines. The most common depressant is alcohol.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how spatial disorientation affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

quiz.prompt: Why is hypoxia especially dangerous to pilot decision-making?
quiz.explanation: Early judgment and self-awareness impairment can prevent a pilot from recognizing the need to act.
options: It may impair judgment before symptoms are recognized
It always causes immediate pain
It improves night vision
It only affects passengers

quiz.prompt: Match hypoxia category.
quiz.explanation: Different mechanisms can produce similar performance impairment.

pairs.right: Low available oxygen pressure
Reduced oxygen-carrying capacity
Poor circulation
Cells cannot use oxygen

quiz.prompt: What is the recommended response to a vestibular illusion in cloud?
quiz.explanation: Reliable instruments, not vestibular sensation, should guide control.
options: Rely on a disciplined instrument cross-check
Turn toward the sensation
Close the eyes and continue
Descend without checking attitude

quiz.prompt: The graveyard-spiral figure shows altitude loss during a prolonged turn. What begins a safe recovery?
options: Recognize the bank on instruments and level the wings smoothly
Pull back harder while remaining banked
Trust the sensation of level flight
Increase the bank to stop descent

Static stability describes the initial tendency after displacement; dynamic stability describes motion over time as the airplane returns, diverges, or oscillates.
The contribution of the wing to static directional stability is usually small.

modules.title: Airplane Flying Handbook
modules.description: Airplane flight training, maneuvers, takeoffs, landings, transitions, and emergencies.

sections.title: Introduction to Flight Training
sections.summary: Apply training objectives and airmanship, faa regulations and certification framework, flight instructor and learner responsibilities, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Training objectives and airmanship
lessons.concept: The overall purpose of primary and intermediate flight training, as outlined in this handbook, is the acquisition and honing of basic airmanship skills.
lessons.explanation: [Figure 1-1] Airmanship is a broad term that includes a sound knowledge of and experience with the principles of flight; the knowledge, experience, and ability to operate an aircraft with competence and precision both on the ground and in the air; and the application of sound judgment that results in optimal operational safety and efficiency. [Figure 1-2] Learning to fly an aircraft has often been compared to learning to drive an automobile. Since aircraft operate in a threedimensional environment, they require a depth of knowledge and type of motor skill development that is more sensitive to this situation, such as: ⦁ Coordination–the ability to use the hands and feet together subconsciously and in the proper relationship to produce desired results in the airplane.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how training objectives and airmanship affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "The overall purpose of primary and intermediate flight training, as outlined in this handbook, ___."
options: is the acquisition and honing of basic airmanship skills
procedures for the exchange of flight controls
outlining operating rules
steering and directional control are its primary functions
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The overall purpose of primary and intermediate flight training, as outlined in this handbook, is the acquisition and honing of basic airmanship skills.

lessons.title: FAA regulations and certification framework
lessons.concept: Standards are established for the certification of airmen and aircraft, as well as outlining operating rules.
lessons.explanation: Also note that the goal of flight training is a safe and competent pilot; passing required practical tests for pilot certification is only incidental to this goal. Role of the FAA The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) is empowered by the U.S. Congress to promote aviation safety by prescribing safety standards for civil aviation.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how faa regulations and certification framework affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Standards are established for the certification of airmen and aircraft, as well as outlining operating rules.

lessons.title: Flight instructor and learner responsibilities
lessons.concept: A good flight instructor has a thorough understanding of the learning process, knowledge of the fundamentals of instruction, and the ability to communicate effectively with the learner.
lessons.explanation: The flight instructor is responsible for teaching and training. A flight instructor normally meets broad flying experience requirements, passes rigid knowledge and practical tests, and demonstrates the ability to apply recommended teaching techniques before being certificated. A pilot training program is dependent on the quality of the ground and flight instruction given.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how flight instructor and learner responsibilities affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on flight instructor and learner responsibilities: "___, knowledge of the fundamentals of instruction, and the ability to communicate effectively with the learner."
options: is the acquisition and honing of basic airmanship skills
A good flight instructor has a thorough understanding of the learning process
procedures for the exchange of flight controls
the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
practice.explanation: The cited page states: A good flight instructor has a thorough understanding of the learning process, knowledge of the fundamentals of instruction, and the ability to communicate effectively with the learner.

lessons.title: Positive exchange of flight controls
lessons.concept: Prior to any flight, a briefing should be conducted that includes the procedures for the exchange of flight controls.
lessons.explanation: During flight training, there should be a clear understanding between the learner and flight instructor of who has control of the aircraft. The following three-step process for the exchange of flight controls is highly recommended. When a flight instructor wishes the learner to take control of the aircraft, he or she should say to the learner, “You have the flight controls.” The learner should acknowledge immediately by saying, “I have the flight controls.” The flight instructor should then confirm by again saying, “You have the flight controls.” Part of the procedure should be a visual check to ensure that the other person actually has the flight controls.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how positive exchange of flight controls affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: clearing procedures

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Prior to any flight, a briefing should be conducted that includes the procedures for the exchange of flight controls.

lessons.title: Flight deck management and collision avoidance
lessons.concept: These include, but are not limited to, collision avoidance procedures including proper scanning techniques and clearing procedures, runway incursion avoidance, stall awareness, positive transfer of controls, and flight deck workload management.
lessons.explanation: Safety Considerations In the interest of safety and good habit pattern formation, there are certain basic flight safety practices and procedures that should be emphasized by the flight instructor, and adhered to by both instructor and learner, beginning with the very first dual instruction flight. Collision Avoidance All pilots should be alert to the potential for midair collision and impending loss of separation. The general operating and flight rules in 14 CFR part 91 set forth the concept of “see and avoid.” This concept requires that vigilance shall be maintained at all times by each person operating an aircraft regardless of whether the operation is conducted under IFR or VFR.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how flight deck management and collision avoidance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.explanation: Situational awareness begins during preparation and only ends when the airplane is safely and securely returned to its tie-down or hangar, or if a decision is made not to go. This chapter covers the essential elements for the regulatory basis of flight including: 1. An airplane’s airworthiness requirements, 2.

lessons.title: Cockpit checks and passenger briefing
lessons.concept: ⦁ Takeoff Briefing–made out loud by the pilot even when no other person is there to listen.
lessons.explanation: Verify that the directional gyro and the magnetic compass are in agreement. If the directional gyro has a heading bug, it may be set to the runway heading that is in use or as assigned by air traffic control (ATC). ⦁ Avionics–set with the appropriate frequencies, initial navigation sources and courses, autopilot preselects, transponder codes, and other settings and configurations based on the airplane’s equipment and flight requirements.

lessons.title: Engine starting and hand propping hazards
lessons.concept: The consequences of the hazards associated with hand propping are serious to fatal.
lessons.explanation: The pilot should be attentive for sounds, vibrations, smells, or smoke that are not consistent with normal after-start operational experience. Any concerns should lead to a shutdown and further investigation. Hand Propping The procedures for hand propping should always be in accordance with the AFM/POH and performed only by persons who are competent with hand propping procedures.

lessons.title: Taxiing and brake checks
lessons.concept: Taxiing checklists are sometimes specified by the AFM/POH, and the pilot should accomplish any items that are required.
lessons.explanation: If there are no specific checklist items, taxiing still provides an opportunity to verify the operation and cross-check of the flight instruments. In general, the flight instruments should indicate properly with the airspeed at or near zero (depending on taxi speed, wind speed and direction, and lower limit sensitivity); the attitude indicator should indicate pitch and roll level (depending on airplane attitude) with no flags; the altimeter should indicate the proper elevation within prescribed limits; the turn indicator should show the correct direction of turn with the ball movement toward the outside of the turn with no flags; the directional gyro should be set and crossed checked to the magnetic compass and verified accurate to the direction of taxi; and the vertical speed indicator (VSI) should read zero. These checks can be accompli

lessons.title: Wind correction during taxi
lessons.concept: Since the wind is striking the airplane from behind, these control positions reduce the tendency of the wind to get under the tail and the wing and to nose the airplane over.
lessons.explanation: Moving the aileron into the UP position reduces the effect of the wind striking that wing, thus reducing the lifting action. This control movement also causes the downwind aileron to be placed in the DOWN position, thus a small amount of lift and drag on the downwind wing, further reducing the tendency of the upwind wing to rise. [Figure 2-15] When taxiing with a quartering tailwind, the elevator should be held in the DOWN position, and the upwind aileron, DOWN.

sections.title: Basic Flight Maneuvers

lessons.title: Attitude flying and integrated controls
lessons.concept: Flight instructors face the challenge of teaching beginning pilots about attitude awareness; which requires understanding the motions of flight.
lessons.explanation: Chapter 3: Basic Flight Maneuvers Introduction Airplanes operate in an environment that is unlike an automobile. Drivers tend to drive with a fairly narrow field of view and focus primarily on forward motion. Beginning pilots tend to practice the same.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how attitude flying and integrated controls affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Straight-and-level flight
lessons.concept: Straight-and-Level Flight Straight-and-level flight is flight in which heading and altitude are maintained.
lessons.explanation: For the first several hours of instruction, flight instructors may choose to use flight instrument covers to develop a beginning pilot’s skill or to correct a pilot’s poor habit of fixating on instruments by forcing them to use outside visual references for aircraft control. The beginning pilot, not being familiar with the intricacies of flight by references to instruments, including such things as instrument lag and gyroscopic precession, will invariably make excessive attitude corrections and end up “chasing the instruments.” Airplane attitude by reference to the natural horizon, however, presents immediate and accurate indications many times larger than on any instrument. The beginning pilot should understand that anytime airplane attitude by reference to the natural horizon cannot be established or maintained, the situation has become

lessons.concept: All the factors that affect the airplane during level constant-altitude turns affect the airplane during climbing turns.
lessons.explanation: The pilot should establish and maintain an appropriate constant bank during the turn. ⦁ The pilot should maintain a constant airspeed and constant rate of turn in both right and left turns. The coordination of all flight controls is a primary factor.

lessons.concept: Common errors in the performance of climbs and climbing turns are: 1.
lessons.explanation: When turns are simultaneous with a climb, it is most effective to limit the turns to shallow bank angles. This provides for an efficient rate of climb. If a medium or steep banked turn is used, climb performance is degraded or possibly non-existent.

sections.title: Energy Management
sections.summary: Apply kinetic and potential energy, power, pitch, and flightpath, energy state awareness, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Kinetic and potential energy
lessons.concept: Viewing the Airplane as an Energy System The total mechanical energy of an airplane in flight is the sum of its potential energy from altitude and kinetic energy from airspeed.
lessons.explanation: An accurate mental model of the airplane as an energy system. The competency to effectively coordinate control inputs to achieve and maintain altitude and airspeed targets. The ability to identify, assess, and mitigate the risks associated with mismanagement of energy.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how kinetic and potential energy affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: energy from altitude and kinetic energy from airspeed
the pilot is always acting on the airplane’s energy state
airspeed targets within the airplane’s energy envelope
airspeed than the best glide speed
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Viewing the Airplane as an Energy System The total mechanical energy of an airplane in flight is the sum of its potential energy from altitude and kinetic energy from airspeed.

lessons.title: Power, pitch, and flightpath
lessons.concept: Modifying a popular adage, the principle can be restated as “pitch plus power controls energy state.” This central principle serves to guide a set of general energy control rules to achieve and maintain any desired vertical flight path and airspeed targets within the airplane’s energy envelope.
lessons.explanation: Applying the respective role of the controls to manage the airplane’s energy state leads to a set of simple “rules” for proper throttleelevator coordination to effectively control vertical flight path and airspeed. What are these basic rules of energy control? Rules of Energy Control The central principle encapsulating the role of the throttle and elevator for managing the airplane’s energy can be summed up as follows: coordinated throttle and elevator inputs control the airplane’s energy state.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how power, pitch, and flightpath affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: adjust pitch attitude and throttle setting as needed to maintain the new path-speed profile
the pilot is always acting on the airplane’s energy state

and airspeed targets within the airplane’s energy envelope
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Modifying a popular adage, the principle can be restated as “pitch plus power controls energy state.” This central principle serves to guide a set of general energy control rules to achieve and maintain any desired vertical flight path and airspeed targets within the airplane’s energy envelope.

lessons.title: Energy state awareness
lessons.concept: Upon reaching new desired energy state, adjust pitch attitude and throttle setting as needed to maintain the new path-speed profile.
lessons.explanation: As can be visualized in Figure 4-8, there are three general energy control rules for coordinating the throttle and elevator to move the airplane from one energy state to another: Rule #1: If you want to move to a new energy state that demands more total energy, then: Throttle: increase throttle setting so that thrust is greater than drag, thus increasing total energy; Elevator: adjust pitch attitude as appropriate to distribute the total energy being gained over altitude and airspeed: a. To climb at constant speed, pitch up just enough to maintain the desired speed; b. To accelerate at constant altitude, gradually pitch down just enough to maintain path.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how energy state awareness affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Managing energy errors
lessons.concept: Managing Energy Errors In addition to learning effective techniques for maintaining stabilized path-speed profiles (e.g., tracking the glideslope) and transitioning from one profile to another during flight (e.g., leveling off from a descent), pilots should develop skills for managing unwanted deviations in...
lessons.explanation: Since many inflight “energy crises” start as undetected, ignored or poorly managed path-speed deviations, pilots need the skills to recognize, correct and prevent these deviations. Although the intention is to correct altitude and airspeed deviations, the pilot is always acting on the airplane’s energy state. Thus, it is important to translate altitude-speed deviations into energy errors.

lessons.concept: Although the intention is to correct altitude and airspeed deviations, the pilot is always acting on the airplane’s energy state.
lessons.explanation: Managing Energy Errors In addition to learning effective techniques for maintaining stabilized path-speed profiles (e.g., tracking the glideslope) and transitioning from one profile to another during flight (e.g., leveling off from a descent), pilots should develop skills for managing unwanted deviations in vertical flight path and airspeed—returning the airplane to its target energy state. Since many inflight “energy crises” start as undetected, ignored or poorly managed path-speed deviations, pilots need the skills to recognize, correct and prevent these deviations. Thus, it is important to translate altitude-speed deviations into energy errors.

sections.title: Upset Prevention and Recovery
sections.summary: Apply loss-of-control risk and prevention, aerodynamic factors in an upset, stall recognition and recovery, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Loss-of-control risk and prevention
lessons.concept: Chapter 5: Maintaining Aircraft Control: Upset Prevention and Recovery Training Introduction Safe pilots prevent loss of control in flight (LOC-I), which is the leading cause of fatal general aviation accidents in the U.S.
lessons.explanation: LOC-I includes any significant deviation of an aircraft from the intended flightpath and it often results from an airplane upset. Maneuvering represents the most common phase of flight for general aviation LOC-I accidents; however, LOC-I accidents occur in all phases of flight. To prevent LOC-I accidents, it is important for pilots to recognize and maintain a heightened awareness of situations that increase the risk of loss of control.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how loss-of-control risk and prevention affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing loss-of-control risk and prevention, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "___, it is important for pilots to recognize and maintain a heightened awareness of situations that increase the risk of loss of control."
options: To prevent LOC-I accidents
UPRT Training Core Concepts Airplane upsets are by nature time-critical events
it is important to understand factors and situations that can lead to a stall
thereafter maintain straight flight at the same speed with an angle of
practice.explanation: The cited page states: To prevent LOC-I accidents, it is important for pilots to recognize and maintain a heightened awareness of situations that increase the risk of loss of control.

lessons.title: Aerodynamic factors in an upset
lessons.concept: These cues include aerodynamic buffet, loss of roll authority, or inability to arrest a descent.
lessons.explanation: The ability to separate time-critical information from distractions takes practice, experience, and knowledge of the airplane and its systems. Cross-checks are necessary not only to corroborate other information that has been presented, but also to determine if information might be missing or invalid. For example, a stall warning system may fail and therefore not warn a pilot of close proximity to a stall, so other cues need to be used to avert a stall and possible LOC-I.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how aerodynamic factors in an upset affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is briefing aerodynamic factors in an upset. Which source-listed item belongs in that briefing?
options: or inability to arrest a descent
it is important to understand factors and situations that can lead to a stall
It discussed the importance of developing proficiency in slow flight
positive transfer of controls
practice.explanation: The cited page states: These cues include aerodynamic buffet, loss of roll authority, or inability to arrest a descent.

lessons.title: Stall recognition and recovery
lessons.concept: For these reasons, it is important to understand factors and situations that can lead to a stall, and develop proficiency in stall recognition and recovery.
lessons.explanation: Specifically, a stall occurs when the AOA—the angle between the chord line of the wing and the relative wind—exceeds the wing’s critical AOA. It is possible to exceed the critical AOA at any airspeed, at any attitude, and at any power setting. Critical angle of attack and stall.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how stall recognition and recovery affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Spin awareness
lessons.concept: Spin Awareness A spin is an aggravated stall condition that may result after a stall occurs.
lessons.explanation: Mishandling of yaw control during a stall increases the likelihood of a spin entry. A spin results in the airplane following a downward corkscrew path. During a spin, the airplane rotates around its vertical axis affected by different lift and drag forces on each wing, and the airplane descends due to gravity, rolling, yawing, and pitching in a spiral path.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how spin awareness affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

condition that may result after a stall occurs
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Spin Awareness A spin is an aggravated stall condition that may result after a stall occurs.

lessons.title: Unusual attitude recognition
lessons.concept: UPRT Training Core Concepts Airplane upsets are by nature time-critical events; they can also place pilots in unusual and unfamiliar attitudes that sometimes require counterintuitive control movements.
lessons.explanation: Upsets have the potential to put a pilot into a life-threatening situation compounded by panic, diminished mental capacity, and potentially incapacitating spatial disorientation. Real-world upset situations often provide very little time to react, but exposure to such events during training can reduce surprise and mitigate confusion during an actual unexpected upset. The goal is to equip the pilot to promptly recognize an escalating threat pattern or sensory overload and quickly identify and correct an impending upset.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how unusual attitude recognition affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.explanation: Initial flight training thus provides skills to operate an airplane in a safe manner, generally within normal “expected” environments, with the addition of some instruction in upset and stall situations. This chapter discussed the elements of basic airplane control, with emphasis on AOA. It offered a discussion of circumstances and scenarios that can lead to LOC-I, including stalls and airplane upsets.

sections.title: Takeoffs and Departure Climbs
sections.summary: Apply takeoff planning and performance, normal and crosswind takeoffs, short-field and obstacle takeoffs, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Takeoff planning and performance
lessons.concept: Prior to Takeoff Before going to the airplane, the pilot should check the POH/AFM performance charts to determine the predicted performance and decide if the airplane is capable of a safe takeoff and climb for the conditions and location.
lessons.explanation: In most airplanes, this is the result of the pilot rotating the nose up to increase the angle of attack (AOA). ⦁ The initial climb begins when the airplane leaves the surface and a climb pitch attitude has been established. Normally, it is considered complete when the airplane has reached a safe maneuvering altitude or an en route climb has been established.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how takeoff planning and performance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on takeoff planning and performance: "Prior to Takeoff Before going to the airplane, the pilot should check the POH/AFM performance charts to determine the predicted performance and decide if the airplane is capable of a safe ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: by the manufacturer) and VY has been established
snow and may require climbing over an obstacle
takeoff and climb for the conditions and location
wingtip strobes should be illuminated immediately prior to initiating the takeoff roll
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Prior to Takeoff Before going to the airplane, the pilot should check the POH/AFM performance charts to determine the predicted performance and decide if the airplane is capable of a safe takeoff and climb for the conditions and location.

lessons.title: Normal and crosswind takeoffs
lessons.concept: Therefore, the pilot must be familiar with the principles and techniques involved in crosswind takeoffs, as well as those for normal takeoffs.
lessons.explanation: ⦁ Failure to attain/maintain best rate-of-climb airspeed (VY) or desired climb airspeed. ⦁ Failure to employ the principles of attitude flying during climb-out, resulting in “chasing” the airspeed indicator. Crosswind Takeoff While it is usually preferable to take off directly into the wind whenever possible or practical, there are many instances when circumstances or judgment indicate otherwise.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how normal and crosswind takeoffs affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: snow and may require climbing over an obstacle
takeoff and climb for the conditions and location
the pilot must be familiar with the principles and techniques involved in crosswind takeoffs
the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Therefore, the pilot must be familiar with the principles and techniques involved in crosswind takeoffs, as well as those for normal takeoffs.

lessons.title: Short-field and obstacle takeoffs
lessons.concept: Initial Climb On short-field takeoffs, the landing gear and flaps should remain in takeoff position until the airplane is clear of obstacles (or as recommended by the manufacturer) and VY has been established.
lessons.explanation: In these airplanes, it may be necessary to allow the airplane to lift off in ground effect and then reduce pitch attitude to level until the airplane accelerates to VX with the wheels just clear of the runway surface. This method is preferable to forcing the airplane to remain on the ground with forward elevator-control pressure until VX is attained. Holding the airplane on the ground unnecessarily puts excessive pressure on the nose-wheel and may result in “wheel barrowing.” It also hinders both acceleration and overall airplane performance.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how short-field and obstacle takeoffs affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: snow and may require climbing over an obstacle

takeoff and climb for the conditions and location
(VYSE) until short final with the landing assured
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Initial Climb On short-field takeoffs, the landing gear and flaps should remain in takeoff position until the airplane is clear of obstacles (or as recommended by the manufacturer) and VY has been established.

lessons.title: Soft-field takeoffs and rejected takeoffs
lessons.concept: Soft/Rough-Field Takeoff and Climb Takeoffs and climbs from soft fields require the use of operational techniques for getting the airplane airborne as quickly as possible to eliminate the drag caused by tall grass, soft sand, mud, and snow and may require climbing over an obstacle.
lessons.explanation: The technique makes judicious use of ground effect to reduce landing gear drag and requires an understanding of the airplane’s slow speed characteristics and responses. These same techniques are also useful on a rough field where the pilot should get the airplane off the ground as soon as possible to avoid damaging the landing gear. Taking off from a soft surface or through soft surfaces or long, wet grass reduces the airplane’s ability to accelerate during the takeoff roll and may prevent the airplane from reaching adequate takeoff speed if the pilot applies normal takeoff techniques.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how soft-field takeoffs and rejected takeoffs affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Ground Reference Maneuvers
sections.summary: Apply wind drift and ground track, rectangular course, s-turns across a road, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Wind drift and ground track
lessons.concept: Drift and Ground Track Control Wind direction and velocity variations create the need for flightpath corrections during a ground reference maneuver.
lessons.explanation: Before performing any maneuver, the pilot should complete the required checklist items, make any radio announcements (such as on a practice area frequency), and safety clearing turns. As a general note, a ground reference maneuver should not exceed a bank angle of 45° or an airspeed greater than the maneuvering speed. As part of preflight planning, the pilot should determine the predicted (POH/AFM) stall speed at 50° or at the highest bank angle expected during the maneuver to assure there will be a safety margin above the stall speed during the maneuver.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wind drift and ground track affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on wind drift and ground track: "Drift and Ground Track Control Wind direction and velocity variations create the need ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers
maneuver is the rectangular course
for flightpath corrections during a ground reference maneuver
in the same manner as the first half
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Drift and Ground Track Control Wind direction and velocity variations create the need for flightpath corrections during a ground reference maneuver.

lessons.title: Rectangular course
lessons.concept: Rectangular Course A principal ground reference maneuver is the rectangular course.
lessons.explanation: When proficiency has been demonstrated by flying directly over the ground reference line, the pilot should then practice flying a straight parallel path that is offset from the ground reference. The offset parallel path should not be more than three-fourths of a mile from the reference line. The maneuver should be flown offset from the ground references with the pilot angling the airplane’s longitudinal axis into the wind sufficiently to cancel the effect of drift while maintaining a parallel track.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how rectangular course affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Rectangular Course A principal ground reference ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: maneuver is the rectangular course
for flightpath corrections during a ground reference maneuver
the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers
in the same manner as the first half
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Rectangular Course A principal ground reference maneuver is the rectangular course.

lessons.title: S-turns across a road
lessons.concept: The following are the most common errors made while performing S-turns across a road: 1.
lessons.explanation: As the groundspeed increases, the pilot should increase the bank angle to maintain a constant-radius turn over the ground. At the 90° crosswind position, the airplane should also have the correct wind correction angle. As the airplane turns downwind and the groundspeed increases, the bank angle should be increased so that the rate of turn maintains a constant-radius turn.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how s-turns across a road affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about s-turns across a road: "As the airplane turns downwind and the groundspeed increases, the bank angle should be increased so that ___."
options: the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers
for flightpath corrections during a ground reference maneuver
the rate of turn maintains a constant-radius turn
constant-altitude turns affect the airplane during climbing turns
practice.explanation: The cited page states: As the airplane turns downwind and the groundspeed increases, the bank angle should be increased so that the rate of turn maintains a constant-radius turn.

lessons.title: Turns around a point
lessons.concept: Eights Around Pylons Eights around pylons is a ground reference maneuver with the same principles and techniques of correcting for wind drift as used in turns around a point and the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers.
lessons.explanation: Eights around pylons utilizes two ground reference points called “pylons.” Turns around each pylon are made in opposite directions to follow a ground track in the form of a figure 8. The pattern involves flying downwind between the pylons and upwind outside of the pylons. It may include a short period of straightand-level flight while proceeding diagonally from one pylon to the other.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how turns around a point affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: for flightpath corrections during a ground reference maneuver
maneuver is the rectangular course
the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers
in the same manner as the first half
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Eights Around Pylons Eights around pylons is a ground reference maneuver with the same principles and techniques of correcting for wind drift as used in turns around a point and the same objectives as other ground track maneuvers.

lessons.title: Eights on pylons and pivotal altitude
lessons.concept: Attempting to correct pivotal altitude by the using the altimeter is ineffective.
lessons.explanation: The pilot may make corrections for temporary variations, such as those caused by gusts or inattention, by reducing the bank angle slightly to fly relatively straight to bring forward a lagging visual reference line or by increasing the bank angle temporarily to turn back a visual reference line that has moved ahead. With practice, these corrections may become slight enough to be barely noticeable. It is important to understand that variations in pylon position are according to the apparent movement of the visual reference line.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how eights on pylons and pivotal altitude affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Attempting to correct pivotal altitude by the using the altimeter is ineffective.

sections.title: Airport Traffic Patterns
sections.summary: Apply pattern entries, legs, and departures, wind correction and spacing, collision avoidance and communications, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Pattern entries, legs, and departures
lessons.concept: A pilot is not expected to have extensive knowledge of all traffic patterns at all airports, but if the pilot is familiar with the basic rectangular pattern, it is easy to make proper approaches and departures from most airports, regardless of whether or not they have control towers.
lessons.explanation: The tower operator can instruct pilots to enter the traffic pattern at any point or to make a straight-in approach without flying the usual rectangular pattern. Many other deviations are possible if the tower operator and the pilot work together in an effort to keep traffic moving smoothly. Jets or heavy airplanes will frequently fly wider and/or higher patterns than lighter airplanes, and will sometimes make a straight-in approach for landing.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pattern entries, legs, and departures affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on pattern entries, legs, and departures: "A pilot is not expected to have extensive knowledge of all traffic patterns at all airports, but if the pilot is familiar with the basic rectangular pattern, it is easy to make proper approaches and departures from most airports, ___."
options: a gradual descent to the intended touchdown point
regardless of whether or not they have control towers
If the turn to final would create a collision hazard
UPRT Training Core Concepts Airplane upsets are by nature time-critical events
practice.explanation: The cited page states: A pilot is not expected to have extensive knowledge of all traffic patterns at all airports, but if the pilot is familiar with the basic rectangular pattern, it is easy to make proper approaches and departures from most airports, regardless of whether or not they have control towers.

lessons.title: Wind correction and spacing
lessons.concept: Depending on the wind condition, the pilot should establish the base leg at a sufficient distance from the approach end of the landing runway to permit a gradual descent to the intended touchdown point.
lessons.explanation: The base leg is the transitional part of the traffic pattern between the downwind leg and the final approach leg. While on the base leg, the ground track of the airplane is perpendicular to the extended centerline of the landing runway, although the longitudinal axis of the airplane may not be aligned with the ground track if turned into the wind to counteract drift. While on the base leg and before turning onto the final approach, the pilot should ensure that there is no close proximity to another aircraft already established on final approach.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wind correction and spacing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing wind correction and spacing, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "Depending on the wind condition, the pilot should establish the base leg at a sufficient distance from the approach end of the landing runway to permit ___."
options: regardless of whether or not they have control towers
If the turn to final would create a collision hazard
a gradual descent to the intended touchdown point
towards a predetermined point on the landing runway
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Depending on the wind condition, the pilot should establish the base leg at a sufficient distance from the approach end of the landing runway to permit a gradual descent to the intended touchdown point.

lessons.title: Collision avoidance and communications
lessons.concept: If the turn to final would create a collision hazard, a go-around or avoidance maneuver is in order.
lessons.explanation: While on the base leg and before turning onto the final approach, the pilot should ensure that there is no close proximity to another aircraft already established on final approach. When two or more aircraft are approaching an airport for the purpose of landing, the aircraft at the lower altitude has the right-of-way. However, pilots should not take advantage of this rule to cut in front of another aircraft that is on final approach to land or to overtake that aircraft.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how collision avoidance and communications affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Normal Approaches and Landings
sections.summary: Apply stabilized approach concept, normal approach and roundout, crosswind approach and touchdown, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Stabilized approach concept
lessons.concept: Stabilized Approach Concept A stabilized approach is one in which the pilot establishes and maintains a constant-angle glide path towards a predetermined point on the landing runway.
lessons.explanation: On final approach, the pilot should verify correct runway alignment and runway number. Pilots often refer to moving map displays driven by GPS, and these devices should increase situational awareness and safety. If there is a doubt over the landing surface, the pilot should go around and consider the situation further.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how stabilized approach concept affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "Stabilized Approach Concept A stabilized approach is one in which the pilot establishes and maintains a constant-angle glide path ___."
options: towards a predetermined point on the landing runway
are capable of further steepening the bank angle
“Crosswind Approach and Landing” section of this chapter
a gradual descent to the intended touchdown point
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Stabilized Approach Concept A stabilized approach is one in which the pilot establishes and maintains a constant-angle glide path towards a predetermined point on the landing runway.

lessons.title: Normal approach and roundout
lessons.concept: Change in runway shape if approach becomes narrow or steep.
lessons.explanation: Immediately after rolling out on final approach, the pilot adjusts the pitch attitude, power, and trim so that the airplane is descending directly toward the aiming point at the appropriate airspeed in the landing configuration. If it appears that the airplane is going to overshoot the desired landing spot, a steeper approach results by reducing power and lowering the pitch attitude to maintain airspeed. If available and not fully extended, the pilot may further extend the flaps.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how normal approach and roundout affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about normal approach and roundout: "Change in runway shape if ___."
options: towards a predetermined point on the landing runway
“Crosswind Approach and Landing” section of this chapter
approach becomes narrow or steep
a gradual descent to the intended touchdown point
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Change in runway shape if approach becomes narrow or steep.

lessons.title: Crosswind approach and touchdown
lessons.concept: Procedures for using ailerons in crosswind conditions are explained in the “Crosswind Approach and Landing” section of this chapter.
lessons.explanation: During the after-landing roll, they are used to keep the wings level in much the same way they are used in flight. If a wing starts to rise, aileron control is applied toward that wing to lower it. The amount required depends on speed because as the forward speed of the airplane decreases, the ailerons become less effective.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how crosswind approach and touchdown affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

tall grass
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Procedures for using ailerons in crosswind conditions are explained in the “Crosswind Approach and Landing” section of this chapter.

lessons.title: Go-around decision and execution
lessons.concept: First, on most airplanes full flaps produce more drag than the landing gear; and second, in case the airplane inadvertently touches down as the go-around is initiated, it is desirable to have the landing gear in the down-and-locked position.
lessons.explanation: Unless otherwise specified in the AFM/POH, it is generally recommended that the flaps be retracted (at least partially) before retracting the landing gear for two reasons. After a positive rate of climb is established, the landing gear is retracted. The landing gear is retracted only after the initial or rough trim is accomplished and when it is certain the airplane will remain airborne.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how go-around decision and execution affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Forward slips and side slips
lessons.concept: In both sideslips and forward slips, the point may be reached where full rudder is required to maintain heading even though the ailerons are capable of further steepening the bank angle.
lessons.explanation: The pilot controls airspeed using elevator control. When a crosswind is present, the pilot should lower the upwind wing such that the airplane is banked into the crosswind since slipping into the wind makes it easier to remain on the original flightpath. 048 In most light airplanes, the steepness of a slip is limited by the amount of rudder travel available.

sections.title: Specialized and Emergency Landings
sections.summary: Apply short-field approach and landing, soft-field approach and landing, power-off accuracy approach, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Short-field approach and landing
lessons.concept: The short-field approach and landing is an accuracy approach to an aiming point.
lessons.explanation: After the landing gear has been extended, if applicable, or when beginning a suitable final approach, the pilot simultaneously adjusts the power and the pitch attitude to establish and maintain the proper descent angle and airspeed. During a stabilized approach, small changes in the airplane’s pitch attitude and power setting are needed when making corrections to the angle of descent and airspeed. The procedures previously outlined in the section on the stabilized approach concept are used.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how short-field approach and landing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: accuracy approach to an aiming point

towards a predetermined point on the landing runway
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The short-field approach and landing is an accuracy approach to an aiming point.

lessons.title: Soft-field approach and landing
lessons.concept: Soft-Field Approach and Landing Landing on fields that are rough or have soft surfaces, such as snow, sand, mud, or tall grass, requires unique procedures.
lessons.explanation: Excessive and/or unnecessary braking after touchdown. Failure to maintain directional control. Failure to recognize and abort a poor approach that cannot be completed safely.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how soft-field approach and landing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Soft-Field Approach and Landing Landing on fields that are rough or have soft surfaces, such as snow, sand, mud, or tall grass, requires unique procedures.

lessons.title: Power-off accuracy approach
lessons.concept: After reaching the base key position, the approach and landing are the same as in the 90° power-off approach.
lessons.explanation: Many inexperienced pilots may gain a conception of it as a particular landmark, such as a tree, crossroad, or other visual reference, to be reached at a certain altitude. This misconception leaves the pilot at a total loss any time such objects are not present. Both altitude and geographical location should be varied as much as is practical to eliminate any such misconceptions.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how power-off accuracy approach affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Faulty approaches and bounced landings
lessons.concept: Faulty Approaches and Landings Landing involves many precise, time-sensitive, and sequential control inputs.
lessons.explanation: When corrected early, small errors are often not noticeable. On the other hand, uncorrected errors may place the airplane and occupants in an undesirable state. Since pilot training normally includes exposure to landing deviations and their appropriate remedies, this section covers several common landing imperfections.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how faulty approaches and bounced landings affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Emergency approaches and off-airport landing
lessons.concept: Emergency Approaches and Landings (Simulated) During dual training flights, the instructor should give simulated emergency landings by retarding the throttle and calling “simulated emergency landing.” The objective of these simulated emergency landings is to develop a pilot’s accuracy, judgment, planning,...
lessons.explanation: A simulated emergency landing may be given with the airplane in any configuration. If the simulated power failure occurs while above best glide speed, the pilot allows the airplane to slow (or may even bleed off speed by climbing) until reaching best glide speed. When reaching that speed, the nose can be lowered and the airplane trimmed to maintain that speed.

sections.title: Performance Maneuvers
sections.summary: Apply steep turns and load management, steep spirals and chandelles, lazy eights and maneuver coordination, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Steep turns and load management
lessons.concept: Because of higher load factors, steep turns should be performed at an airspeed that does not exceed the airplane’s design maneuvering speed (VA) or operating maneuvering speed (VO).
lessons.explanation: Pilots should also understand that load factors increase dramatically during a level turn beyond 60° of bank. Note that the design of a standard category general aviation airplane accommodates a load factor up to 3.8. A level turn using 75° of bank exceeds that limit.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how steep turns and load management affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

in the same manner as the first half
constant-altitude turns affect the airplane during climbing turns
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Because of higher load factors, steep turns should be performed at an airspeed that does not exceed the airplane’s design maneuvering speed (VA) or operating maneuvering speed (VO).

lessons.title: Steep spirals and chandelles
lessons.concept: Common errors when performing steep spirals are: 1.
lessons.explanation: In addition, noting the altitude lost during each revolution would help the pilot determine when to roll out in an actual emergency so as not to be too high or too low to make a safe approach. During rollout, the smooth and accurate application of the flight controls allow the airplane to recover to a wings-level glide with no change in airspeed. Recovering to normal cruise flight would proceed after the establishment of a wings-level glide.

lessons.title: Lazy eights and maneuver coordination
lessons.concept: Common errors when performing lazy eights are: 1.
lessons.explanation: Upon arriving at 180° point, a climbing turn should be started immediately in the opposite direction toward the preselected reference points to complete the second half of the lazy eight in the same manner as the first half. [Figure 10-4E] Power should be set so as not to enter the maneuver at an airspeed that would exceed manufacturer’s recommendations, which is generally no greater than VA or VO. Power and bank angle have significant effect on the altitude gained or lost; if excess power is used for a given bank angle, altitude is gained at the completion of the maneuver; however, if insufficient power is used for a given bank angle, altitude is lost.

sections.title: Night Operations
sections.summary: Apply night vision and visual illusions, night preflight and lighting, night takeoff, navigation, and landing, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Night vision and visual illusions
lessons.concept: Night Illusions Visual illusions are especially hazardous because pilots rely on their eyes for correct information.
lessons.explanation: If continuous, the possible physical reactions can be nausea, dizziness, grogginess, unconsciousness, headaches, or confusion. Pilots should try to eliminate or screen out any light source that might cause an unwanted reaction to blinking or flickering lights. ⦁ Sunglasses can aid the dark adaptation process, which is delayed by prolonged exposure to bright sunlight.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how night vision and visual illusions affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on night vision and visual illusions: "Night Illusions Visual illusions are especially hazardous because ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: to the safety and efficiency of night flying
that is required for VFR flight at night
pilots rely on their eyes for correct information
the using the altimeter is ineffective
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Night Illusions Visual illusions are especially hazardous because pilots rely on their eyes for correct information.

lessons.title: Night preflight and lighting
lessons.concept: Airplane Equipment and Lighting 14 CFR part 91, section 91.205(c) specifies the basic minimum airplane equipment that is required for VFR flight at night.
lessons.explanation: The lights of cities and towns can be seen at surprising distances at night, and if this adjacent chart is not available to identify those landmarks, confusion could result. Regardless of the equipment used, organization of the flight deck eases the burden and enhances safety. Organize equipment and charts and place them within easy reach prior to taxiing.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how night preflight and lighting affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Airplane Equipment and Lighting 14 CFR part 91, section 91.205(c) specifies the basic minimum airplane equipment that is required for VFR flight at night.

lessons.title: Night takeoff, navigation, and landing
lessons.concept: The use of radio navigation aids and communication facilities add significantly to the safety and efficiency of night flying.
lessons.explanation: Rotating beacons at airports, lighted obstructions, lights of cities or towns, and lights from major highway traffic all provide excellent visual checkpoints. If using a global positioning system (GPS) for navigation, the pilot should ensure that it works properly. All necessary waypoints should be loaded before the flight, and the database should be checked for accuracy prior to taking off and again once in flight.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how night takeoff, navigation, and landing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Night emergencies and inadvertent IMC
lessons.concept: Night Emergencies Perhaps the greatest concern about flying a single-engine airplane at night is the possibility of a complete engine failure and the subsequent emergency landing.
lessons.explanation: Utilize the visual descent point (VDP) found on many nonprecision instrument approach procedure charts. Recognize that the chances of being involved in an approach accident increase when some emergency or other activity distracts from usual procedures. Maintain optimum proficiency in landing procedures.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how night emergencies and inadvertent imc affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.
