sections.title: Transition to Complex Airplanes
sections.summary: Apply complex-airplane systems, retractable landing gear, constant-speed propeller, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Complex-airplane systems
lessons.concept: This flap can be multi-slotted making it the most complex of the trailing-edge systems.
lessons.explanation: Small deflections of the slotted flap give a higher drag than the hinge flap but less than the split. This allows the slotted flap to be used for takeoff. The Fowler flap deflects down and aft to increase the wing area.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how complex-airplane systems affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about complex-airplane systems: "This flap can be multi-slotted making it ___."
options: Manage the “heat system” with the throttle
propeller control in the low pitch/high rpm position
also the procedures for coping with uncommanded reverse
the most complex of the trailing-edge systems
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This flap can be multi-slotted making it the most complex of the trailing-edge systems.

lessons.title: Retractable landing gear
lessons.concept: Retractable Landing Gear The primary benefits of being able to retract the landing gear are increased climb performance and higher cruise airspeeds due to a decrease in drag after gear retraction.
lessons.explanation: Retractable landing gear systems may be operated either hydraulically or electrically or may employ a combination of the two systems. Warning indicators are provided in the flight deck to show the pilot when the wheels are down and locked and when they are up and locked or if they are in intermediate positions. Systems for emergency operation are also provided.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how retractable landing gear affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "Retractable Landing Gear The primary benefits of being able to retract the landing gear are increased climb performance and higher cruise airspeeds due ___."
options: a turbocharged engine runs at higher operating temperatures than a nonturbocharged engine
Manage the “heat system” with the throttle
on most airplanes full flaps produce more drag than the landing gear
to a decrease in drag after gear retraction
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Retractable Landing Gear The primary benefits of being able to retract the landing gear are increased climb performance and higher cruise airspeeds due to a decrease in drag after gear retraction.

lessons.title: Constant-speed propeller
lessons.concept: Constant-Speed Propeller Operation The engine is started with the propeller control in the low pitch/high rpm position.
lessons.explanation: From that point, any further reduction in airspeed causes the engine rpm to decrease. Conversely, if the airspeed increases, the pitch angle of the propeller blades increase until the high pitch stop is reached. The engine rpm then begins to increase.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how constant-speed propeller affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about constant-speed propeller: "Constant-Speed Propeller Operation The engine is started with the ___."
options: a turbocharged engine runs at higher operating temperatures than a nonturbocharged engine
propeller control in the low pitch/high rpm position
Manage the “heat system” with the throttle
the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Constant-Speed Propeller Operation The engine is started with the propeller control in the low pitch/high rpm position.

lessons.title: Flaps, trim, and operating technique
lessons.concept: Due to the heat of compression of the induction air, a turbocharged engine runs at higher operating temperatures than a nonturbocharged engine.
lessons.explanation: Because turbocharged engines operate at high altitudes, their environment is less efficient for cooling. At altitude, the air is less dense and, therefore, cools less efficiently. Also, the less dense air causes the compressor to work harder.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how flaps, trim, and operating technique affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for flaps, trim, and operating technique. Which phrase completes "Due to the heat of compression of the induction air, ___."?
options: a turbocharged engine runs at higher operating temperatures than a nonturbocharged engine
Manage the “heat system” with the throttle
propeller control in the low pitch/high rpm position
temperatures fall into the normal operating range
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Due to the heat of compression of the induction air, a turbocharged engine runs at higher operating temperatures than a nonturbocharged engine.

lessons.title: Gear and propeller malfunctions
lessons.concept: Manage the “heat system” with the throttle, propeller rpm, mixture, and cowl flaps.
lessons.explanation: Turbocharged engines are especially heat sensitive. The key to turbocharger operation is effective heat management. Monitor the condition of a turbocharged engine with manifold pressure gauge, tachometer, exhaust gas temperature/turbine inlet temperature gauge, and cylinder head temperature gauge.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how gear and propeller malfunctions affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

to a decrease in drag after gear retraction

on most airplanes full flaps produce more drag than the landing gear
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Manage the “heat system” with the throttle, propeller rpm, mixture, and cowl flaps.

sections.title: Multiengine Systems and Normal Operations
sections.summary: Apply multiengine aerodynamics, systems and performance, critical engine and vmc, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Multiengine aerodynamics
lessons.concept: Chapter 13: Transition to Multiengine Airplanes Introduction This chapter is devoted to the factors associated with the operation of small multiengine airplanes.
lessons.explanation: For the purpose of this handbook, a “small” multiengine airplane is a reciprocating or turbopropeller-powered airplane with a maximum certificated takeoff weight of 12,500 pounds or less. This discussion assumes a conventional design with two engines—one mounted on each wing. Reciprocating engines are assumed unless otherwise noted.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how multiengine aerodynamics affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about multiengine aerodynamics: "This chapter is devoted to the factors associated ___."
options: There is a dramatic performance loss associated with the loss of an engine
with the operation of small multiengine airplanes
taxi lights
which are unique to gas turbine engines
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This chapter is devoted to the factors associated with the operation of small multiengine airplanes.

lessons.title: Systems and performance
lessons.concept: There is a dramatic performance loss associated with the loss of an engine, particularly just after takeoff.
lessons.explanation: Any airplane’s climb performance is a function of thrust horsepower, which is in excess of that required for level flight. In a hypothetical twin with each engine producing 200 thrust horsepower, assume that the total level flight thrust horsepower required is 175. In this situation, the airplane would ordinarily have a reserve of 225 thrust horsepower available for climb.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how systems and performance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing systems and performance, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "___, particularly just after takeoff."
options: thereafter maintain straight flight at the same speed with an angle of

which are unique to gas turbine engines
There is a dramatic performance loss associated with the loss of an engine
practice.explanation: The cited page states: There is a dramatic performance loss associated with the loss of an engine, particularly just after takeoff.

lessons.title: Critical engine and VMC
lessons.concept: VMC was previously defined in 14 CFR part 23, section 23.149 as the calibrated airspeed at which, when the critical engine is suddenly made inoperative, it is possible to maintain control of the airplane with that engine still inoperative, and thereafter maintain straight flight at the same speed with an angle of...
lessons.explanation: ⦁ VMC—currently defined in 14 CFR part 23, section 23.2135(c) as the calibrated airspeed at which, following the sudden critical loss of thrust, it is possible to maintain control of the airplane. VMC is typically marked with a red radial line on most airspeed indicators [Figure 13-1]. This definition still applies to airplanes certified under that regulation.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how critical engine and vmc affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

thereafter maintain straight flight at the same speed with an angle of

(VYSE) until short final with the landing assured

lessons.title: Normal takeoff and climb
lessons.concept: Short-Field Takeoff and Climb The short-field takeoff and climb differs from the normal takeoff and climb in the airspeeds and initial climb profile.
lessons.explanation: Some AFM/POHs give separate short-field takeoff procedures and performance charts that recommend specific flap settings and airspeeds. Other AFM/POHs do not provide separate short-field procedures. In the absence of such specific procedures, the airplane should be operated only as recommended in the AFM/POH.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how normal takeoff and climb affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about normal takeoff and climb: "Short-Field Takeoff and Climb The short-field takeoff and climb differs from the normal takeoff and ___."
options: There is a dramatic performance loss associated with the loss of an engine
taxi lights
climb in the airspeeds and initial climb profile
by the manufacturer) and VY has been established
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Short-Field Takeoff and Climb The short-field takeoff and climb differs from the normal takeoff and climb in the airspeeds and initial climb profile.

lessons.title: Approach, landing, and go-around
lessons.concept: Exterior lights, such as landing and taxi lights, and wingtip strobes should be illuminated immediately prior to initiating the takeoff roll, day or night.
lessons.explanation: Sharp turns onto the runway combined with a rolling takeoff are not a good operating practice and may be prohibited by the AFM/POH due to the possibility of “unporting” a fuel tank pickup. The takeoff itself may be prohibited by the AFM/POH under any circumstances below certain fuel levels. The flight controls should be positioned for a crosswind, if present.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how approach, landing, and go-around affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Engine-Inoperative Operations
sections.summary: Apply engine-failure recognition and control, drag reduction and configuration, identify, verify, feather, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Engine-failure recognition and control
lessons.concept: The final approach should be made with power and at a speed recommended by the manufacturer; if a recommended speed is not furnished, the speed should be no slower than the single-engine best rate-of-climb speed (VYSE) until short final with the landing assured, but in no case less than critical engine-out minimum...
lessons.explanation: [Figure 13-10] The FAA recommends a stabilized approach concept. To the greatest extent practical, on final approach and within 500 feet AGL, the airplane should be on speed, in trim, configured for landing, tracking the extended centerline of the runway, and established in a constant angle of descent toward an aim point in the touchdown zone. Absent unusual flight conditions, only minor corrections are required to maintain this approach to the round out and touchdown.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how engine-failure recognition and control affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about engine-failure recognition and control: "The final approach should be made with power and at a speed recommended by the manufacturer; if a recommended speed is not furnished, the speed should be no slower than the single-engine best rate-of-climb speed ___, but in no case less than critical engine-out minimum..."
options: the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
by the manufacturer) and VY has been established
(VYSE) until short final with the landing assured
maximum takeoff power available on all engines
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The final approach should be made with power and at a speed recommended by the manufacturer; if a recommended speed is not furnished, the speed should be no slower than the single-engine best rate-of-climb speed (VYSE) until short final with the landing assured, but in no case less than critical engine-out minimum...

lessons.title: Drag reduction and configuration
lessons.concept: The emphasis in a short-field approach is on configuration (full flaps), a stabilized approach with a constant angle of descent, and precise airspeed control.
lessons.explanation: Many manufacturers do not publish short-field landing techniques or performance charts in the AFM/POH. In the absence of specific shortfield approach and landing procedures, the airplane should be operated as recommended in the AFM/POH. No operations should be conducted contrary to the AFM/POH recommendations.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how drag reduction and configuration affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on drag reduction and configuration: "___, a stabilized approach with a constant angle of descent, and precise airspeed control." What belongs in the blank?
options: The emphasis in a short-field approach is on configuration (full flaps)
(VYSE) until short final with the landing assured
the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
towards a predetermined point on the landing runway
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The emphasis in a short-field approach is on configuration (full flaps), a stabilized approach with a constant angle of descent, and precise airspeed control.

lessons.title: Identify, verify, feather
lessons.concept: The “identify” step is for the pilot to initially identify the failed engine.
lessons.explanation: Confirmation on the engine gauges may or may not be possible, depending upon the failure mode. Identification should be primarily through the control inputs required to maintain straight flight, not the engine gauges. The “verify” step directs the pilot to retard the throttle of the engine thought to have failed.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how identify, verify, feather affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "The “identify” step is for the pilot ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: the securing failed engine checklist completed
the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
to initially identify the failed engine
When an engine failure occurs during the initial climb
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The “identify” step is for the pilot to initially identify the failed engine.

lessons.title: Restart and securing procedures
lessons.concept: The affected engine should be feathered and the securing failed engine checklist completed.
lessons.explanation: If fuel vapor formation is suspected, fuel boost pump operation may be used to eliminate flow and pressure fluctuations. Although it is a natural desire among pilots to save an ailing engine with a precautionary shutdown, the engine should be left running if there is any doubt as to needing it for further safe flight. Catastrophic failure accompanied by heavy vibration, smoke, blistering paint, or large trails of oil, on the other hand, indicate a critical situation.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how restart and securing procedures affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on restart and securing procedures: "The affected engine should be feathered and ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
(VYSE) until short final with the landing assured
the securing failed engine checklist completed
When an engine failure occurs during the initial climb
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The affected engine should be feathered and the securing failed engine checklist completed.

lessons.title: Single-engine approach and landing
lessons.concept: Engine Inoperative Approach and Landing The approach and landing with OEI is essentially the same as a two-engine approach and landing.
lessons.explanation: At very low power settings, the pilot may not even be aware of a failure. If a failure is suspected, the pilot should advance both engine mixtures, propellers, and throttles significantly, to the takeoff settings if necessary, to correctly identify the failed engine. The power on the operative engine can always be reduced later.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how single-engine approach and landing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Engine Inoperative Approach and Landing The approach and landing with OEI is essentially ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: (VYSE) until short final with the landing assured
The emphasis in a short-field approach is on configuration (full flaps)
After reaching the base key position
the same as a two-engine approach and landing
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Engine Inoperative Approach and Landing The approach and landing with OEI is essentially the same as a two-engine approach and landing.

lessons.title: Engine failure after liftoff and decision-making
lessons.concept: When an instructor simulates an engine failure, the learner should respond with the appropriate memory items and retard the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position.
lessons.explanation: Assuming zero thrust will be set, the instructor promptly moves the propeller control forward and sets the appropriate manifold pressure and rpm. It is vital that the learner be kept informed of the instructor's intentions. At this point the instructor may say words to the effect, "I have the right engine; you have the left.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how engine failure after liftoff and decision-making affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Transition to Tailwheel Airplanes
sections.summary: Apply tailwheel stability and directional control, ground operations and takeoff, wheel landings, three-point landings, and go-arounds, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Tailwheel stability and directional control
lessons.concept: The tailwheel also supports the airplane, but steering and directional control are its primary functions.
lessons.explanation: Although still termed “conventional-gear airplanes,” tailwheel designs are most likely to be encountered today by pilots who have first learned in nose-wheels. Therefore, tailwheel operations are approached as they appear to a pilot making a transition from nosewheel designs. Landing Gear The main landing gear forms the principal support of the airplane on the ground.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how tailwheel stability and directional control affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "The tailwheel also supports the airplane, but ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: the timing of the touchdown (early and later)
vehicle has positioned itself directly under the nose
steering and directional control are its primary functions
UPRT Training Core Concepts Airplane upsets are by nature time-critical events
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The tailwheel also supports the airplane, but steering and directional control are its primary functions.

lessons.title: Ground operations and takeoff
lessons.concept: In aircraft that are completely blind ahead, all taxi movements should be started with a small turn to ensure no other plane or ground vehicle has positioned itself directly under the nose while the pilot’s attention was distracted with getting ready to takeoff.
lessons.explanation: Visibility In the normal nose-high attitude, the engine cowling may be high enough to restrict the pilot’s vision of the area directly ahead of the airplane while on the ground. Consequently, objects directly ahead are difficult, if not impossible to see. In taxiing such an airplane, the pilot should alternately turn the nose from one side to the other (zigzag) or make a series of short S-turns.

lessons.title: Wheel landings, three-point landings, and go-arounds
lessons.concept: Note: The only difference between three-point and wheel landings is the timing of the touchdown (early and later).
lessons.explanation: If the touchdown is made at too high a rate of descent, the tail is forced down by its own weight, resulting in a sudden increase in lift. If the pilot now pushes forward in an attempt to again make contact with the surface, a potentially dangerous pilot-induced oscillation may develop. It is far better to respond to a bounced wheel landing attempt by initiating a go-around or converting to a three-point landing if conditions permit.

sections.title: Transition to Turboprop Airplanes
sections.summary: Apply turboprop engine arrangements, power, propeller, and condition controls, operating limits and indications, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Turboprop engine arrangements
lessons.concept: The major differences between flying a turboprop and other non-turbine-powered airplanes are found in the handling of the airplane’s powerplant and its associated systems, which are unique to gas turbine engines.
lessons.explanation: The turbopropeller-powered airplane flies and handles just like any other airplane of comparable size and weight, since the aerodynamics are the same. The turbopropellerpowered airplane also has the advantage of being equipped with a constant speed, full feathering and reversing propeller—something normally not found on piston-powered airplanes. Gas Turbine Engine Both piston (reciprocating) engines and gas turbine engines are internal combustion engines.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how turboprop engine arrangements affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing turboprop engine arrangements, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "The major differences between flying a turboprop and other non-turbine-powered airplanes are found in the handling of the airplane’s powerplant and its associated systems, ___."
options: three sets of controls for each engine
Engine Start Procedures
There is a dramatic performance loss associated with the loss of an engine
which are unique to gas turbine engines
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The major differences between flying a turboprop and other non-turbine-powered airplanes are found in the handling of the airplane’s powerplant and its associated systems, which are unique to gas turbine engines.

lessons.title: Power, propeller, and condition controls
lessons.concept: Powerplant (engine and propeller) operation is achieved by three sets of controls for each engine: the power lever, propeller lever, and condition lever.
lessons.explanation: The accessory drive at the aft end of the engine provides power to drive fuel pumps, fuel control, oil pumps, a starter/generator, and a tachometer transmitter. At this point, the speed of the drive (N1) is the true speed of the compressor side of the engine, approximately 37,500 rpm. [Figure 15-6] The power lever serves to control engine power in the range from idle through takeoff power.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how power, propeller, and condition controls affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

propeller control in the low pitch/high rpm position
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Powerplant (engine and propeller) operation is achieved by three sets of controls for each engine: the power lever, propeller lever, and condition lever.

lessons.title: Operating limits and indications
lessons.concept: Engine temperatures may approach the maximum in a matter of 2 or 3 seconds before the engine stabilizes and temperatures fall into the normal operating range.
lessons.explanation: Vigilant monitoring of temperature and acceleration on the part of the pilot remain crucial until the engine is running at a stable speed. Successful engine starting depends on assuring the correct minimum battery voltage before initiating start or employing a ground power unit (GPU) of adequate output. After fuel is introduced to the combustion chamber during the start sequence, “light-off” and its associated heat rise occur very quickly.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how operating limits and indications affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: Engine Start Procedures

and temperatures fall into the normal operating range
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Engine temperatures may approach the maximum in a matter of 2 or 3 seconds before the engine stabilizes and temperatures fall into the normal operating range.

lessons.concept: Engine Start Procedures, Run-up, Takeoff, and Initial Climb 5.

lessons.title: Approach, landing, and emergencies
lessons.concept: Like takeoffs and departures, approach and landing should be accomplished in accordance with a standard approach and landing profile.
lessons.explanation: Power capability is reduced by high-density altitude and power use may have to be modulated to keep engine temperature within limits. In a turboprop airplane, the pilot can close the throttles(s) at any time without concern for cooling the engine too rapidly. Consequently, rapid descents with the propellers in low pitch can be dramatically steep.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how approach, landing, and emergencies affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

quiz.explanation: The cited page states: The major differences between flying a turboprop and other non-turbine-powered airplanes are found in the handling of the airplane’s powerplant and its associated systems, which are unique to gas turbine engines.

sections.title: Transition to Jet Airplanes
sections.summary: Apply jet engine principles, high-speed aerodynamics, jet systems and limitations, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Jet engine principles
lessons.concept: Air drawn into the engine for the gas generator is further compressed and constitutes the core airflow.
lessons.explanation: While a turbojet engine uses the entire gas generator’s output to produce thrust in the form of a high-velocity exhaust gas jet, the lower velocity and cooler bypass air produces some of the thrust produced by a turbofan engine. The turbofan engine design increases the thrust of the jet engine, particularly at lower speeds and altitudes. Although less efficient at higher altitudes, the turbofan engine increases acceleration, decreases the takeoff roll, improves initial climb performance, and often has the effect of decreasing fuel consumption.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how jet engine principles affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "Air drawn into the engine for the gas generator ___."
options: is further compressed and constitutes the core airflow
up over the upper surface causes a buffet
flare is also important
quickly transition to the required nose-down flight attitude in order to maintain airspeed
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Air drawn into the engine for the gas generator is further compressed and constitutes the core airflow.

lessons.title: High-speed aerodynamics
lessons.concept: At high-speed cruise, a shock wave that becomes too strong as the airflow speeds up over the upper surface causes a buffet.
lessons.explanation: All other things being equal, shock wave strength increases as the local airflow speed ahead of the shock wave increases. Mach buffet is a function of the speed of the airflow over the wing—not necessarily the forward speed of the airplane, and the shock wave strength, rather than a stall, creates the airflow separation. Mach buffet may result from two different conditions in cruise.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how high-speed aerodynamics affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about high-speed aerodynamics: "At high-speed cruise, a shock wave that becomes too strong as the airflow speeds ___."
options: up over the upper surface causes a buffet
maximum takeoff power available on all engines
into consideration when landing a jet airplane
thereafter maintain straight flight at the same speed with an angle of
practice.explanation: The cited page states: At high-speed cruise, a shock wave that becomes too strong as the airflow speeds up over the upper surface causes a buffet.

lessons.title: Jet systems and limitations
lessons.concept: It is essential that pilots understand not only the normal procedures and limitations of thrust reverser use, but also the procedures for coping with uncommanded reverse.
lessons.explanation: Idle reverse on a propeller produces a large amount of drag. On a jet engine, however, selecting idle reverse produces very little reverse thrust. In a jet airplane, the pilot should select reverse, apply reverse thrust as appropriate, and remain within any AFM limitations.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how jet systems and limitations affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "It is essential that pilots understand not only the normal procedures and limitations of thrust reverser use, but ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: flare is also important
also the procedures for coping with uncommanded reverse
into consideration when landing a jet airplane
UPRT Training Core Concepts Airplane upsets are by nature time-critical events
practice.explanation: The cited page states: It is essential that pilots understand not only the normal procedures and limitations of thrust reverser use, but also the procedures for coping with uncommanded reverse.

lessons.title: Performance and energy management
lessons.concept: Proper thrust management through the flare is also important.
lessons.explanation: It is essential that the flare maneuver be initiated at the proper height—not too high and not too low. Beginning the flare too high or reducing the thrust too early may result in the airplane floating beyond the target touchdown point or may include a rapid pitch up as the pilot attempts to prevent a high sink rate touchdown. The flare that is initiated too late may result in a hard touchdown.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how performance and energy management affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about performance and energy management: "Proper thrust management through the ___."
options: also the procedures for coping with uncommanded reverse

flare is also important
positive transfer of controls
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Proper thrust management through the flare is also important.

lessons.title: Jet takeoff and climb
lessons.concept: ⦁ Landing climb—the speed that guarantees adequate performance in arresting the descent and making a goaround from the final stages of landing with the airplane in the full landing configuration and maximum takeoff power available on all engines.
lessons.explanation: The speeds are as follows: ⦁ VSO—stall speed in the landing configuration. ⦁ VREF—1.3 times the stall speed in the landing configuration. ⦁ Approach climb—the speed that guarantees adequate performance in a go-around situation with an inoperative engine.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how jet takeoff and climb affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: ⦁ Landing climb—the speed that guarantees adequate performance in arresting the descent and making a goaround from the final stages of landing with the airplane in the full landing configuration and maximum takeoff power available on all engines.

lessons.title: Approach, landing, and rejected takeoff
lessons.concept: Landing Speeds As in the takeoff planning, there are certain speeds that should be taken into consideration when landing a jet airplane.
lessons.explanation: Certified landing field length requirements are computed for the stop made with speed brakes deployed and maximum wheel braking. Reverse thrust is not used in establishing the certified landing distances. However, reversers should definitely be used, if available.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how approach, landing, and rejected takeoff affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Transition to Light-Sport Airplanes
sections.summary: Apply light-sport categories and limitations, control response and low inertia, weight-shift-control aircraft, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Light-sport categories and limitations
lessons.concept: Light-Sport Airplane Synopsis • The airplane must meet the weight, speed, and other criteria listed in 14 CFR part 1, section 1 that define an LSA.
lessons.explanation: ASTM Industry Consensus Standards for LSA cover the following areas: • • • • • • • • • Design and performance Required equipment Quality assurance Production acceptance tests Aircraft operating instructions Maintenance and inspection procedures Identification and recording of major repairs and major alterations Continued airworthiness Manufacturer's assembly instructions (E-LSA aircraft) Using the ASTM Industry Consensus Standards, an LSA manufacturer can design and manufacture their aircraft and assess its compliance to the consensus standards. The manufacturer then, through evaluation services offered by a designated airworthiness representative, completes the process by submitting the required paperwork to the FAA. Upon approval, an LSA manufacturer is permitted to sell ready-to-fly S-LSA aircraft.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how light-sport categories and limitations affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on light-sport categories and limitations: "Light-Sport Airplane Synopsis • The airplane must meet the weight, speed, and ___, section 1 that define an LSA." What belongs in the blank?
options: the FAA released a rule that created a light-sport classification for airplane
other criteria listed in 14 CFR part 1
composite construction
thereafter maintain straight flight at the same speed with an angle of
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Light-Sport Airplane Synopsis • The airplane must meet the weight, speed, and other criteria listed in 14 CFR part 1, section 1 that define an LSA.

lessons.title: Control response and low inertia
lessons.concept: The key is to relax, have reasonable patience, and input only appropriate flight control pressures needed to get the required response.
lessons.explanation: Due to design requirements for low stall speeds, LSAs typically have low rotation and climb speeds with impressive climb rates. Like other airplanes, the pilot should be flying the published speeds as given in the airplane’s POH. Stick (yoke or stoke) forces tend to be light, which may lead a transitioning pilot to initially over-control.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how control response and low inertia affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: the FAA released a rule that created a light-sport classification for airplane

the appropriate propeller control toward the FEATHER position
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The key is to relax, have reasonable patience, and input only appropriate flight control pressures needed to get the required response.

lessons.title: Weight-shift-control aircraft
lessons.concept: In 2004, the FAA released a rule that created a light-sport classification for airplane, gyroplane, lighter-than-air, weight-shift-control, glider, and powered parachute.
lessons.explanation: A pilot seeking a transition into light-sport airplane flying should follow a systematic, structured light-sport airplane training course under the guidance of a competent instructor with recent experience in the specific training airplane. Light-Sport Aircraft Background Several groups were instrumental in the development and success of the LSA concept. These included the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), Light Aircraft Manufacturers Association, American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) International, and countless individuals who promoted the concept since the early 1990s.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how weight-shift-control aircraft affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

(VYSE) until short final with the landing assured
practice.explanation: The cited page states: In 2004, the FAA released a rule that created a light-sport classification for airplane, gyroplane, lighter-than-air, weight-shift-control, glider, and powered parachute.

lessons.title: Powered parachutes
lessons.concept: Chapter Summary LSAs are a category of small, lightweight aircraft that may include advanced systems, such a parachutes, EFIS, and composite construction.
lessons.explanation: Should a stall occur, decrease the aircraft’s pitch attitude rapidly in order to increase airspeed to allow for a recovery. Stalls that occur at low altitudes are especially dangerous because the closer to the ground the stall occurs, the less time there is to recover. For this reason, when climbing at a low altitude, excessive pitch attitude is discouraged.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how powered parachutes affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Gyroplanes and transition discipline
lessons.concept: In the event of an engine failure in an LSA, quickly transition to the required nose-down flight attitude in order to maintain airspeed.
lessons.explanation: Too low of an airspeed during the landing flare may lead to insufficient energy to arrest the descent and may result in a hard landing. Maintaining power during the approach will result in a reduced angle of attack and will extend the landing flare allowing more time to make adjustments to the aircraft during the landing. Always remember that rapid power reductions require an equally rapid reduction in pitch attitude to maintain airspeed.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how gyroplanes and transition discipline affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Emergency Procedures
sections.summary: Apply emergency priorities and checklists, engine failure and restart, fire, smoke, and electrical emergencies, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Emergency priorities and checklists
lessons.concept: Some light airplane emergency checklists direct the pilot to shut off the electrical master switch.
lessons.explanation: If the flames are snuffed out, no attempt should be made to restart the engine. If the engine compartment fire is oil-fed, as evidenced by thick black smoke, as opposed to a fuel-fed fire, which produces bright orange flames, the pilot should consider stopping the propeller rotation by feathering or other means, such as (with constant-speed propellers) placing the pitch control lever to the minimum rpm position and raising the nose to reduce airspeed until the propeller stops rotating. This procedure stops an engine-driven oil (or hydraulic) pump from continuing to pump the flammable fluid that is feeding the fire.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how emergency priorities and checklists affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

Emergency Approaches and Landings (Simulated) During dual training flights
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Some light airplane emergency checklists direct the pilot to shut off the electrical master switch.

lessons.title: Engine failure and restart
lessons.concept: When an engine failure occurs during the initial climb, the pilot should lower the nose of the airplane and establish the proper glide attitude.
lessons.explanation: Keep a retractable gear up unless the AFM/POH advises otherwise. A landing in snow should be executed like a ditching, in the same configuration and with the same regard for loss of depth perception (white out) in reduced visibility and on wide-open terrain. Engine Failure After Takeoff (Single-Engine) A number of variables and pilot actions factor into a successful emergency landing shortly after takeoff.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how engine failure and restart affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Fire, smoke, and electrical emergencies
lessons.concept: Selecting electrical switches that were ON before the fire indication one at a time, permitting a short time lapse after each switch is turned on to check for signs of odor, smoke, or sparks.
lessons.explanation: If electrical power is absolutely essential for the flight, an attempt may be made to identify and isolate the faulty circuit by: 1. Turning the electrical master switch OFF. Turning all individual electrical switches OFF.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how fire, smoke, and electrical emergencies affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Flight-control and system malfunctions
lessons.concept: Cabin Fire Cabin fires generally result from one of three sources: (1) careless smoking on the part of the pilot and/or passengers; (2) electrical system malfunctions; or (3) heating system malfunctions.
lessons.explanation: Selecting electrical switches that were ON before the fire indication one at a time, permitting a short time lapse after each switch is turned on to check for signs of odor, smoke, or sparks. This procedure, however, has the effect of recreating the original problem. The most prudent course of action is to land as soon as possible.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how flight-control and system malfunctions affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Forced landing planning and execution
lessons.concept: Pilots of powerless single-engine airplanes are left with no choice but to make a forced landing.
lessons.explanation: Pilots of twin-engine airplanes may elect to continue the flight to the nearest airport. However, consideration should be given to the possibility that a wing could be seriously impaired and lead to structural failure. Even a brief but intense fire could cause dangerous structural damage.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how forced landing planning and execution affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Post-crash survival and reporting
lessons.concept: [Figure 18-3] Deactivation of the airplane’s electrical system before touchdown reduces the likelihood of a post-crash fire.
lessons.explanation: However, this advantage has to be weighed against the possible side effects of a collapsing gear, such as a ruptured fuel tank. As always, the manufacturer’s recommendations as outlined in the AFM/POH should be followed. When a normal touchdown is assured, and ample stopping distance is available, a gear-up landing on level, but soft terrain or across a plowed field may result in less airplane damage than a gear-down landing.

glossary.definition: The consequences of the hazards associated with hand propping are serious to fatal.
Flight instructors face the challenge of teaching beginning pilots about attitude awareness; which requires understanding the motions of flight.
Straight-and-Level Flight Straight-and-level flight is flight in which heading and altitude are maintained.
All the factors that affect the airplane during level constant-altitude turns affect the airplane during climbing turns.
Viewing the Airplane as an Energy System The total mechanical energy of an airplane in flight is the sum of its potential energy from altitude and kinetic energy from airspeed.

Modifying a popular adage, the principle can be restated as “pitch plus power controls energy state.” This central principle serves to guide a set of general energy control rules to achieve and maintain any desired vertical flight path and airspeed targets within the airplane’s energy envelope.

Chapter 5: Maintaining Aircraft Control: Upset Prevention and Recovery Training Introduction Safe pilots prevent loss of control in flight (LOC-I), which is the leading cause of fatal general aviation accidents in the U.S.
These cues include aerodynamic buffet, loss of roll authority, or inability to arrest a descent.
For these reasons, it is important to understand factors and situations that can lead to a stall, and develop proficiency in stall recognition and recovery.

Prior to Takeoff Before going to the airplane, the pilot should check the POH/AFM performance charts to determine the predicted performance and decide if the airplane is capable of a safe takeoff and climb for the conditions and location.
Therefore, the pilot must be familiar with the principles and techniques involved in crosswind takeoffs, as well as those for normal takeoffs.

Initial Climb On short-field takeoffs, the landing gear and flaps should remain in takeoff position until the airplane is clear of obstacles (or as recommended by the manufacturer) and VY has been established.

modules.title: Aviation Weather Handbook
modules.description: Weather theory, aviation hazards, observations, analysis, advisories, forecasts, and tools.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.summary: Apply purpose and organization of aviation weather information, pilot responsibility for weather knowledge and decisions, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Purpose and organization of aviation weather information
lessons.concept: The information included is not prescriptive.
lessons.explanation: 1 Introduction This handbook describes the United States (U.S.) aviation weather program, products, and services. It also documents weather theory and its application to aviation. The objective of this handbook is to help the pilot and operator understand the basics of weather, aviation weather hazards, and aviation weather products.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how purpose and organization of aviation weather information affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Weather information dissemination and pilot reports
lessons.concept: En route controllers advise pilots of hazardous weather that may impact operations within 150 nautical miles (NM) of the controller’s assigned sector or jurisdiction and may solicit Pilot Weather Reports (PIREP) from pilots.
lessons.explanation: An ARTCC is a facility established to provide air traffic control (ATC) service to pilots operating on instrument flight rules (IFR) flight plans within controlled airspace, principally during the en route phase of flight. When equipment capabilities and controller workload permit, certain advisory/assistance services may be provided to pilots operating under visual flight rules (VFR). En route controllers become familiar with pertinent weather information and stay aware of current weather information needed to perform ATC duties.

lessons.explanation: Weather Information 3.3.1 Weather Briefings Prior to every flight, pilots should gather all information vital to the nature of the flight. This includes a weather briefing obtained by the pilot using online weather resources, a dispatcher, or Flight Service. Historically, Flight Service has been the primary source for obtaining preflight briefings.

lessons.title: Standard, abbreviated, and outlook briefings
lessons.concept: To obtain an appropriate weather briefing, pilots need to know which of the three types of briefings is needed for the flight—standard, abbreviated, or outlook.
lessons.explanation: Pilots may receive a regulatory compliant briefing without contacting Flight Service. Pilots who prefer to contact Flight Service are encouraged to conduct a self-briefing prior to calling. Conducting a self-briefing before contacting Flight Service provides familiarity of weather conditions applicable to the route of flight and promotes a better understanding of weather information.

sections.title: The Earth's Atmosphere
sections.summary: Apply atmospheric composition and air parcels, troposphere, stratosphere, and standard atmosphere, pressure, density, and temperature with altitude, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Atmospheric composition and air parcels
lessons.concept: In meteorology, an air parcel is used as a tool to describe certain atmospheric processes, and air parcels will be referred to in this handbook.
lessons.explanation: An air parcel is an imaginary volume of air to which any or all of the basic properties of atmospheric air may be assigned. A parcel is large enough to contain a very large number of molecules, but small enough so that the properties assigned to it are approximately uniform. It is not given precise numerical definition, but a cubic centimeter of air might fit well into most contexts where air parcels are discussed.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how atmospheric composition and air parcels affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

are known as the lower atmosphere
previously called Helicopter Emergency Medical Services HEMS) Tool
practice.explanation: The cited page states: In meteorology, an air parcel is used as a tool to describe certain atmospheric processes, and air parcels will be referred to in this handbook.

lessons.title: Troposphere, stratosphere, and standard atmosphere
lessons.concept: Both the tropopause and the troposphere are known as the lower atmosphere.
lessons.explanation: The height of the boundary layer varies depending on terrain and time of day and is directly affected by surface heating and cooling. It has an important role in transporting heat and moisture into the atmosphere. The transition boundary between the troposphere and the layer above is called the tropopause.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how troposphere, stratosphere, and standard atmosphere affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Pressure, density, and temperature with altitude
lessons.concept: Standard atmosphere is a hypothetical vertical distribution of atmospheric temperature, pressure, and density that, by international agreement, is taken to be representative of the atmosphere for purposes of pressure altimeter calibrations, aircraft performance calculations, aircraft and missile design, ballistic...
lessons.explanation: Please note that in this figure the temperature is not necessarily to scale with the height but provides an approximation of how the temperature changes with height. Vertical Structure of the Atmosphere 4.4 The Standard Atmosphere Continuous fluctuations of atmospheric properties create problems for engineers and meteorologists who need a fixed standard for reference. To solve this problem, they defined a standard atmosphere that represents an average of conditions throughout the atmosphere for all latitudes, seasons, and altitudes.

sections.title: Heat and Temperature
sections.summary: Apply temperature measurement and scales, heat transfer, daily and seasonal temperature variation, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Temperature measurement and scales
lessons.concept: 5.5.2 Temperature Scales Many scientists use the Kelvin (K) scale, a thermodynamic (absolute) temperature scale, where absolute zero, the theoretical absence of all thermal energy, is 0 K.
lessons.explanation: Temperature is an indicator of the internal energy of air. A thermometer is an instrument used to measure temperature. Higher temperatures correspond to higher molecular energies, while lower temperatures correspond to lower molecular energies.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how temperature measurement and scales affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on temperature measurement and scales: "5.5.2 Temperature Scales Many scientists use the Kelvin (K) scale, a thermodynamic (absolute) temperature scale, where absolute zero, ___, is 0 K." What belongs in the blank?
options: heat transfer and imbalances
transfer as a consequence of temperature difference
information at airports where an ASOS/AWOS has failed
the theoretical absence of all thermal energy
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 5.5.2 Temperature Scales Many scientists use the Kelvin (K) scale, a thermodynamic (absolute) temperature scale, where absolute zero, the theoretical absence of all thermal energy, is 0 K.

lessons.title: Heat transfer
lessons.concept: 5.6 Heat Transfer Heat transfer is energy transfer as a consequence of temperature difference.
lessons.explanation: When a physical body (e.g., an object or fluid) is at a different temperature than its surroundings or another body, transfer of thermal energy, also known as heat transfer (or heat exchange), occurs in such a way that the body and the surroundings reach thermal equilibrium (balance). Heat transfer always occurs from a hot body to a cold body. Where there is a temperature difference between objects in proximity, heat transfer between them can never be stopped; it can only be slowed down.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how heat transfer affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing heat transfer, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "5.6 Heat Transfer Heat transfer is energy ___."
options: the theoretical absence of all thermal energy
heat exchange should not be understated
transfer as a consequence of temperature difference
atmospheric circulations, weather, and ocean currents
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 5.6 Heat Transfer Heat transfer is energy transfer as a consequence of temperature difference.

lessons.title: Daily and seasonal temperature variation
lessons.concept: Water temperature changes occur to depths of 6 meters (m) (20 ft) or more on a daily basis, and 200 m to 600 m (650 ft to 1950 ft) annually.
lessons.explanation: Heat flow differences are another reason why water bodies warm up and cool down more slowly than land. Incoming solar radiation penetrates water to significant depths but can only heat the top skin layer of soil and rock. Also, since water is a fluid, its heat energy can be circulated through great volumes and depths via convection.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how daily and seasonal temperature variation affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Water temperature changes occur to depths of 6 meters (m) (20 ft) or more on a daily basis, and 200 m to 600 m (650 ft to 1950 ft) annually.

lessons.title: Temperature effects on aircraft performance
lessons.concept: As a foundation for the study of temperature effects on aviation and weather, this chapter describes temperature, temperature measurement, and heat transfer and imbalances.
lessons.explanation: Air temperature varies with time from one season to the next, between day and night, and even from one hour to the next. Air temperature also varies from one location to another, from high altitudes and latitudes to low altitudes and latitudes. Temperature can be critical to some flight operations.

lessons.title: Hydrologic cycle and phase changes
lessons.concept: Evaporation of this runoff into the atmosphere begins the hydrologic cycle over again.
lessons.explanation: 6.2.7 Precipitation Precipitation results when tiny condensation particles grow in the atmosphere through collision and coalescence and then fall to the Earth’s surface. 6.2.8 Runoff Runoff occurs when there is excessive precipitation and the ground is saturated (i.e., cannot absorb any more water). This runoff flows into streams and rivers and eventually back into the sea.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how hydrologic cycle and phase changes affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

begins the hydrologic cycle over again
that fall from the atmosphere and reach the ground
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Evaporation of this runoff into the atmosphere begins the hydrologic cycle over again.

lessons.title: Humidity, saturation, and dewpoint
lessons.concept: Relative humidity can be confusing because it does not indicate the actual water vapor content of the air, but rather how close the air is to saturation.
lessons.explanation: 6.4 Relative Humidity Relative humidity is the ratio, usually expressed as a percentage, of water vapor actually in the air parcel compared to the amount of water vapor the air parcel could hold at a particular temperature and pressure. 𝑅𝑒𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒 𝐻𝑢𝑚𝑖𝑑𝑖𝑡𝑦 = 𝑊𝑎𝑡𝑒𝑟 𝑣𝑎𝑝𝑜𝑟 𝑐𝑜𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝑊𝑎𝑡𝑒𝑟 𝑣𝑎𝑝𝑜𝑟 𝑐𝑎𝑝𝑎𝑐𝑖𝑡𝑦 While relative humidity is the most common method of describing atmospheric moisture, it is also the most misunderstood. An air parcel with 100 percent relative humidity is saturated, while an air parcel with relative humidity less than 100 percent is unsaturated.

lessons.explanation: An average hurricane releases 52 million trillion (5.2 x 1019) joules per day as water vapor condenses into clouds and precipitation. This is equivalent to about 40 times the total worldwide energy consumption per day in 2005!

sections.title: Earth-Atmosphere Heat Imbalances
sections.summary: Apply unequal solar heating, radiation budget and greenhouse effects, seasonal and geographic energy transport, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Unequal solar heating
lessons.concept: 7 Earth-Atmosphere Heat Imbalances 7.1 Introduction Weather is not a capricious act of nature but, rather, the atmosphere’s response to unequal rates of radiational heating and cooling across the surface of the Earth and within its atmosphere.
lessons.explanation: The absorption of incoming solar radiation causes heating, while the emission of outgoing terrestrial radiation causes cooling. However, imbalances in the rate of heating and cooling create temperature gradients.2 Atmospheric circulations and weather are the atmosphere’s never-ending attempt to redistribute this heat and achieve equilibrium. This chapter provides a background on the interaction between the Earth and the atmosphere for a better understanding of the weather that will affect flight operations.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how unequal solar heating affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: A learner is recalling the handbook wording on unequal solar heating: "The absorption of incoming solar radiation causes heating, ___." What belongs in the blank?
options: the sky is overcast (see Figure 7-2)
atmospheric circulations, weather, and ocean currents
influxes of solar particle and electromagnetic radiation
while the emission of outgoing terrestrial radiation causes cooling
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The absorption of incoming solar radiation causes heating, while the emission of outgoing terrestrial radiation causes cooling.

lessons.title: Radiation budget and greenhouse effects
lessons.concept: Greenhouse warming is enhanced during nights when the sky is overcast (see Figure 7-2).
lessons.explanation: Heat energy from the Earth can be trapped by clouds, leading to higher temperatures as compared to nights with clear skies. The air is not allowed to cool as much with overcast skies. Under partly cloudy skies, some heat is allowed to escape, and some remains trapped.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how radiation budget and greenhouse effects affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing radiation budget and greenhouse effects, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "Greenhouse warming is enhanced during nights when ___."
options: while the emission of outgoing terrestrial radiation causes cooling
the sky is overcast (see Figure 7-2)

influxes of solar particle and electromagnetic radiation
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Greenhouse warming is enhanced during nights when the sky is overcast (see Figure 7-2).

lessons.title: Seasonal and geographic energy transport
lessons.concept: This poleward heat transport is accomplished by atmospheric circulations, weather, and ocean currents.
lessons.explanation: must be transported polar by some mechanism(s). 7.5 Seasons Seasons are caused by the tilt of the Earth’s rotational axis as the Earth orbits the Sun (see Figure 7-6). The Earth’s rotational axis is tilted by 23½° from the perpendicular drawn to the plane of the Earth’s orbit about the Sun and points the same direction in space all year long.

sections.title: Atmospheric Pressure and Altimetry
sections.summary: Apply pressure measurement and station pressure, sea-level pressure and pressure systems, pressure altitude and density altitude, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Pressure measurement and station pressure
lessons.concept: Engineering 8.2.3 Station Pressure The pressure measured at an airport is called station pressure, or the actual pressure at field elevation.
lessons.explanation: Units of Pressure Units of Pressure Standard Atmosphere Value at Sea Level Common Use Hectopascals (hPa) 1013.2 hPa METAR/SPECI Millibars (mb or mbar) 1013.2 mb U.S. Weather Charts Inches of mercury (inHg or Hg) 29.92 inHg U.S. Aviation Pounds per square inch (psi) 14.7 psi U.S.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pressure measurement and station pressure affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Choose the phrase omitted from this handbook statement: "The pressure measured at an airport is called station pressure, or ___."
options: people cannot readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes
of altitude rather than units of pressure
website that includes overlay of forecast cloud bases
the actual pressure at field elevation
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The pressure measured at an airport is called station pressure, or the actual pressure at field elevation.

lessons.title: Sea-level pressure and pressure systems
lessons.concept: Temperature’s Effect on Pressure 8.2.5 Sea Level Pressure Since pressure varies greatly with altitude, people cannot readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes.
lessons.explanation: Vertical expansion of the warm column has made it taller than the column at standard temperature. Contraction of the cold column has made it shorter than the standard column. Since the total pressure decrease is the same in each column, the rate of decrease of pressure with height in warm air is less than standard, while the rate of decrease in pressure with height in cold air is greater than standard.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how sea-level pressure and pressure systems affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about sea-level pressure and pressure systems: "Temperature’s Effect on Pressure 8.2.5 Sea Level Pressure Since pressure varies greatly with altitude, ___."
options: Since existing conditions in a real atmosphere are seldom standard
people cannot readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes
density altitude is 12
Standard atmosphere is a hypothetical vertical distribution of atmospheric temperature
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Temperature’s Effect on Pressure 8.2.5 Sea Level Pressure Since pressure varies greatly with altitude, people cannot readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes.

lessons.title: Pressure altitude and density altitude
lessons.concept: The altimeter is graduated to read increments of altitude rather than units of pressure.
lessons.explanation: Assuming constant pressure, temperature, and volume, air with a greater amount of water vapor is less dense than air with a lesser amount of water vapor. This is because dry air molecules have a larger mass (weight) than water vapor molecules, and density is directly related to mass (see Figure 8-11). Water Vapor’s Effects on Density 8.4 Altimetry The altimeter is essentially an aneroid barometer.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pressure altitude and density altitude affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: density altitude is 12
people cannot readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes
Standard atmosphere is a hypothetical vertical distribution of atmospheric temperature
of altitude rather than units of pressure
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The altimeter is graduated to read increments of altitude rather than units of pressure.

lessons.title: Altimeter settings and errors
lessons.concept: Since existing conditions in a real atmosphere are seldom standard, altitude indications on the altimeter are seldom actual or true altitudes.
lessons.explanation: 8.4.1 Altitude Altitude seems like a simple term; it means the vertical elevation of an object above the surface of the Earth. However, in aviation, it can have many meanings. True altitude is the actual vertical distance above MSL.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how altimeter settings and errors affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Since existing conditions in a real atmosphere are seldom standard, altitude indications on the altimeter are seldom actual or true altitudes.

lessons.title: Pressure tendency and operational interpretation
lessons.concept: For example, if temperature at a pressure altitude of 10,000 ft is 20 °C, density altitude is 12,700 ft.
lessons.explanation: High Density Altitude’s Effects on Flight High density altitude also can be a problem at cruising altitude. When air temperature is higher (warmer) than standard atmosphere, the higher density altitude lowers the service ceiling. A pilot’s aircraft will perform as though it were at 12,700 ft indicated with a normal temperature of -8 °C.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: For example, if temperature at a pressure altitude of 10,000 ft is 20 °C, density altitude is 12,700 ft.

sections.title: Global Circulations and Jet Streams
sections.summary: Apply global pressure belts and circulation cells, coriolis force and prevailing winds, jet streams and flight planning, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Global pressure belts and circulation cells
lessons.concept: Earth Circulation System Between each of these circulation cells are bands of high and low pressure at the surface.
lessons.explanation: The high-pressure band is located about 30° N/S latitude and at each pole. Low-pressure bands are found at the Equator and 50° to 60° N/S latitude. Usually, fair and dry/hot weather is associated with high pressure, and rainy and stormy weather is associated with low pressure.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how global pressure belts and circulation cells affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Restore the missing source detail about global pressure belts and circulation cells: "Earth Circulation System Between each of these circulation cells are bands ___."
options: the flow often meanders southward and northward in waves

circulation patterns that overpower the trade wind circulation
of high and low pressure at the surface
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Earth Circulation System Between each of these circulation cells are bands of high and low pressure at the surface.

lessons.title: Coriolis force and prevailing winds
lessons.concept: The winds blow from west to east in jet streams, but the flow often meanders southward and northward in waves.
lessons.explanation: Consider the number of deserts located along the 30° N/S latitude around the world compared to the region between 50°–60° N/S latitude. The higher latitudes, especially near the west coast of continents, tend to have more precipitation due to more storms moving around the Earth at these latitudes. 9.4 Jet Streams Jet streams are relatively narrow bands of strong wind in the upper levels of the atmosphere.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how coriolis force and prevailing winds affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.
