of high and low pressure at the surface
Wind Three primary forces affect the flow of wind
practice.explanation: The cited page states: The winds blow from west to east in jet streams, but the flow often meanders southward and northward in waves.

lessons.title: Jet streams and flight planning
lessons.concept: In reality, jet streams are typically much wider.
lessons.explanation: Jet Stream Wind Speeds The jet stream is often indicated by a line on maps and is displayed this way by television meteorologists. The line generally points to the location of the strongest wind (see Figure 9-6). A jet stream is a region where winds increase toward a core of highest speed, rather than just a distinct location.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how jet streams and flight planning affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Wind
sections.summary: Apply pressure-gradient, coriolis, and friction forces, geostrophic and gradient wind, local sea, lake, valley, and mountain breezes, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Pressure-gradient, Coriolis, and friction forces
lessons.concept: Wind Three primary forces affect the flow of wind: Pressure Gradient Force (PGF), Coriolis force, and friction.
lessons.explanation: The four primary cardinal directions are north (N), south (S), east (E), and west (W). There are also four intermediate directions, such as northeast (NE), northwest (NW), southeast (SE), and southwest (SW). Additionally, there are eight subdivisions, including north-northeast (NNE), north-northwest (NNW), south-southeast (SSE), south-southwest (SSW), east-northeast (ENE), east-southeast (ESE), west-southwest (WSW), and west-northwest (WNW).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pressure-gradient, coriolis, and friction forces affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "___: Pressure Gradient Force (PGF), Coriolis force, and friction." Which detail completes the statement?
options: which itself is directly proportional to the contour/isobar gradient
variable wind
the flow often meanders southward and northward in waves
Wind Three primary forces affect the flow of wind
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Wind Three primary forces affect the flow of wind: Pressure Gradient Force (PGF), Coriolis force, and friction.

lessons.title: Geostrophic and gradient wind
lessons.concept: Direction of Pressure Gradient Force Wind speed is directly proportional to the PGF, which itself is directly proportional to the contour/isobar gradient.
lessons.explanation: PGF is directed from higher height/pressure to lower height/pressure and is perpendicular to contours/isobars. Whenever a pressure difference develops over an area, the PGF begins moving the air directly across the contours/isobars. PGF is directed across contours/isobars towards lower height/pressure.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how geostrophic and gradient wind affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Direction of Pressure Gradient Force Wind speed is directly proportional to the PGF, ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: which itself is directly proportional to the contour/isobar gradient
Wind Three primary forces affect the flow of wind
land breeze
of high and low pressure at the surface
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Direction of Pressure Gradient Force Wind speed is directly proportional to the PGF, which itself is directly proportional to the contour/isobar gradient.

lessons.title: Local sea, lake, valley, and mountain breezes
lessons.concept: Local winds include sea breeze, land breeze, lake breeze, lake effect, valley breeze, mountain-plains wind circulation, and mountain breeze.
lessons.explanation: Air rises over the warmer surface and sinks over the cooler surface. A local wind circulation is easiest to identify when synoptic-scale wind patterns are weak. A sea breeze (see Figure 10-13) is a coastal local wind that blows from sea to land and is caused by temperature differences when the sea surface is colder than the adjacent land.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how local sea, lake, valley, and mountain breezes affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

land breeze
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Local winds include sea breeze, land breeze, lake breeze, lake effect, valley breeze, mountain-plains wind circulation, and mountain breeze.

lessons.title: Wind shear, gusts, and sudden shifts
lessons.concept: Adverse winds include crosswinds, gusts, tailwind, variable wind, sudden wind shift, wind shear, and mountain wind hazards.
lessons.explanation: Winds cause the formation, dissipation, and redistribution of weather. Winds also affect aircraft during all phases of flight. Adverse wind is a category of hazardous aviation weather that is responsible for many weather-related accidents.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wind shear, gusts, and sudden shifts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Crosswind components and operational effects
lessons.concept: A crosswind is a wind that has a component directed perpendicularly to the heading of an aircraft (see Figure 10-22).
lessons.explanation: Mountain Breeze Surface wind flows from the mountain down the sloping terrain into the valley. Air rises over the valley and sinks over the sloping terrain. The potential of drift produced by crosswind is critical to air navigation and can have its biggest impact during takeoff and landing.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how crosswind components and operational effects affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Air Masses, Fronts, and Wave Cyclones
sections.summary: Apply air-mass source regions and modification, warm, cold, stationary, and occluded fronts, wave cyclone development, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Air-mass source regions and modification
lessons.concept: Air mass source regions range from extensive snow-covered polar areas to deserts to tropical oceans.
lessons.explanation: The area from which an air mass originates is called a source region. The United States is not a favorable source region because of the relatively frequent passage of weather disturbances that disrupt any opportunity for an air mass to stagnate and take on the properties of the underlying region. The longer the air mass stays over its source region, the more likely it will acquire the properties of the surface below.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how air-mass source regions and modification affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Fill the blank using the cited content on air-mass source regions and modification: "Air mass source regions range from extensive snow-covered ___."
options: Clouds and precipitation can occur in the areas of frontal lift along
11.3.4 Occluded Front Cold fronts typically move faster than warm fronts
Large masses of air stagnate over inland continental areas
polar areas to deserts to tropical oceans
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Air mass source regions range from extensive snow-covered polar areas to deserts to tropical oceans.

lessons.title: Warm, cold, stationary, and occluded fronts
lessons.concept: 11.3.4 Occluded Front Cold fronts typically move faster than warm fronts, so in time they catch up to warm fronts.
lessons.explanation: As the two fronts merge, an occluded front forms (see Figure 11-8). At the occluded front, the cold air undercuts the retreating cooler air mass associated with the warm front, further lifting the already rising warm air. Clouds and precipitation can occur in the areas of frontal lift along, ahead of, and behind the surface position of an occluded front.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how warm, cold, stationary, and occluded fronts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

may form along or ahead of the front
11.3.4 Occluded Front Cold fronts typically move faster than warm fronts
freezing rain and/or freezing drizzle below a front
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 11.3.4 Occluded Front Cold fronts typically move faster than warm fronts, so in time they catch up to warm fronts.

lessons.title: Wave cyclone development
lessons.concept: Chapter 11, Air Masses, Fronts, and the Wave Cyclone Model 11-6
lessons.explanation: A continuous line of thunderstorms, or squall line, may form along or ahead of the front. Squall lines present a serious hazard to pilots as squall-type thunderstorms are intense and move quickly. Behind a fast-moving cold front, the skies usually clear rapidly, and the front leaves behind gusty, turbulent winds and colder temperatures.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how wave cyclone development affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Vertical Motion and Clouds
sections.summary: Apply convection, convergence, orographic, and frontal lift, adiabatic cooling and cloud formation, cloud forms and height families, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Convection, convergence, orographic, and frontal lift
lessons.concept: Four of the most common types of vertical motion are orographic effects, frictional effects, frontal lift, and buoyancy.
lessons.explanation: Air Parcel Vertical Motion Characteristics Unsaturated Parcel Saturated Temperature Change Dewpoint Change Relative Humidity Temperature Change Dewpoint Change Relative Humidity Ascending (rising) -3°C/1,000 ft -0.5°C/1,000 ft Increases -1.2°C to -3°C/1,000 ft Identical to temperature change 100% Descending (subsiding) +3°C/1,000 ft +0.5°C/1,000 ft Decreases 12.4 Common Sources of Vertical Motion There are many sources of vertical motion in the atmosphere. 12.4.1 Orographic Effects Winds blowing across mountains and valleys cause the moving air to alternately ascend and descend. If relief is sufficiently great, the resulting expansional cooling and compressional warming of air affects the development and dissipation of clouds and precipitation.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how convection, convergence, orographic, and frontal lift affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Adiabatic cooling and cloud formation
lessons.concept: Further lifting of the saturated parcel results in condensation, cloud formation, and latent heat release.
lessons.explanation: The Lifted Condensation Level (LCL) is the level at which a parcel of moist air lifted dry adiabatically becomes saturated. At this altitude, the temperature-dewpoint spread is zero and relative humidity is 100 percent. Because the heat added during condensation offsets some of the cooling due to expansion, the parcel now cools at the moist adiabatic lapse rate, which varies between approximately 1.2°C per 1,000 ft (4°C per km) for very warm saturated parcels to 3°C per 1,000 ft (9.8°C per km) for very cold saturated parcels.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how adiabatic cooling and cloud formation affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Further lifting of the saturated parcel results in condensation, cloud formation, and latent heat release.

lessons.title: Cloud forms and height families
lessons.concept: There are four basic cloud forms (appearances) observed in the Earth’s atmosphere (see Table 12-2).
lessons.explanation: See Appendix A, Cloud Types, for cloud types. Cloud Forms Cirri-form High-level clouds that form above 20,000 ft (6,000 m) and are usually composed of ice crystals. High-level clouds are typically thin and white in appearance but can create an array of colors when the Sun is low on the horizon.

sections.title: Atmospheric Stability
sections.summary: Apply parcel method and lapse rates, stable and unstable air, temperature inversions, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Parcel method and lapse rates
lessons.concept: This final case is an example of an unstable parcel.
lessons.explanation: If the lifted parcel is the same temperature as the surrounding air, it will be the same density and remain at the same level. In this case, the parcel is neutrally stable. If the lifted parcel is warmer and, therefore, less dense (lighter) than the surrounding air, it will continue to rise on its own until it reaches the same temperature as its environment.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how parcel method and lapse rates affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Which source-grounded fact about parcel method and lapse rates is correct?
options: An air parcel lifted upward would be initially stable
This final case is an example of an unstable parcel.
the atmosphere by changing the temperature lapse rate
ft due to vertical visibility into fog
practice.explanation: The cited page states: This final case is an example of an unstable parcel.

lessons.title: Stable and unstable air
lessons.concept: An air parcel lifted upward would be initially stable, but at some point, above its LCL, it would become unstable.
lessons.explanation: Absolute Instability Example 13.3.4 Conditional Instability Conditional instability (see Figure 13-4) is the state of a column of unsaturated air in the atmosphere when its lapse rate of temperature is less than the dry adiabatic lapse rate but greater than the moist adiabatic lapse rate. The term “conditional” means the parcel must be lifted to a particular level (altitude) before it becomes unstable and rises because of its own buoyancy. The Level of Free Convection (LFC) is the level at which a parcel of air lifted dry adiabatically until saturated and moist adiabatically thereafter would first become warmer than the surrounding environmental air (i.e., unstable) in a conditionally unstable column of air in the atmosphere.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how stable and unstable air affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "___, but at some point, above its LCL, it would become unstable." Which detail completes the statement?
options: This final case is an example of an unstable parcel.
will typically underestimate the amount of instability present
layer of below-freezing air based at the surface
An air parcel lifted upward would be initially stable
practice.explanation: The cited page states: An air parcel lifted upward would be initially stable, but at some point, above its LCL, it would become unstable.

lessons.title: Temperature inversions
lessons.concept: Wind can act to change the stability of a column of air in the atmosphere by changing the temperature lapse rate.
lessons.explanation: Stability increases when wind blows colder air into the bottom of the air column (cold air advection) and/or warmer air at the top (warm air advection). Conversely, stability decreases when wind blows warmer air into the bottom of the air column and/or colder air at the top. A column of air in the atmosphere will become more stable when it descends (subsides) (see Figure 13-7).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how temperature inversions affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: The cited page states, "Wind can act to change the stability of a column of air in ___." Which detail completes the statement?
options: will typically underestimate the amount of instability present
This final case is an example of an unstable parcel.
the atmosphere by changing the temperature lapse rate
circulation patterns that overpower the trade wind circulation
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Wind can act to change the stability of a column of air in the atmosphere by changing the temperature lapse rate.

lessons.title: Stability indices and aviation impacts
lessons.concept: In cases of elevated convection, stability indices based on near-surface measurements (such as the LI) will typically underestimate the amount of instability present.
lessons.explanation: 13.6.1 Surface-Based Convection Surface-based convection occurs within a surface-based layer (i.e., a layer in which the lowest portion is based at or very near the Earth’s surface) and is primarily generated by the daily heating of the surface of the Earth by the energy from the Sun. 13.6.2 Elevated Convection Elevated convection occurs within an elevated layer (i.e., a layer in which the lowest portion is based above the Earth’s surface). Elevated convection often occurs when air near the ground is relatively cool and stable (e.g., during periods of isentropic lift, when an unstable layer of air is present aloft, etc.).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how stability indices and aviation impacts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Precipitation
sections.summary: Apply precipitation growth processes, rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain, precipitation intensity and flight visibility, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Precipitation growth processes
lessons.concept: Two growth processes exist that allow cloud droplets (or ice crystals) to grow large enough to reach the ground as precipitation before they evaporate (or sublimate).
lessons.explanation: Because of their microscopic size, the rate at which cloud droplets fall is incredibly slow. An average cloud droplet falling from a cloud base at 3,300 ft (1,000 m) would require about 48 hours to reach the ground. It would never complete this journey, because it would evaporate within minutes after falling below the cloud base.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how precipitation growth processes affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

that fall from the atmosphere and reach the ground
ground as precipitation before they evaporate (or sublimate)
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Two growth processes exist that allow cloud droplets (or ice crystals) to grow large enough to reach the ground as precipitation before they evaporate (or sublimate).

lessons.title: Rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain
lessons.concept: Snow Temperature Environment 14.4.2 Ice Pellets Ice pellets (sleet) occur when there is a shallow layer aloft with above-freezing temperatures and with a deep layer of below-freezing air based at the surface.
lessons.explanation: As snow falls into the shallow warm layer, the snowflakes partially melt. As the precipitation reenters air that is below freezing, it refreezes into ice pellets (see Figure 14-3). Ice Pellets Temperature Environment 14.4.3 Freezing Rain Freezing rain occurs when there is a deep layer aloft with above-freezing temperatures and with a shallow layer of below-freezing air at the surface.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Precipitation intensity and flight visibility
lessons.concept: When arriving or departing from an airport reporting precipitation of light or greater intensity, expect clouds to be more than 4,000 ft thick.
lessons.explanation: 14.2 Necessary Ingredients for Formation Precipitation formation requires three ingredients: water vapor, sufficient lift to condense the water vapor into clouds, and a growth process that allows cloud droplets to grow large and heavy enough to fall as precipitation. Significant precipitation usually requires clouds to be at least 4,000 ft thick. The heavier the precipitation, the thicker the clouds are likely to be.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how precipitation intensity and flight visibility affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Weather Radar
sections.summary: Apply radar principles and reflectivity, nexrad products and mosaics, airborne radar operation, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Radar principles and reflectivity
lessons.concept: It is essential to understand some principles of weather radar.
lessons.explanation: Weather Radar The radar used by the NWS is called the Weather Surveillance Radar—1988 Doppler (WSR-88D). The prototype radar was built in 1988. This will allow correct interpretation of WSR-88D images.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how radar principles and reflectivity affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: NEXRAD products and mosaics
lessons.concept: Undershooting may occur at and near the radar site even in mosaic products when the precipitation is above the highest elevation angle.
lessons.explanation: Radar overshooting occurs more often in the mountainous western United States where some radars are located on a mountaintop (e.g., the WSR-88D near Cedar City, UT). Radar undershooting occurs when precipitation occurs above the lowest radar beam, usually with high-cloud-based precipitation near the radar site. This often occurs in the western United States during the summer months.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how nexrad products and mosaics affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Undershooting may occur at and near the radar site even in mosaic products when the precipitation is above the highest elevation angle.

lessons.title: Airborne radar operation
lessons.concept: Precipitation Attenuation versus Wavelength As the wavelength of the radar decreases, the amount of precipitation attenuation increases.
lessons.explanation: The WSR-88D’s 10-cm wavelength is not significantly attenuated by precipitation. However, aircraft radars, which typically have 3-cm wavelengths, have a significant precipitation attenuation problem. As a result, aircraft weather radar typically only shows the leading edge of extreme intensity echoes.

lessons.explanation: Hazards associated with stratiform precipitation include possible widespread icing above the freezing level, low ceilings, and reduced visibilities. sections.title: Mountain Weather
sections.summary: Apply mountain waves and rotor clouds, orographic clouds and precipitation, downslope winds and turbulence, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Mountain waves and rotor clouds
lessons.concept: The mountain-induced flow fields to be discussed in this section are frequently accompanied by visual indicators (such as lenticular and rotor clouds or blowing dust).
lessons.explanation: It also is possible to have hybrid or intermediate forms (i.e., waves that are only partially trapped). The most severe mountain wind events usually occur when the large-scale (or synoptic) winds are strongest, from late autumn to early spring. During the remainder of the year, when the synoptic winds are normally much weaker, hazardous winds in the vicinity of mountains are more likely to be associated with thunderstorms and their outflow fields.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how mountain waves and rotor clouds affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Orographic clouds and precipitation
lessons.concept: Wind Fields Figure 16-15 provides a schematic of mountain waves and possible associated clouds.
lessons.explanation: 16.2.6.5 Other Phenomena In addition to the vortex phenomena previously discussed, vortices or strong shear zones may be generated locally by strong flow past individual mountain peaks and crags, or through gaps and passes across mountain ranges. The point is that strong wind flow in the vicinity of irregular terrain can produce a multitude of disturbances of varying size and strength, many without reliable visual indicators. Their presence should be suspected when flying downwind of rugged terrain, whenever the wind flow at ridge level exceeds about 20 kt.

lessons.concept: In this case, strong downslope winds on the lee slopes can reach 100 kt gusts, creating a low-level turbulence hazard for all aircraft.
lessons.explanation: (Source: Durran and Klemp, 1983)4 Figure 16-5. Schematic of a Vertically Propagating Mountain Wave Often accompanying these high-altitude effects is the occurrence of very strong surface winds that result from the wave breaking aloft. Further, these extremely strong low-level winds often abruptly terminate in a “jump” located some distance down the lee slope or well to the lee of the mountains themselves.

lessons.title: Mountain obscuration and density altitude
lessons.concept: This amplification is a consequence of the normal decrease in air density with altitude.
lessons.explanation: This vertical propagation of the wave means that the effects of the mountain range can be felt at heights significantly above the actual altitude of the peaks (at times reaching in excess of 60,000 ft). As a result, aircraft flying at virtually any altitude may have to deal with significant turbulence and wave-induced altitude excursions. In fact, the amplitude of this type of wave actually increases with height above the mountain (in the absence of atmospheric features, such as strong inversions or shear layers that would tend to partially reflect or absorb the upward-moving wave energy).

sections.title: Tropical Weather
sections.summary: Apply tropical circulation and convergence, tropical waves and cyclones, hurricane structure and hazards, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Tropical circulation and convergence
lessons.concept: In some areas of the world, seasonal temperature differences between land and water areas generate rather large circulation patterns that overpower the trade wind circulation; these areas are monsoon regions.
lessons.explanation: 17.2 Circulation Chapter 7, Earth-Atmosphere Heat Imbalances, stated that wind blowing out of the subtropical high-pressure belts toward the Equator form the northeast and southeast trade winds of the two hemispheres. These trade winds converge in the vicinity of the Equator where air rises. This convergence zone is referred to as the Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how tropical circulation and convergence affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: In some areas of the world, seasonal temperature differences between land and water areas generate rather large circulation patterns that overpower the trade wind circulation; these areas are monsoon regions.

lessons.title: Tropical waves and cyclones
lessons.concept: 17.3.5 Tropical Cyclones “Tropical cyclone” is a general term for any low that originates over tropical oceans.
lessons.explanation: Tropical cyclones are classified according to their intensity based on the average wind speeds. Wind gusts in these storms may be as much as 50 percent higher than the average wind speeds. Over the north Atlantic and northeast Pacific Oceans, tropical cyclone classifications are: 1.

lessons.title: Hurricane structure and hazards
lessons.concept: Poorly constructed frame homes can sustain complete collapse of all walls as well as the loss of the roof structure.
lessons.explanation: Windborne debris damage will occur to nearly all unprotected windows and many protected windows. 130–156 mph 113–136 kt 209–251 km/h Nearly all older (pre-1994 construction) mobile homes will be destroyed. A high percentage of newer mobile homes also will be destroyed.

lessons.concept: When a storm takes on middle latitude characteristics, it is said to be extratropical, meaning “outside the Tropics.” Tropical cyclones produce weather conditions that differ somewhat from those produced by their higher latitude cousins and invite investigation.
lessons.explanation: 17.3.5.4 Weather in a Tropical Depression While in its initial developing stage, the cyclone is characterized by a circular area of broken-to-overcast clouds in multiple layers. sections.title: Weather and Obstructions to Visibility
sections.summary: Apply radiation, advection, upslope, and steam fog, haze, smoke, dust, and blowing snow, precipitation restrictions, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Radiation, advection, upslope, and steam fog
lessons.concept: This may occur through cooling of the air to its dewpoint (producing radiation fog, advection fog, or upslope fog), or by adding moisture and thereby elevating the dewpoint (producing frontal fog or steam fog).
lessons.explanation: When fog is composed of ice crystals, it is termed ice fog. If fog is so shallow that it is not an obstruction to vision at a height of 6 ft (2 m) above the surface, it is called shallow (ground) fog. Fog forms when the temperature and dewpoint of the air become identical (or nearly so).

lessons.title: Precipitation restrictions
lessons.concept: 18.1.5 Precipitation Precipitation is any of the forms of water particles, whether liquid or solid, that fall from the atmosphere and reach the ground.
lessons.explanation: Fog evaporates, but haze and smoke must be dispersed by the movement of air. A thick layer of clouds above haze or smoke may block sunlight, preventing dissipation. Visibility will improve little, if any, during the day.

lessons.concept: Flight visibility may approach zero in stratus clouds.
lessons.explanation: An observer on a mountain in a stratus layer would call it fog. Stratus and fog frequently exist together. In many cases, there is no real line of distinction between the fog and stratus; rather, one gradually merges into the other.

sections.title: Turbulence
sections.summary: Apply convective and mechanical turbulence, clear-air turbulence, low-level wind shear, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Convective and mechanical turbulence
lessons.concept: 19.2 Causes of Turbulence Turbulence is caused by convective currents (called convective turbulence), obstructions in the wind flow (called mechanical turbulence), and wind shear.
lessons.explanation: 19.2.1 Convective Turbulence Convective turbulence is turbulent vertical motions that result from convective currents and the subsequent rising and sinking of air. For every rising current, there is a compensating downward current. The downward currents frequently occur over broader areas than do the upward currents; therefore, they have a slower vertical speed than do the rising currents.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how convective and mechanical turbulence affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: cloudless regions that causes violent buffeting of aircraft
obstructions in the wind flow (called mechanical turbulence)
even though there may be no lenticular clouds to identify the condition
non-convective low-level turbulence are depicted with black dots
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 19.2 Causes of Turbulence Turbulence is caused by convective currents (called convective turbulence), obstructions in the wind flow (called mechanical turbulence), and wind shear.

lessons.title: Clear-air turbulence
lessons.concept: Wind Shear Turbulence Associated with a Temperature Inversion 19.2.3.2 Clear-Air Turbulence (CAT) CAT is defined as sudden severe turbulence occurring in cloudless regions that causes violent buffeting of aircraft.
lessons.explanation: CAT is a higher altitude turbulence (normally above 15,000 ft) particularly between the core of a jet stream and the surrounding air. This includes turbulence in cirrus clouds, within and in the vicinity of standing lenticular clouds and, in some cases, in clear air in the vicinity of thunderstorms. Generally, though, CAT definitions exclude turbulence caused by thunderstorms, low-altitude temperature inversions, thermals, strong surface winds, or local terrain features.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how clear-air turbulence affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: obstructions in the wind flow (called mechanical turbulence)
at upper levels near jet steams or near the ground due to convection)
circulation patterns that overpower the trade wind circulation
cloudless regions that causes violent buffeting of aircraft
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Wind Shear Turbulence Associated with a Temperature Inversion 19.2.3.2 Clear-Air Turbulence (CAT) CAT is defined as sudden severe turbulence occurring in cloudless regions that causes violent buffeting of aircraft.

lessons.title: Low-level wind shear
lessons.concept: Wind Shear Example It is important to remember that wind shear can affect any flight at any altitude (e.g., at upper levels near jet steams or near the ground due to convection).
lessons.explanation: Wind shear can subject an aircraft to violent updrafts and downdrafts, as well as abrupt changes to the horizontal movement of the aircraft. While wind shear may be reported, it often remains undetected and is a silent aviation weather hazard. Always be alert to the possibility of wind shear, especially when flying in and around thunderstorms and frontal systems.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how low-level wind shear affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Mountain-wave and wake turbulence
lessons.concept: Therefore, when the flightpath traverses a mountain-wave-type of flow, it is desirable to fly at turbulence penetration speed and avoid flight over areas where the terrain drops abruptly, even though there may be no lenticular clouds to identify the condition.
lessons.explanation: Wind shear and its accompanying CAT in jet streams are more intense above, and to the lee of, mountain wave ranges. CAT should be anticipated whenever the flightpath traverses a strong jet stream in the vicinity of mountainous terrain. Both vertical and horizontal wind shear are, of course, greatly intensified in mountain wave conditions.

sections.title: Icing
sections.summary: Apply structural icing formation and types, induction and instrument icing, icing forecasts, reports, and avoidance, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Structural icing formation and types
lessons.concept: Structural icing can be categorized into three types: rime, clear (or glaze), and mixed.
lessons.explanation: This explains why airplanes collect ice when they pass through a liquid cloud or precipitation composed of supercooled droplets. 20.3 Structural Icing Structural icing is the ice that sticks to the outside of the airplane. It occurs when supercooled water droplets strike the airframe and freeze.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how structural icing formation and types affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

occur in the vicinity of fronts
pilots need to know
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Structural icing can be categorized into three types: rime, clear (or glaze), and mixed.

lessons.title: Induction and instrument icing
lessons.concept: Icing with Fronts A favored location for severe clear icing is freezing rain and/or freezing drizzle below a front.
lessons.explanation: Rain forms above the frontal surface at temperatures warmer than freezing. Subsequently, it falls through air at temperatures below freezing and becomes supercooled. The SLDs freeze on impact with an aircraft.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how induction and instrument icing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Icing with Fronts A favored location for severe clear icing is freezing rain and/or freezing drizzle below a front.

lessons.title: Icing forecasts, reports, and avoidance
lessons.concept: 20.3.7 Icing with Fronts Most icing reports occur in the vicinity of fronts.
lessons.explanation: Although icing occurs at all levels above the freezing level in a building cumuliform cloud, it is most intense in the upper portion of the cloud where the updraft is concentrated and SLDs are plentiful. Icing can extend to great heights in towering cumulus and cumulonimbus where strong updrafts allow SLDs to exist at temperatures as cold as -40°C. Icing in a cumuliform cloud is usually clear or mixed with rime in the upper levels.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how icing forecasts, reports, and avoidance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

sections.title: Arctic Weather
sections.summary: Apply arctic climate and air masses, ice fog, blowing snow, and whiteout, cold-weather altimetry and operations, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Arctic climate and air masses
lessons.concept: Large masses of air stagnate over inland continental areas; thus, Arctic continental areas are air mass source regions.
lessons.explanation: 21.2.2 Land and Water Figure 21-1 shows the water and the land distribution in the Arctic. Arctic mountain ranges are effective barriers to air movement. A large portion of the Arctic Ocean is covered throughout the year by a deep layer of ice, known as the permanent ice pack.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how arctic climate and air masses affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: The cited page states: Large masses of air stagnate over inland continental areas; thus, Arctic continental areas are air mass source regions.

lessons.title: Ice fog, blowing snow, and whiteout
lessons.concept: 21.4.2 Blowing and Drifting Snow Over the frozen Arctic Ocean and along the coastal areas, blowing snow, drifting snow, and strong winds are common hazards during autumn and winter.
lessons.explanation: Ice fog is almost always present at air temperatures of -45°C in the vicinity of a source of water vapor. Such sources of water vapor are the open water of fast-flowing streams or of the sea, herds of animals, volcanoes, and especially products of combustion for heating, automobiles, and aircraft. At temperatures warmer than -30°C, these sources can cause steam fog of liquid water droplets, which may turn into ice fog when cooled.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how ice fog, blowing snow, and whiteout affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Cold-weather altimetry and operations
lessons.concept: Even though a large portion of the Arctic Ocean is ice-covered through much of the year, the ice and the water below contain more heat than the surrounding cold land, thus moderating the climate.
lessons.explanation: Large masses of air stagnate over inland continental areas; thus, Arctic continental areas are air mass source regions. A large portion of the Arctic Ocean is covered throughout the year by a deep layer of ice, known as the permanent ice pack. This ice pack goes through a seasonal cycle where ice melts in the spring and summer and increases in the autumn and winter.

sections.title: Thunderstorms
sections.summary: Apply thunderstorm ingredients and life cycle, air-mass and severe thunderstorms, lightning, hail, and tornadoes, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Thunderstorm ingredients and life cycle
lessons.concept: Necessary Ingredients for Thunderstorm Cell Formation 22.3 Thunderstorm Cell Life Cycle A thunderstorm cell is the convective cell of a cumulonimbus cloud having lightning and thunder.
lessons.explanation: Virtually all showers and thunderstorms form in an air mass that is classified as conditionally unstable. A conditionally unstable air mass needs a lifting mechanism strong enough to release the instability. Lifting mechanisms include converging winds around surface lows and troughs, fronts, upslope flow, drylines, outflow boundaries generated by prior storms, and local winds, such as sea breeze, lake breeze, land breeze, and valley breeze circulations.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how thunderstorm ingredients and life cycle affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

well in advance of the parent thunderstorm cell
engine water ingestion
Further lifting of the saturated parcel results in condensation
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Necessary Ingredients for Thunderstorm Cell Formation 22.3 Thunderstorm Cell Life Cycle A thunderstorm cell is the convective cell of a cumulonimbus cloud having lightning and thunder.

lessons.title: Air-mass and severe thunderstorms
lessons.concept: The downdraft spreads out along the surface, well in advance of the parent thunderstorm cell, as a mass of cool, gusty air.
lessons.explanation: Thunderstorm Cell Life Cycle The cell transitions to the mature stage when precipitation reaches the surface. Precipitation descends through the cloud and drags the adjacent air downward, creating a strong downdraft alongside the updraft. The arc-shaped leading edge of downdraft air resembles a miniature cold front and is called a gust front.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how air-mass and severe thunderstorms affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Lightning, hail, and tornadoes
lessons.concept: Hazards include low ceiling and visibility, lightning, adverse winds, downbursts, turbulence, icing, hail, rapid altimeter changes, static electricity, tornadoes, and engine water ingestion.
lessons.explanation: 22.7 Hazards All thunderstorms have conditions that are hazards to aviation. These hazards occur in numerous combinations. While not every thunderstorm contains all hazards, it is not possible to visually determine which hazards a thunderstorm contains.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how lightning, hail, and tornadoes affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Downbursts and microbursts
lessons.concept: Smaller, shorter-lived downbursts are called microbursts.
lessons.explanation: The downward moving column of air in a typical thunderstorm is large. Convective clouds, shower cells, and thunderstorm cells sometimes produce intense downdrafts called downbursts that create strong, often damaging winds and wind shear. Downbursts (see Figure 22-7) can create hazardous conditions for pilots and have been responsible for many LLWS accidents.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how downbursts and microbursts affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Convective turbulence and icing
lessons.concept: While not every thunderstorm contains all hazards, it is not possible to visually determine which hazards a thunderstorm contains.
lessons.explanation: 22.7 Hazards All thunderstorms have conditions that are hazards to aviation. These hazards occur in numerous combinations. Hazards include low ceiling and visibility, lightning, adverse winds, downbursts, turbulence, icing, hail, rapid altimeter changes, static electricity, tornadoes, and engine water ingestion.

lessons.title: Detection, avoidance, and escape margins
lessons.concept: Weather Avoidance Radar (Aircraft Radar) Airborne weather avoidance radar is, as the name implies, for avoiding severe weather—not for penetrating it.
lessons.explanation: If the updraft velocity in the thunderstorm approaches or exceeds the velocity of the falling raindrops, very high concentrations of water may occur. It is possible that these concentrations can be in excess of the quantity of water that turbine engines are designed to ingest. Therefore, severe thunderstorms may contain areas of high water concentration, which could result in flameout and/or structural failure of one or more engines.

sections.title: Space Weather
sections.summary: Apply solar activity and geomagnetic storms, radiation and ionospheric effects, communication, navigation, and polar-route impacts, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Solar activity and geomagnetic storms
lessons.concept: These solar eruptions may cause radio blackouts, magnetic storms, ionospheric storms, and radiation storms at Earth.
lessons.explanation: Weather The Sun is the dominant source of the conditions commonly described as space weather. Emissions from the Sun are both continuous (e.g., solar luminescence and solar wind) and eruptive (e.g., coronal mass ejections (CME) and flares). Similar to the charged particles that come from the Sun, Galactic Cosmic Rays (GCR) are charged particles that originate in more distant supernovae and contribute to the space weather conditions near Earth.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how solar activity and geomagnetic storms affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Complete the cited handbook statement about solar activity and geomagnetic storms: "___, magnetic storms, ionospheric storms, and radiation storms at Earth."
options: These solar eruptions may cause radio blackouts
influxes of solar particle and electromagnetic radiation

while the emission of outgoing terrestrial radiation causes cooling
practice.explanation: The cited page states: These solar eruptions may cause radio blackouts, magnetic storms, ionospheric storms, and radiation storms at Earth.

lessons.title: Radiation and ionospheric effects
lessons.concept: 23.11 Ionospheric Storms Ionospheric storms arise from large influxes of solar particle and electromagnetic radiation.
lessons.explanation: Events that are of high energy may last for only a few hours. Diversity in the duration of solar radiation storms exists, just as there are many factors that contribute to the acceleration and propagation of the charged particles near Earth. Solar radiation storms can occur at any point in the solar cycle but tend to be most common during the years around solar maximum.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how radiation and ionospheric effects affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: Recall the FAA wording for radiation and ionospheric effects. Which phrase completes "23.11 Ionospheric Storms Ionospheric storms arise from large ___."?

while the emission of outgoing terrestrial radiation causes cooling
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 23.11 Ionospheric Storms Ionospheric storms arise from large influxes of solar particle and electromagnetic radiation.

lessons.title: Communication, navigation, and polar-route impacts
lessons.concept: The high latitude and polar ionosphere are a sink for charged particles that alter the local ionization and provide steep local ionization gradients to deflect HF radio waves, as well as increase local absorption.
lessons.explanation: HF COM on the Earth’s day side can be adversely affected when a solar flare occurs and its photons rapidly alter the electron density of the lower altitudes of the ionosphere, causing fading, noise, or a total blackout. Usually these disruptions are short-lived (tens of minutes to a few hours), so the outage ends fairly quickly. HF COM at high latitudes and polar regions are adversely affected for longer periods, sometimes days, due to some space weather events.

sections.title: Surface Observations and METAR/SPECI
sections.summary: Apply observation systems and quality control, metar/speci format, wind, visibility, and runway visual range, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Observation systems and quality control
lessons.concept: 24.2.2 Automated Observation Automated observations are derived from instruments and algorithms without human input or oversight.
lessons.explanation: There are three general types of surface weather observations: • Manual. 24.2.1 Manual Observation Manual surface weather observations are made by a human weather observer who is certified by the FAA. While manual observations were the primary type prior to the mid-1990s, automated and augmented observations make up the vast majority of today’s surface observations in the United States.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how observation systems and quality control affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.prompt: While reviewing observation systems and quality control, which source detail correctly fills the blank? "24.2.2 Automated Observation Automated observations are derived from ___."

FEW012 SCT030 BKN050
observations is the skew-T diagram (see Figure 25-22)
instruments and algorithms without human input or oversight
practice.explanation: The cited page states: 24.2.2 Automated Observation Automated observations are derived from instruments and algorithms without human input or oversight.

lessons.title: METAR/SPECI format
lessons.concept: METAR/SPECI Coding Format Chapter 24, Observations 24-7
lessons.explanation: 1 Any other meteorological situation designated by the responsible agency of which, in the opinion of the observer, is critical. METAR/SPECI has two major sections: the body (consisting of a maximum of 11 groups) and the remarks (consisting of two categories). When an element does not occur, or cannot be observed, the corresponding group is omitted from that particular report.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how metar/speci format affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Wind, visibility, and runway visual range
lessons.concept: An Example of a Decoded METAR for an Aviation Weather Website + KPNS (Pensacola Intl, FL, US) Text: KPNS 030053Z 36005KT 10SM CLR 30/23 A2992 RMK AO2 SLP132 T03000233 Temperature: 30.0 °C (86 °F) Dewpoint: 23.3 °C (74 °F) [RH = 67%] Pressure 29.92 inHg (1013.3 mb) [Sea level pressure: 1013.2 mb] (altimeter): Winds:...
lessons.explanation: The majority of current preflight briefing services, including many weather applications, provide the decoded report in a plain language format in addition to the coded report. Table 24-1 provides an example of a decoded METAR. While the decoded version has been welcomed by many pilots, it is still important for all pilots to know how to decode the METAR/SPECI in case the source does not provide a decoded version.

lessons.title: Weather, sky condition, and ceiling
lessons.concept: The sky condition group would be coded as: FEW012 SCT030 BKN050.
lessons.explanation: Clouds at 1,200 ft obscure 2/8 of the sky (FEW). Higher clouds at 3,000 ft obscure an additional 1/8 of the sky, and because the observer cannot see above the 1,200-ft layer, they are to assume that the higher 3,000-ft layer also exists above the lower layer (SCT). The highest clouds at 5,000 ft obscure 2/8 of the sky, and, again, since the observer cannot see past the 1,200 and 3,000-ft layers, they are to assume the higher 5,000-ft layer also exists above the lower layers (BKN).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how weather, sky condition, and ceiling affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.concept: For example, a temperature of 1.5°C and a missing dewpoint would be coded as 02/.
lessons.explanation: For example, a temperature of 4°C with a dewpoint of -2°C would be coded as 04/M02; a temperature of -2°C would be coded as M02. If temperature is not available, the entire temperature/dewpoint group is not coded. If dewpoint is not available, temperature is coded followed by a solidus (/) and no entry is made for dewpoint.

sections.title: Aircraft and Radar Observations
sections.summary: Apply pilot weather reports, automated aircraft reports, weather radar observations, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Pilot weather reports
lessons.concept: There are three kinds of aircraft observations: Pilot Weather Reports (PIREP), Aircraft Reports (AIREP), and Volcanic Activity Reports (VAR).
lessons.explanation: A maintenance indicator ($) is coded when an automated system detects that maintenance is needed on the system. Both PIREPs and AIREPS have two types: • Routine PIREPs and Urgent PIREPs. PIREPs are reported by the pilot (or aircrew), while AIREPs can either be reported by the pilot or generated from sensors onboard the aircraft (automated AIREPs).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how pilot weather reports affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.concept: A maintenance indicator ($) is coded when an automated system detects that maintenance is needed on the system.
lessons.explanation: There are three kinds of aircraft observations: Pilot Weather Reports (PIREP), Aircraft Reports (AIREP), and Volcanic Activity Reports (VAR). Both PIREPs and AIREPS have two types: • Routine PIREPs and Urgent PIREPs. PIREPs are reported by the pilot (or aircrew), while AIREPs can either be reported by the pilot or generated from sensors onboard the aircraft (automated AIREPs).

lessons.explanation: EDR is the ICAO standard dimension for automated turbulence reporting. EDR is a state of the atmosphere measure rather than a state of the aircraft measure, and is, therefore, independent of aircraft type. Note: This information is restricted.

lessons.concept: This includes, but is not limited to, LLWS reports, thunderstorm lines, coverage and movement, lightning, SO2 gas smell, clouds observed but not encountered, and geographical or local descriptions of where the phenomenon occurred.
lessons.explanation: The remarks (/RM) group is used to report a phenomenon that is considered important but does not fit in any of the other groups. Hazardous weather is reported first. LLWS is described to the extent possible.

sections.title: Satellite and Upper-Air Observations
sections.summary: Apply visible, infrared, and water-vapor imagery, soundings and upper-air observations, weather cameras, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Visible, infrared, and water-vapor imagery
lessons.concept: 24.7.2 Imagery Types Four types of satellite imagery are commonly used: GeoColor, visible, infrared (IR), and water vapor.
lessons.explanation: Polar-orbiting satellites orbit the Earth at a much lower altitude. Their track is such that the Earth rotates underneath the satellite as it orbits from the North Pole to the South Pole, then back to the North Pole. The vast majority of weather satellite imagery for aviation comes from NOAA’s Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellites (GOES).
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how visible, infrared, and water-vapor imagery affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

the actual water vapor content of the air

lessons.title: Soundings and upper-air observations
lessons.concept: Weather Balloon) Since the late 1930s, the NWS has taken routine scheduled upper air observations with radiosondes attached to weather balloons, usually referred to as soundings.
lessons.explanation: Data is then transmitted from those stations to other locations. At times, it can be several hours old (or more) by the time it reaches operational users in the United States (in polar areas it is much quicker). There are some direct ground stations closer to the United States that can cut the latency to about 45 minutes when utilizing the newer polar satellites.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how soundings and upper-air observations affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Weather cameras
lessons.concept: Radiosonde Network 24.9 Aviation Weather Cameras 24.9.1 FAA Aviation Weather Camera Network In July 2007 the FAA, under the leadership of Walter Combs, began a program to assist Alaska pilots with their significant needs for weather information.
lessons.explanation: Combs’ passion for safe flight in Alaska provided the fuel for much improved situational awareness, seeing the weather picture in mountain passes. His work, along with management support, created the Aviation Weather Camera Network. The FAA’s Aviation Weather Camera Network (see Figure 24-28 and Figure 24-29), consists of nearly 500 camera facilities in remote or mountainous locations.

lessons.title: Weather-camera trends and observation limitations
lessons.concept: VEIA Example at Bald Mountain (K7BM) in Colorado 24.9.3 Visual Weather Observation System (VWOS) VWOS, which is available on the FAA’s Aviation Weather Camera Network, is an advanced camera system that is comprised of a suite of weather sensors and 360-degree camera images that collectively observe and report...
lessons.explanation: VWOS uses automated processes to self-check and validate its operations and data outputs. It provides operators with visual and textual weather observations as guidance, for use with other weather information, to make flight decisions into airports that do not possess an AWOS/METAR. In the future, VEIA will be a core component of VWOS.

lessons.title: Surface analysis charts
lessons.concept: Some of the WPC’s surface analysis charts are combined with radar or satellite imagery (see Figure 25-8 and Figure 25-9) as well as having different background features (e.g., terrain).
lessons.explanation: A chart in this general form is the one commonly referred to as the weather map. See Figure 25-5 for a schematic example of a surface chart. Schematic of Surface Chart Pressure Patterns Figure 25-7 is one example of several surface analysis products available on the WPC’s website.

lessons.title: Upper-air analysis
lessons.concept: In an effort to mitigate these disruptions, since 2015, Flight Standards approved the use of NWS Real-Time Mesoscale Analysis (RTMA) for operators to use temperature information at airports where an ASOS/AWOS has failed.
lessons.explanation: Results of the SRMP supports a path forward to enable the use of Analyzed Weather as an approved source of weather information when weather information is not available from an ASOS or AWOS. At terminal locations that provide METARs, occasionally the information required for flight operations is missing and this impacts the ability to sustain operations. Improved back-up information from an approved weather source is required for efficiency and to sustain safe operations.

lessons.concept: The chart contains an analysis of isobars, pressure systems, and fronts.
lessons.explanation: 05: The wave period is five seconds. In some charts by the OPC, only the wave height (in feet) is plotted. The NWS Unified Surface Analysis Chart is a surface analysis product produced collectively and collaboratively by the NWS WPC, the OPC, the NHC, and WFO Honolulu.

lessons.title: Using analysis without treating it as a forecast
lessons.concept: Weather Balloon) Analysis A common means of analyzing radiosonde observations is the skew-T diagram (see Figure 25-22).
lessons.explanation: Pressure patterns cause and characterize much of the weather. Typically, lows and troughs are associated with clouds and precipitation, while highs and ridges are associated with fair weather, except in winter when valley fog may occur. The location and strength of the jet stream can be viewed at 300 mb, 250 mb, and 200 mb levels.

sections.title: SIGMET, AIRMET, and CWA
sections.summary: Apply advisory purpose and issuance, sigmet criteria, convective sigmet, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Advisory purpose and issuance
lessons.concept: A CWA is issued for hazardous weather when there is no existing in-flight advisory from the AWC or the AAWU.
lessons.explanation: The issuance time of the AIRMET bulletin is updated to reflect the time of the amendment. The ending valid time remains unchanged. Weather Advisory (CWA) A CWA is a concise description of the occurrence or expected occurrence of specified weather phenomena that meet or approach in-flight advisory (e.g., AIRMET, SIGMET, or Convective SIGMET) criteria.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how advisory purpose and issuance affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

layer of below-freezing air based at the surface

lessons.title: SIGMET criteria
lessons.concept: When conditions do not meet or are not expected to meet Convective SIGMET criteria within a region at the scheduled time of issuance, a “CONVECTIVE SIGMET...NONE” message is transmitted.
lessons.explanation: Each bulletin contains all valid Convective SIGMETs within the region. Convective SIGMETs are valid for two hours or until superseded by the next hourly issuance. A Convective SIGMET bulletin must be transmitted each hour for each region.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how sigmet criteria affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Convective SIGMET
lessons.concept: 26.2.4.2.6 Convective SIGMET Cancellations Convective SIGMETs are not cancelled but are superseded by the next Convective SIGMET in the series.
lessons.explanation: 26.2.4.2.7 Convective SIGMET Amendments Amended Convective SIGMETs are not issued. Instead, a new Convective SIGMET is issued for that region. 26.2.4.2.8 Convective SIGMET Corrections Corrections to Convective SIGMETs are issued as necessary.

lessons.title: AIRMET and G-AIRMET
lessons.concept: 26.3.2 AIRMET Standardization All AIRMETs follow these standards: • All heights or altitudes are referenced to above MSL, unless otherwise noted, and annotated using the height in hundreds of feet, consisting of three digits (e.g., 040).
lessons.explanation: o Range of freezing levels over the area is specified. o Lowest freezing levels AGL at intervals of 4,000 ft MSL (or surface (SFC) as appropriate) are specified. Non-convective LLWS potential below 2,000 ft AGL (LLWS POTENTIAL).

lessons.title: Tropical cyclone advisories
lessons.concept: VAA in Graphical Format 26.6 Aviation Tropical Cyclone Advisory (TCA) The aviation TCA is intended to provide tropical cyclone forecast guidance, through 24 hours, for international aviation safety and routing purposes.
lessons.explanation: 26.5.1.4 VAA Graphic Example Figure 26-11. TCAs are issued by the WMO’s TCAC for all ongoing tropical cyclone activity in their respective areas of responsibility. Two of the world’s TCACs are in the United States: the NWS NHC in Miami, FL, and the NWS CPHC in Honolulu, HI.

lessons.title: Space-weather advisories
lessons.concept: Space Weather Advisory Issuance Criteria Effect Sub-Effect Parameter Used MOD SEV GNSS Amplitude Scintillation S4 (dimensionless) 0.5 0.8 GNSS Phase Scintillation Sigma-phi (radians) 0.4 0.7 GNSS Vertical Total Electron Content (TEC) TEC units 125 175 Effective dose (microSieverts/hour)* 30 80 Kp index 8 9...
lessons.explanation: SEV advisories will be issued when the SEV threshold is reached at any FL above FL250. For context, the background effective dose rate at FL370 at very high latitudes is approximately 9 micro-Sieverts/hour during solar minimum and 6 micro-Sieverts/hour during solar maximum. These rates decrease progressively toward the equatorial regions to values approximately one quarter of what is observed at very high latitudes.

sections.title: Winds, TAF, and Surface Forecasts
sections.summary: Apply forecast process and uncertainty, terminal aerodrome forecast format, winds and temperatures aloft, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Forecast process and uncertainty
lessons.concept: This chapter will discuss many forecast products produced primarily by the NWS that are either specific to aviation or are public products of interest to aviation users.
lessons.explanation: 27 Forecasts 27.1 Introduction The fourth of five types of aviation weather information discussed in this handbook is forecasts. As with other products discussed in this handbook, the visualization of these products has evolved over the past decade with the use of internet websites. The use of static black and white depictions of aviation forecasts is almost a thing of the past.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how forecast process and uncertainty affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

Gulf of America and Caribbean Area Forecast Medium-Level

lessons.title: Terminal Aerodrome Forecast format
lessons.concept: 27.4.2 Generic Format of the Forecast Text of an NWS-Prepared TAF.
lessons.explanation: Temperature is not included in the NWS TAF, operators may use temperature information from the LAMP/MOS product (see Section 27.16). 27.4.1 TAF Responsibility TAFs are issued by NWS WFOs for nearly 700 U.S. The majority of TAFs provide a 24-hour forecast for the airport, while TAFs for some major airports provide a 30-hour forecast.

lessons.title: Ceiling and visibility forecasts
lessons.concept: Example: 1SM BR VV008 Ceiling is 800 ft due to vertical visibility into fog.
lessons.explanation: The vertical obscuration group (VVhshshs) is used to forecast, in hundreds of feet AGL, the vertical visibility (VV) into a surface-based total obscuration. VVhshshs is this ceiling at the height indicated in the forecast. TAFs do not include forecasts of partial obscurations (i.e., FEW000, SCT000, or BKN000).

lessons.explanation: Two WAFCs, WAFC Washington and WAFC London, have the responsibility to issue the WAFS forecasts. lessons.explanation: 12-Hour Low-Level SIGWX Chart—Example 27.9.1.1 Issuance Low-Level SIGWX Charts are issued four times per day by the NWS AWC (see Table 27-10). Low-Level SIGWX Chart Issuance Schedule Issuance Time ~1720Z ~2310Z Chart ~0530Z ~0935Z Valid Time 12-Hour Prog 00Z 06Z 12Z 18Z 24-Hour Prog 12Z 18Z 00Z 06Z 27.9.1.2 Content Low-Level SIGWX Charts depict weather flying categories, turbulence, and freezing levels (see Figure 27-11). In-flight icing is not depicted on the Low-Level SIGWX Chart.

lessons.title: Upper-air forecasts
lessons.concept: 27.8.1.3 Humidity, Maximum Wind, and Tropopause Forecasts No specific charts are issued for global upper air humidity, maximum wind, height of tropopause, and altitude of FLs.
lessons.explanation: These products are provided in grid point format (e.g., computer format) for use in flight-planning systems. Data from these forecasts are used by the WAFC forecasters to produce the High-Level and Medium-Level SIGWX forecasts, which contain tropopause and jet stream forecasts. Humidity data is produced for FLs 50 (850 mb), 100 (700 mb), 140 (600 mb), and 180 (500 mb).

sections.title: Convective and Specialized Forecasts
sections.summary: Apply convective outlooks, thunderstorm probability and timing, winter-weather forecasts, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Convective outlooks
lessons.concept: The day one, day two, and day three Convective Outlooks also depict areas of general thunderstorms (TSTM).
lessons.explanation: Note: The National Convective Weather Forecast (NCWF) was retired in 2018. The NWS SPC issues narrative and graphical Convective Outlooks (AC) to provide the CONUS NWS WFOs, the public, the media, and emergency managers with the potential for severe (tornado, wind gusts 50 kt or greater, or hail with diameter one inch or greater) and non-severe (general) convection and specific severe weather threats during the following eight days. The Convective Outlook defines areas of marginal risk (MRGL), slight risk (SLGT), enhanced risk (ENH), moderate risk (MDT), or high risk (HIGH) of severe weather based on a percentage probability, which varies for time periods from one day to three days, and then two probabilistic thresholds for days four through eight.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how convective outlooks affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Thunderstorm probability and timing
lessons.concept: The Short-Range Surface Prog Forecast provides precipitation forecasts in the following depiction: • NDFD Rain (Chance—light green): There is a 25 to less than 55 percent probability of measurable rain (≥0.01 in) at the valid time.
lessons.explanation: NDFD Rain (Likely—dark green): There is a greater than or equal to 55 percent probability for measurable rain (≥0.01 in) at the valid time. NDFD Snow (Chance—light blue): There is a 25 to less than 55 percent probability of measurable snowfall (≥0.01 in liquid equivalent) at the valid time. NDFD Snow (Likely—dark blue): There is a greater than or equal to 55 percent probability of measurable snow (≥0.01 in liquid equivalent) at the valid time.

lessons.title: Winter-weather forecasts
lessons.concept: NWS NCO Model Analyses and Guidance Website 27.11.1 Constant Pressure Level Forecasts Constant pressure level forecasts (see Figure 27-27) are just one of the many products produced by NWP models.
lessons.explanation: A User’s Guide, located below the image (lower left), provides descriptions, details, and examples of the various products. Constant pressure level forecasts are the computer model’s depiction of select weather (e.g., wind) at a specified constant pressure level (e.g., 300 mb), along with the altitudes (in meters) of the specified constant pressure level. When considered together, constant pressure level forecasts describe the 3D aspect of pressure systems.

lessons.explanation: 27.19 Aviation Forecast Discussion (AFD) AFDs describe the weather conditions within a multistate or substate-sized area. AFDs are a free-form plain language text product. Common or well-known aviation weather contractions are used as well as local or regional geographic names, such as valleys, mountain ranges, and bodies of water.

sections.title: Aviation Weather Tools
sections.summary: Apply graphical forecasts for aviation tool, layering weather tools into a complete briefing, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Graphical Forecasts for Aviation tool
lessons.concept: This chapter will describe three web-based interactive displays: the GFA Tool, the Flight Service’s Interactive Map, and the GFA-LA (Graphical Forecasts for Aviation – Low Altitude previously called Helicopter Emergency Medical Services HEMS) Tool.
lessons.explanation: 28 Aviation Weather Tools 28.1 Introduction The fifth of five types of aviation weather information discussed in this handbook is aviation weather tools. Aviation weather tools are web-based applications that incorporate multiple weather products into a web-based interactive display. This chapter describes these web-based displays as they are configured in 2022.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how graphical forecasts for aviation tool affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

an air parcel is used as a tool to describe certain atmospheric processes
previously called Helicopter Emergency Medical Services HEMS) Tool

lessons.title: Layering weather tools into a complete briefing
lessons.concept: The GFA Tool is a set of web-based displays intended to provide the necessary aviation weather information to give users a complete picture of the weather that may impact their flight operations.
lessons.explanation: It is a one-stop shop for multiple data fields. The AWC website includes observational data, forecasts, and advisories including thunderstorms, clouds, flight category, precipitation, icing, turbulence, and wind. Hourly model data and forecasts, including information on clouds, flight category, precipitation, icing, turbulence, wind, and graphical output from the NWS’ NDFD, are available.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how layering weather tools into a complete briefing affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.
