glossary.term: Purpose and organization of aviation weather information
glossary.definition: The information included is not prescriptive.

glossary.term: Pilot responsibility for weather knowledge and decisions
glossary.definition: The objective of this handbook is to help the pilot and operator understand the basics of weather, aviation weather hazards, and aviation weather products.
The remaining safety capacity a pilot protects while applying introduction guidance.

glossary.term: FAA and National Weather Service roles
glossary.definition: Among its six major divisions are the National Environmental Satellite, Data, and Information Service (NESDIS) and the NWS.

2 Aviation Weather Service Program 2.1 Introduction The aviation weather service program is a joint effort of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), the Department of Defense (DOD), NASA, and commercial aviation weather providers.

glossary.term: Weather information dissemination and pilot reports
glossary.definition: En route controllers advise pilots of hazardous weather that may impact operations within 150 nautical miles (NM) of the controller’s assigned sector or jurisdiction and may solicit Pilot Weather Reports (PIREP) from pilots.

Pilot reports may include precipitation, which in places in the “/WX” section of the PIREP.

Both the tropopause and the troposphere are known as the lower atmosphere.

glossary.term: Pressure, density, and temperature with altitude
glossary.definition: Standard atmosphere is a hypothetical vertical distribution of atmospheric temperature, pressure, and density that, by international agreement, is taken to be representative of the atmosphere for purposes of pressure altimeter calibrations, aircraft performance calculations, aircraft and missile design, ballistic...
5.5.2 Temperature Scales Many scientists use the Kelvin (K) scale, a thermodynamic (absolute) temperature scale, where absolute zero, the theoretical absence of all thermal energy, is 0 K.
5.6 Heat Transfer Heat transfer is energy transfer as a consequence of temperature difference.
Water temperature changes occur to depths of 6 meters (m) (20 ft) or more on a daily basis, and 200 m to 600 m (650 ft to 1950 ft) annually.
Evaporation of this runoff into the atmosphere begins the hydrologic cycle over again.
Relative humidity can be confusing because it does not indicate the actual water vapor content of the air, but rather how close the air is to saturation.

Latent Heat of Water at 0 °C Latent Heat Type Energy Exchange (J/g) Latent heat of sublimation 2,834 Latent heat of vaporization 2,501 Latent heat of fusion 334 The amount of energy associated with latent heat exchange should not be understated.

7 Earth-Atmosphere Heat Imbalances 7.1 Introduction Weather is not a capricious act of nature but, rather, the atmosphere’s response to unequal rates of radiational heating and cooling across the surface of the Earth and within its atmosphere.
Greenhouse warming is enhanced during nights when the sky is overcast (see Figure 7-2).
This poleward heat transport is accomplished by atmospheric circulations, weather, and ocean currents.
Engineering 8.2.3 Station Pressure The pressure measured at an airport is called station pressure, or the actual pressure at field elevation.
Temperature’s Effect on Pressure 8.2.5 Sea Level Pressure Since pressure varies greatly with altitude, people cannot readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes.
The altimeter is graduated to read increments of altitude rather than units of pressure.

glossary.term: Global pressure belts and circulation cells
glossary.definition: Earth Circulation System Between each of these circulation cells are bands of high and low pressure at the surface.

In reality, jet streams are typically much wider.

modules.title: Risk Management Handbook
modules.description: Practical hazard identification, risk assessment, mitigation, automation, and in-flight decision-making.

sections.title: Introduction to Risk Management
sections.summary: Apply hazards, risk, likelihood, and severity, risk management as a continuous safety process, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Hazards, risk, likelihood, and severity
lessons.concept: Risk Management Analysis Using the PAVE Checklist The accident described above contains risk factors included in the PAVE checklist.
lessons.explanation: The NTSB report, available here, also contains information about this pilot’s decision-making and risk management. Many incidents and accidents occur as a result of poor risk management. The following accident review illustrates the importance of risk-based decision-making, as a means to prevent a negative outcome.

lessons.title: Risk management as a continuous safety process
lessons.concept: This handbook will emphasize using risk management as a process to avoid situations and decisions that might lead to an aircraft accident.
lessons.explanation: Pilot error often results from an over-estimate of capability, a lack of preparation for flight, or a failure to prioritize safety. Varying levels of risk tolerance, or even intentional disregard of risk, may be a factor. Risk management training includes a variety of measures that change the way pilots perceive and deal with hazards both before and during a flight.

lessons.title: PAVE and the 5P check
lessons.concept: Using the PAVE Checklist to Identify Hazards As described in Chapter 1, the PAVE checklist provides a means to identify hazards using four convenient hazard “buckets.” Using all four checklist categories before a flight captures most hazards normally encountered.
lessons.explanation: Postflight Inspections–Inspecting the aircraft following the completion of a flight may identify aircraft hazards before the next person flies the aircraft. Common items include condition of tires and brakes, security of access panels and latches, and leaking operating fluids. Properly securing the aircraft may prevent damage, which could affect the next flight.

sections.title: Assessing Risk
sections.summary: Apply likelihood and severity, risk matrices and acceptability, cumulative and residual risk, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Likelihood and severity
lessons.concept: Risk Assessment Components Risk is the composite of the likelihood (probability) and the severity (consequences) of a particular outcome.
lessons.explanation: Chapter 4: Assessing Risk Introduction Once hazards and their associated risks have been identified, they should be assessed to determine the overall risk level presented by each hazard. This process begins before the flight using the process outlined in the previous chapter. While risk assessment may seem subjective at first, pilots can learn effective risk-management through routine practice and application.

sections.title: Mitigating Risk
sections.summary: Apply avoid, reduce, transfer, and accept, controls for pilot, aircraft, and environment, validating controls and monitoring residual risk, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Avoid, reduce, transfer, and accept
lessons.concept: Additionally, addressing discrepancies and conducting proper maintenance can increase reliability and reduce risk.
lessons.explanation: Use of and careful consideration of the IMSAFE checklist (illness, medication, stress, alcohol, fatigue, emotion) reduces aeromedical risk. Mitigating Aircraft Risks Factoring performance data to create safety margins mitigates some risks. Using an aircraft with redundant systems or an aircraft with automation that reduces pilot workload also lowers accident risk.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how avoid, reduce, transfer, and accept affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

of loss of control in IMC
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Additionally, addressing discrepancies and conducting proper maintenance can increase reliability and reduce risk.

lessons.title: Controls for pilot, aircraft, and environment
lessons.concept: For example, on a day when a line of afternoon thunderstorms is forecast, a pilot could plan for an early morning departure or wait until the thunderstorms dissipate and depart late in the afternoon.
lessons.explanation: Pilots can plan for altitudes that keep the aircraft above or below icing conditions. Many instrument pilots who operate aircraft that are not approved for flight in known icing conditions apply personal minimums to avoid conditions below certain temperatures or where the freezing level is at or below the minimum en-route altitude. Change Departure Time or Date Advancing or delaying a departure may reduce risk likelihood.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how controls for pilot, aircraft, and environment affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

flying a single-engine airplane versus a multiengine airplane
practice.explanation: The cited page states: For example, on a day when a line of afternoon thunderstorms is forecast, a pilot could plan for an early morning departure or wait until the thunderstorms dissipate and depart late in the afternoon.

lessons.title: Validating controls and monitoring residual risk
lessons.concept: Use a formal risk management process to reduce risk on all complex flights.
lessons.explanation: If not using a FRAT, continue to use the PAVE checklist to identify hazards and assess and mitigate the associated risks. Chapter Summary Risk mitigation identifies hazards and reduces the likelihood or potential severity of associated risks. It may allow a pilot to undertake a flight that would otherwise generate unacceptable risk.

sections.title: Threat and Error Management
sections.summary: Apply threats, errors, and undesired aircraft states, traps and error chains, defenses, recovery, and learning, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Threats, errors, and undesired aircraft states
lessons.concept: Defenses against Threats, Errors, and Undesired Aircraft States When a pilot perceives a flight condition that requires attention, the pilot may know what to do immediately or may need time to process alternatives.
lessons.explanation: Flight crew error creates an undesired aircraft state described as: • Incorrect aircraft position, speed, attitude, or configuration. An in-flight situation that causes pilot confusion and increased workload. Reduced safety margins and increased risk from threats or errors.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how threats, errors, and undesired aircraft states affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

options: is a weakness in those defenses
Undesired Aircraft States When a pilot perceives a flight condition that requires attention
clearance may be more susceptible to this error
manage risks and make informed aeronautical decisions
practice.explanation: The cited page states: Defenses against Threats, Errors, and Undesired Aircraft States When a pilot perceives a flight condition that requires attention, the pilot may know what to do immediately or may need time to process alternatives.

lessons.title: Traps and error chains
lessons.concept: Pilots familiar with a particular airport who routinely receive the same taxi clearance may be more susceptible to this error.
lessons.explanation: The NTSB docket for this incident provides extensive information regarding expectation bias and is available here. Expectation bias often occurs on the ground while taxiing. Pilots may expect a particular taxi route and perceive the clearance as expected rather than as given by ATC.

sections.title: Automation and Flight Path Management
sections.summary: Apply automation levels and appropriate use, mode awareness and input verification, flightpath monitoring and manual-flight readiness, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Automation levels and appropriate use
lessons.concept: 12291-2 Choosing the appropriate level of automation for the task and adjusting as circumstances dictate is essential to effective use of automation.
lessons.explanation: Balancing Automated & Manual Flight Pilots choose the level of flight path automation. Risk management strategies suggest using automation as an aid to manage workload rather than to compensate for lack of proficiency. A balance of automated and manual flight that takes workload and proficiency into consideration gives the pilot greater opportunity to monitor the flight path and aircraft state.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how automation levels and appropriate use affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.concept: The white “LOC” and “GS” annunciators indicate approach mode is armed but not engaged.
lessons.explanation: If this should occur unexpectedly, it could lead to increased workload, confusion, or result in an undesired aircraft state. LOC HDG AP ALT 9000FT GS Figure 7-3. lessons.title: Flightpath monitoring and manual-flight readiness
lessons.concept: A pilot who has become unaccustomed to manual flight may not be prepared to handle the situation.
lessons.explanation: Pilots who consistently rely on an autopilot for flight path management may experience degraded ability to fly manually when required to do so. For example, when flying in icing conditions, an automated system may make control inputs to compensate for ice accumulation. However, if the system exceeds its limits, it could disconnect and leave the aircraft in an undesired state.

sections.title: Aeronautical Decision-Making in Flight
sections.summary: Apply continuous inflight risk reassessment, time-critical decisions and changing plans, continuation bias, diversion, and landing options, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Continuous inflight risk reassessment
lessons.concept: Chapter 8: Aeronautical Decision-Making in Flight Introduction This chapter focuses on the pilot aeronautical decision-making (ADM) skills used to mitigate risk factors while in flight.
lessons.explanation: Advisory Circular (AC) 60-22, Aeronautical Decision-Making [Figure 8-1], provides additional information, background references, definitions, and other pertinent information about ADM training in the general aviation environment and is available here. Advisory Circular (AC) 60-22, Aeronautical Decision-Making, includes a wealth of information for pilots. Accidents still occur despite advances in training methods, aircraft technology, and services available to pilots.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how continuous inflight risk reassessment affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

Chapter 8: Aeronautical Decision-Making in Flight Introduction This chapter focuses on the pilot aeronautical decision-making (ADM) skills used to mitigate risk factors while in flight.
In a single-pilot operation, pilots often gather, organize, and manage available resources before flight to make it easier to assess and manage risks and make informed aeronautical decisions.

glossary.term: Continuation bias, diversion, and landing options
glossary.definition: If the pilot prepares for scenarios that may occur during a flight, such as a diversion or precautionary landing, it becomes easier to consider and perform that option with the needed information at hand.
